topics.title: Voltage follower, inverting and non-inverting amplifiers using op-amps - Frequency response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage follower, inverting and non-inverting amplifiers using op-amps - Frequency response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer)

**Formal Definition:** A voltage follower is an op-amp configuration in which the output is connected directly to the inverting input terminal, and the input signal is applied to the non-inverting input terminal. The closed-loop voltage gain is exactly unity (Av = 1), and the output voltage is identical in both magnitude and phase to the input voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The voltage follower is the fundamental building block for impedance buffering. It exhibits **infinite (theoretically) input impedance** and **zero output impedance**, making it a perfect intermediary between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a voltage follower as a **"repeater person"** standing between a weak, shy speaker (high-impedance source) and a large audience (low-impedance load). The shy speaker whispers the message to the repeater, who then amplifies their *ability to deliver* the same message at the same volume to the audience. The message content (voltage) is unchanged, but the delivery *strength* (current capability) is massively increased.

### 1.2 Inverting Amplifier

**Formal Definition:** An inverting amplifier is an op-amp circuit configuration where the input signal is applied to the inverting (−) terminal through an input resistor R1, a feedback resistor Rf is connected between the output and the inverting input, and the non-inverting (+) terminal is grounded. The output is **180° out of phase** with the input, and the closed-loop gain is determined by the ratio Rf/R1.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a seesaw. When you push down on one side (apply Vin), the other side goes up — but in the *opposite direction* (inversion). The mechanical advantage (gain) depends on how far the pivot is placed from each end (resistor ratio).

### 1.3 Non-Inverting Amplifier

**Formal Definition:** A non-inverting amplifier is an op-amp configuration where the input signal is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal, and the inverting terminal is grounded through resistor R1 with feedback resistor Rf from output to the inverting terminal. The output is **in phase** with the input, and the closed-loop gain is Av = 1 + (Rf/R1).

**Conceptual Analogy:** A person using a megaphone — the speaker speaks into the front (non-inverting input) and the sound comes out the same way it went in (in phase), only louder.

### 1.4 Frequency Response

**Formal Definition:** Frequency response describes how the closed-loop gain of an op-amp varies with the frequency of the input signal. Due to internal parasitic capacitances, op-amps exhibit a single-pole low-pass response, where gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** beyond the dominant pole frequency. The product of closed-loop gain and bandwidth remains constant, known as the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The widely used μA741 op-amp has a Gain-Bandwidth Product of approximately **1 MHz** and a slew rate of **0.5 V/μs**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Analysis of the Voltage Follower

- The output Vout is fed back entirely to the inverting input (100% negative feedback).
- Due to the virtual short concept: **V+ = V−**.
- Since V+ = Vin and V− = Vout, the result is **Vout = Vin**.
- The gain formula reduces to a simple identity: **Av = 1**.
- Provides **current gain** (and hence **power gain**) without voltage amplification.

### 2.2 Operational Analysis of the Inverting Amplifier

- Input Vin is applied to the inverting node via R1.
- The non-inverting input is grounded, so V+ = 0.
- By virtual ground concept, V− ≈ 0 V.
- KCL at the inverting node:

$$i_{in} = i_f$$

- This yields the negative gain expression and explains phase inversion.

### 2.3 Operational Analysis of the Non-Inverting Amplifier

- Input Vin is applied to V+ directly.
- V− is a voltage divider output between R1 and Rf.
- Virtual short: V+ = V−.
- Solving the voltage divider gives the gain expression.

### 2.4 Operational Analysis of Frequency Response

- The open-loop gain of an op-amp is **frequency-dependent** due to internal compensation capacitor (typically 30 pF in 741).
- The dominant pole equation is:

$$A_{OL}(f) = \frac{A_{OL(DC)}}{1 + j(f/f_p)}$$

- Above the dominant pole, gain falls at −20 dB/decade.
- Closed-loop bandwidth is the frequency where the closed-loop gain curve intersects the open-loop gain curve.
- Slew Rate (SR) limitation: maximum rate of change of output voltage = **0.5 V/μs** (for 741).
- Maximum undistorted sine wave frequency for a given peak output Vp:

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Configuration | Closed-Loop Gain (Av) | Input Impedance (Zin) | Output Impedance (Zout) | Phase Shift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Follower** | 1 | Very High (≈ ∞) | Very Low (≈ 0) | 0° |
| **Inverting Amplifier** | −Rf / R1 | R1 | Very Low | 180° |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | 1 + Rf/R1 | Very High (≈ ∞) | Very Low | 0° |

| Frequency Parameter | Formula | Typical Value (μA741) |
|---|---|---|
| Open-Loop DC Gain (AOL) | — | 2 × 10^5 (≈ 106 dB) |
| Dominant Pole Frequency (fp) | — | 5 Hz |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) | Av × fH | **1 MHz** |
| Closed-loop Bandwidth (fH) | GBP / Av | Depends on Av |
| Slew Rate (SR) | dVout/dt max | **0.5 V/μs** |
| Maximum Sine Frequency | SR / (2π Vp) | Depends on Vp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The GBP is **constant** for a given op-amp. If you increase the closed-loop gain by 10×, the bandwidth reduces by 10×. This is a guaranteed board-exam question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Inverting Amplifier Gain

**Given:** Op-amp with negative feedback, input signal Vin applied through R1 to the inverting node, feedback resistor Rf between output and inverting node, non-inverting node grounded.

**Step 1:** Apply the virtual short principle of an ideal op-amp with negative feedback.

$$V_+ = V_-$$

**Step 2:** Since the non-inverting input is grounded:

$$V_+ = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_- = 0 \text{ V (Virtual Ground)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the inverting node. The inverting input draws no current (ideal op-amp), so the current through R1 equals the current through Rf.

$$i_{R_1} = i_{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Express the currents using Ohm's law. Note that current flowing from the input through R1 toward the virtual ground is (Vin − 0)/R1, and current flowing from the virtual ground through Rf toward the output is (0 − Vout)/Rf.

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiply and rearrange.

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = -V_{out} \cdot R_1$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the gain Av = Vout/Vin.

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Final Result:** The closed-loop gain of the inverting amplifier is **Av = −Rf/R1**. The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain

**Given:** Input signal Vin applied to non-inverting terminal; R1 from inverting node to ground; Rf from output to inverting node.

**Step 1:** Virtual short condition:

$$V_+ = V_- = V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** The inverting node forms a voltage divider between Vout and ground, with the node voltage V− equal to the divider output.

$$V_- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 3:** Equate both expressions for V− using the virtual short.

$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the gain Av = Vout/Vin.

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Final Result:** The closed-loop gain of the non-inverting amplifier is **Av = 1 + Rf/R1**. The gain is always positive, meaning **zero phase shift** (in-phase output).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Voltage Follower Gain

**Given:** Output directly connected to inverting input; input at non-inverting terminal.

**Step 1:** Apply virtual short:

$$V_+ = V_-$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that V+ = Vin and V− = Vout (since output is fed back directly).

$$V_{in} = V_{out}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the gain:

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1$$

**Final Result:** The voltage follower has **unity gain** with infinite input impedance and near-zero output impedance.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Closed-Loop Bandwidth (fH)

**Given:** Op-amp with Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) and closed-loop gain Av(CL).

**Step 1:** Recall the open-loop gain with a single dominant pole at fp:

$$A_{OL}(f) = \frac{A_{OL(DC)}}{1 + j(f/f_p)}$$

**Step 2:** The Gain-Bandwidth Product is defined as the product of open-loop DC gain and dominant pole frequency:

$$GBP = A_{OL(DC)} \cdot f_p$$

**Step 3:** At the closed-loop bandwidth fH, the open-loop gain equals the closed-loop gain magnitude (where the two curves intersect on a Bode plot).

$$A_{OL(DC)} \cdot f_p = A_{v(CL)} \cdot f_H$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the closed-loop bandwidth:

$$f_H = \frac{GBP}{A_{v(CL)}}$$

**Final Result:** Higher closed-loop gain reduces the usable bandwidth proportionally. For a 741 op-amp (GBP = 1 MHz) with Av = 10, the bandwidth is **fH = 100 kHz**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Maximum Undistorted Output Frequency (Slew Rate Limit)

**Given:** Op-amp with slew rate SR and required peak output voltage Vp.

**Step 1:** The output of a sine wave Vout(t) = Vp sin(2πft) has a maximum rate of change at the zero crossings.

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\bigg|_{max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

**Step 2:** To avoid slew-rate-induced distortion, the maximum rate of change must be less than the slew rate.

$$2\pi f_{max} V_p \leq SR$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the maximum undistorted frequency:

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

**Numerical Example:** For 741 op-amp with SR = 0.5 V/μs = 5 × 10^5 V/s and Vp = 5 V:

$$f_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{31.416} \approx 15.9 \text{ kHz}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Op-Amp Amplifier Family

```mermaid
graph TD
    Src[Input Signal Source] --> NodeA{Configuration Selector}
    NodeA --> VF[Voltage Follower]
    NodeA --> INV[Inverting Amplifier]
    NodeA --> NONINV[Non-Inverting Amplifier]
    VF --> BufOut[Buffered Output Av=1]
    INV --> InvOut[Inverted Output Av=-Rf/R1]
    NONINV --> NonInvOut[Non-Inverted Output Av=1+Rf/R1]
    BufOut --> Zmatch[Impedance Matching Application]
    InvOut --> PhaseInv[Phase Inversion Application]
    NonInvOut --> PreAmp[Preamplifier Application]
    Zmatch --> FreqResp[Frequency Response Analysis]
    PhaseInv --> FreqResp
    PreAmp --> FreqResp
    FreqResp --> GBPConst[Gain-Bandwidth Product Trade-off]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Signal Path of Inverting Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin[Vin] --> R1[Input Resistor R1]
    R1 --> InvNode[Inverting Node V-=0]
    Ground[Ground 0V] --> NonInvNode[Non-Inverting Node V+=0]
    InvNode --> OpAmp[Op-Amp]
    OpAmp --> Vout[Vout]
    Vout --> Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    Rf --> InvNode
    Rf -.Negative Feedback.-> OpAmp
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Signal Path of Non-Inverting Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin[Vin] --> NonInvNode[Non-Inverting Node V+=Vin]
    NonInvNode --> OpAmp[Op-Amp]
    OpAmp --> Vout[Vout]
    Vout --> Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    Rf --> InvNode[Inverting Node V-]
    InvNode --> R1[Resistor R1]
    R1 --> Ground[Ground]
    Rf -.Negative Feedback.-> OpAmp
```

### 4.4 Frequency Response Characteristics Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    FreqInput[Input Frequency f Hz] --> LowBand{Low Frequency f less than fH}
    FreqInput --> HighBand{High Frequency f greater than fH}
    LowBand --> FlatGain[Flat Response Av Constant Maximum]
    HighBand --> RollOff[Gain Rolls Off at -20dB/decade]
    FlatGain --> DCGain[DC Gain Av]
    RollOff --> Bandwidth[Closed-Loop Bandwidth fH]
    DCGain --> GBPProd[GBP equals Av times fH]
    Bandwidth --> GBPProd
    GBPProd --> Tradeoff[Gain Bandwidth Tradeoff Constant GBP]
    Tradeoff --> SlewCheck{Slew Rate Check}
    SlewCheck -->|Within Limit| SineOK[Undistorted Sine Output]
    SlewCheck -->|Exceeds Limit| TriDist[Triangular Distortion Output]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Decision Matrix for Op-Amp Configurations

```mermaid
graph TD
    NeedAmp[Need Amplification] --> PhaseReq{Phase Requirement}
    PhaseReq -->|Inversion Needed| ChooseInv[Select Inverting Amplifier]
    PhaseReq -->|In-Phase Needed| ChooseNonInv[Select Non-Inverting Amplifier]
    PhaseReq -->|Unity Gain Buffering| ChooseVF[Select Voltage Follower]
    ChooseInv --> R1Inv[Pick R1 from Zin]
    R1Inv --> RfInv[Compute Rf equals Av times R1]
    ChooseNonInv --> R1NonInv[Pick R1]
    R1NonInv --> RfNonInv[Compute Rf equals R1 times Av minus 1]
    ChooseVF --> DirectConn[Direct Output to Inverting Input]
    RfInv --> BWCheck[Check fH equals GBP by Av]
    RfNonInv --> BWCheck
    DirectConn --> BWCheck
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define voltage follower. Mention its ideal input and output impedance.**

**Model Answer:**
A voltage follower is an op-amp circuit in which the output is directly connected to the inverting input terminal, providing a closed-loop voltage gain of unity (Av = 1). The input signal is applied to the non-inverting terminal. Its ideal input impedance is **infinite (∞)** and ideal output impedance is **zero (0)**. It is primarily used as a buffer for impedance matching between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State the significance of Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) of an op-amp.**

**Model Answer:**
The Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) of an op-amp is the product of its closed-loop gain (Av) and the corresponding closed-loop bandwidth (fH). It is a constant for a given op-amp and is equal to the frequency at which the open-loop gain becomes unity. For a 741 op-amp, GBP ≈ **1 MHz**. It indicates the trade-off between gain and bandwidth: increasing one decreases the other proportionally.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Design an inverting amplifier using a 741 op-amp to realize a gain of −50. Choose appropriate resistor values and calculate the closed-loop bandwidth. Assume GBP of 741 is 1 MHz. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for closed-loop gain of a non-inverting amplifier. Compare its input impedance with that of an inverting amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** For an inverting amplifier: Av = −Rf/R1 = −50. Choose R1 = **1 kΩ**. Then Rf = 50 × 1 kΩ = **50 kΩ**.

Closed-loop bandwidth:

$$f_H = \frac{GBP}{A_v} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{50} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

**(b) Derivation:** As derived in Section 3.2, applying the virtual short principle and voltage divider rule at the inverting node:

**Step 1:** V+ = Vin, so V− = Vin (virtual short).
**Step 2:** V− is a divider between Vout and ground: V− = Vout × R1/(R1 + Rf).
**Step 3:** Equating both and solving:

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Comparison of Input Impedance:**

| Parameter | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier |
|---|---|---|
| Input Impedance (Zin) | R1 (limited) | Very high (≈ ∞ for ideal op-amp) |

The inverting amplifier's Zin is restricted by the chosen R1 value (e.g., 1 kΩ), while the non-inverting amplifier's Zin approaches the op-amp's intrinsic input impedance, which is very high.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a voltage follower. Why is it called a unity gain buffer? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 741 op-amp (GBP = 1 MHz, SR = 0.5 V/μs) connected as a non-inverting amplifier with Av = 20, determine: (i) closed-loop bandwidth, (ii) maximum peak output voltage for an undistorted sine wave at 50 kHz. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The voltage follower consists of an op-amp with its output terminal directly connected to the inverting input. The input signal Vin is applied to the non-inverting input, and no external resistors are required. Since V+ = V− (virtual short) and V− = Vout:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_v = 1$$

It is called a **unity gain buffer** because the gain is exactly one (unity), yet it provides significant current and power gain by isolating the high-impedance source from the low-impedance load.

**(b) (i) Closed-loop bandwidth:**

$$f_H = \frac{GBP}{A_v} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{20} = 50{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Maximum peak output voltage at 50 kHz:**

Using the slew rate formula:

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p} \Rightarrow V_p = \frac{SR}{2\pi f_{max}}$$

$$V_p = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ V/s}}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{3.1416 \times 10^5} = 1.59 \text{ V}$$

Maximum peak output voltage ≈ **1.59 V**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in inverting amplifier:** Forgetting the negative sign in Av = −Rf/R1. Always remember — the output is *inverted* (180° phase shift).
> 2. **GBP unit confusion:** Students often write GBP = Av × fH without unit consistency. Remember GBP is in Hz, and Av is dimensionless.
> 3. **Slew rate vs. bandwidth confusion:** Bandwidth limitation (fH) is due to gain roll-off (linear), while slew rate limitation causes *non-linear distortion* (triangular output). Don't interchange the two.
> 4. **Virtual ground misuse:** The virtual ground concept applies *only* to the inverting amplifier (where V+ = 0). It does **not** apply to the non-inverting amplifier.
> 5. **Voltage follower gain error:** Writing Av = 1 + Rf/R1 for a voltage follower is wrong. The voltage follower has *no external resistors*; the gain is **always 1**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Voltage Follower:** Av = 1, infinite Zin, zero Zout; used for impedance buffering.
- **Inverting Amplifier:** Av = −Rf/R1, 180° phase shift, Zin = R1.
- **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** Av = 1 + Rf/R1, 0° phase shift, very high Zin.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP):** Constant = Av × fH; for 741, GBP = **1 MHz**.
- **Closed-loop bandwidth:** fH = GBP / Av; increases when gain is reduced.
- **Slew Rate (SR):** Maximum dVout/dt; for 741, SR = **0.5 V/μs**.
- **Maximum undistorted sine frequency:** fmax = SR / (2π Vp).
- **Gain roll-off:** Above dominant pole, gain decreases at **−20 dB/decade**.
- **Virtual ground:** V− ≈ 0 V **only** in the inverting amplifier.
- **Virtual short:** V+ = V−, always valid in negative feedback configurations.
- **741 op-amp key specs:** AOL = 2 × 10^5, fp = 5 Hz, GBP = 1 MHz, SR = 0.5 V/μs.
- **Phase relationships:** Inverting amplifier = 180°, non-inverting and voltage follower = 0°.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Summing and difference amplifiers using op-amps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Summing and difference amplifiers using op-amps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Summing Amplifier:** A linear application of an operational amplifier in which the output voltage is a weighted algebraic sum of two or more input voltages, achieved by connecting multiple input resistors to the inverting (virtual ground) node of the op-amp. The circuit exploits the principle of **virtual short** and **virtual ground** to ensure that inputs do not interact with one another (no loading effect).

**Difference Amplifier (Subtractor):** A single-op-amp configuration that produces an output voltage proportional to the **algebraic difference** between two input signals applied simultaneously to the inverting and non-inverting terminals, while providing a fixed, well-defined gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Focus (PCEBL407):** In this experiment, students must:
> (i) Design the circuit analytically using design equations,
> (ii) Simulate it in software (e.g., LTSpice / Multisim / Proteus), and
> (iii) Hardware-realize it on a breadboard / trainer kit using **μA741** or **LM358** IC and verify the output using a CRO / DSO and multimeter.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine a kitchen scale (the op-amp) where you place weighted plates on the left pan (inverting inputs) and the right pan (non-inverting input).**

- **Summing amplifier** → You load the *left* pan with several different packets (V₁, V₂, V₃), each through its own string (input resistor). Because the strings are independent, you can add as many packets as you want. The right pan is held fixed to ground. The needle (output) deflects **downward** (inverted) by an amount proportional to the total weight — i.e., the **negative sum**.

- **Difference amplifier** → Now you put one packet on the *left* pan (V₁) and another on the *right* pan (V₂). The needle's deflection is the **difference** between the two weights, scaled by the chosen gear ratio (gain).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for viva:** "Summing" puts all signals on the *same* input node (hence the inverting topology is most natural), while "difference" splits signals on *both* input terminals and uses the resistor-ratio matching to obtain Vout = V₂ − V₁.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Standard supply:** ±V_CC = **±15 V** (dual supply, dual polarity) for μA741
- **Maximum output swing:** ≈ ±13 V (≈ 2 V below rails due to saturation)
- **Standard resistor values used in lab:** **1 kΩ, 10 kΩ, 100 kΩ** (E12 series, 5 % tolerance)
- **Op-amp used:** **μA741** (single, 8-pin DIP) or **LM358** (single supply capable)
- **Ideal op-amp assumptions:** $A_{OL} \to \infty$, $R_{in} \to \infty$, $R_{out} \to 0$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis

These are the *foundation* on which **every** summing/difference amplifier proof rests. They must be memorized verbatim for the viva.

1. **Virtual Short:** $V^{-} = V^{+}$ (the differential input voltage is zero)
2. **No current into terminals:** $I^{-} = I^{+} = 0$ (infinite input impedance)

> [!NOTE]
> The two rules are consequences of $A_{OL} \to \infty$ and $R_{in} \to \infty$ respectively.

---

## 2.2 Inverting Summing Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logic

**Circuit topology:** N input voltages V₁, V₂, …, V_N each connect through resistors R₁, R₂, …, R_N to the inverting node. A feedback resistor R_f connects the output back to the inverting node. The non-inverting terminal is grounded.

| Step | Logic / What happens |
|------|----------------------|
| 1 | Non-inverting terminal is grounded → $V^{+} = 0$ |
| 2 | By virtual short, inverting node is also at 0 V → **virtual ground** |
| 3 | All input currents are independent because the virtual ground holds the node fixed (no interaction between sources) |
| 4 | Each input draws current $I_i = V_i / R_i$ |
| 5 | Total current entering the node = $I_1 + I_2 + \cdots + I_N$ |
| 6 | By KCL, this total current must flow through R_f (since none enters the op-amp) |
| 7 | Voltage across R_f = $-(I_1 + I_2 + \cdots + I_N) \cdot R_f$ → this is Vout |

---

## 2.3 Non-Inverting Summing Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logic

**Circuit topology:** All inputs V₁, V₂, …, V_N connect through resistors to the **non-inverting** terminal. A resistor R_g connects the inverting terminal to ground, and R_f provides feedback from output to inverting terminal.

| Step | Logic / What happens |
|------|----------------------|
| 1 | Use superposition: first find $V^{+}$ due to all inputs with output grounded (it acts as a passive summing node at $V^{+}$) |
| 2 | $V^{+} = \dfrac{\sum V_i / R_i}{1/R_1 + 1/R_2 + \cdots + 1/R_N}$ |
| 3 | Then treat as a non-inverting amplifier: $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}\right) \cdot V^{+}$ |
| 4 | Combine to get the (non-inverted) weighted sum |

---

## 2.4 Difference Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logic

**Circuit topology:** V₁ drives the inverting terminal through R₁, with R_f as feedback. V₂ drives the non-inverting terminal through R₂, with R₃ from the non-inverting terminal to ground.

| Step | Logic / What happens |
|------|----------------------|
| 1 | First find $V^{+}$ (voltage divider from V₂): $V^{+} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$ |
| 2 | By virtual short, $V^{-} = V^{+}$ |
| 3 | Apply KCL at inverting node: $\dfrac{V_1 - V^{-}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f}$ |
| 4 | Solve algebraically for $V_{out}$ |
| 5 | Apply the **resistor matching condition** to obtain clean subtraction |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these equations. They are the **only** formulas the examiner will expect for this experiment.

| # | Circuit | Output Equation | Matching Condition | Unity-Gain Special Case |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Inverting Summing (N inputs)** | $V_{out} = -R_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{V_N}{R_N}\right)$ | None required | $R_f = R_1 = R_2 = R_N \Rightarrow V_{out} = -(V_1+V_2+\cdots+V_N)$ |
| 2 | **Inverting Averaging (N inputs)** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{N}(V_1+V_2+\cdots+V_N)$ | All $R_i = (N \cdot R_f)$ | N = 2: $R_1 = R_2 = 2R_f$ |
| 3 | **Non-Inverting Summing (2 inputs)** | $V_{out} = \left(1+\dfrac{R_f}{R_g}\right)\cdot\dfrac{(V_1 R_2 + V_2 R_1)}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $R_1 = R_2 = R_3$ | $R_f = R_g \Rightarrow V_{out} = V_1 + V_2$ |
| 4 | **Difference (Subtractor)** | $V_{out} = \left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)V_2 - \left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)V_1$ | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f \Rightarrow V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$ |
| 5 | **Scaling (single inverting)** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\,V_{in}$ | N/A | $R_f = R_1 \Rightarrow V_{out} = -V_{in}$ (inverter) |

**Note on units:** All resistor values in **ohms (Ω)**; voltages in **volts (V)**; currents in **amperes (A)**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio mixers** — Summing amplifiers mix multiple microphone/instrument signals into one stereo bus.
- **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs)** — The classic R-2R ladder and weighted-resistor DAC are direct descendants of the summing amplifier.
- **Biomedical instrumentation** — ECG/EEG amplifiers use difference amplifiers to reject huge common-mode 50 Hz mains interference while amplifying tiny (mV-level) bio-signals.
- **Sensor signal conditioning** — Bridge sensors (strain gauge, RTD) produce differential outputs that need amplification.
- **Active filters** — The summing junction is the heart of state-variable and biquad filters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Inverting Summing Amplifier (2 inputs)

**Objective:** Derive the output voltage for a 2-input inverting summer with inputs $V_1$, $V_2$ through $R_1$, $R_2$ respectively, feedback $R_f$, and non-inverting input grounded.

**Step 1 — Identify node voltages using the virtual short rule.**

Because the non-inverting terminal is connected to ground:
$$V^{+} = 0 \text{ V}$$

By the virtual short principle of the ideal op-amp:
$$V^{-} = V^{+} = 0 \text{ V}$$

The inverting node is therefore held at **0 V even though it is not physically connected to ground**. This is called the **virtual ground**.

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the inverting node (call it node A).**

The sum of currents *leaving* node A must equal zero. The three branches contributing are: $R_1$ (from $V_1$), $R_2$ (from $V_2$), and $R_f$ (from $V_{out}$).

$$\frac{V^{-} - V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V^{-} - V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V^{-} = 0$.**

$$\frac{0 - V_1}{R_1} + \frac{0 - V_2}{R_2} + \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

This simplifies to:
$$-\frac{V_1}{R_1} - \frac{V_2}{R_2} - \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $V_{out}$.**

Rearranging:
$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_1}{R_1} - \frac{V_2}{R_2}$$

Multiplying both sides by $R_f$:
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to N inputs by repeating Step 2 for each additional branch.**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f \sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{V_i}{R_i}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic recap:** Virtual short → KCL → substitute → solve. This 4-step recipe works for **every** op-amp circuit in the syllabus.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Difference (Subtractor) Amplifier

**Objective:** Derive the output for the standard single-op-amp difference amplifier with V₁ at the inverting input (via R₁, with R_f feedback) and V₂ at the non-inverting input (via R₂, with R₃ to ground).

**Step 1 — Find $V^{+}$ using the voltage divider rule.**

The non-inverting terminal is fed by V₂ through R₂ and pulled down to ground through R₃. Since **no current** flows into the op-amp's non-inverting terminal ($I^{+} = 0$), R₂ and R₃ form a simple unloaded voltage divider:
$$V^{+} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the virtual short.**

$$V^{-} = V^{+} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the inverting node.**

Two branches enter this node: V₁ through R₁ and V_out through R_f. Their currents must sum to zero (no current into the op-amp):
$$\frac{V_1 - V^{-}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

Multiply through by $R_1 R_f$ to clear denominators:
$$R_f(V_1 - V^{-}) + R_1(V_{out} - V^{-}) = 0$$

Expand:
$$R_f V_1 - R_f V^{-} + R_1 V_{out} - R_1 V^{-} = 0$$

Group the $V_{out}$ term on one side:
$$R_1 V_{out} = R_f V^{-} + R_1 V^{-} - R_f V_1$$

$$R_1 V_{out} = V^{-}(R_1 + R_f) - R_f V_1$$

$$V_{out} = V^{-}\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_f}{R_1}V_1$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V^{-}$ from Step 1.**

$$V_{out} = \left[V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}\right]\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_f}{R_1}V_1$$

**Step 6 — Apply the matching condition $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$.**

Let the common ratio be $k$. Then $\frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{R_3/R_2}{1 + R_3/R_2} = \frac{k}{1+k}$, and $1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + k = \frac{1+k}{1}$. Their product:

$$\frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = \frac{k}{1+k} \cdot (1+k) = k = \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

Therefore:
$$V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}V_2 - \frac{R_f}{R_1}V_1 = \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$$

If additionally $R_f = R_1$:
$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_2 - V_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the matching condition is critical:** Without it, the circuit amplifies the *common-mode* signal $(V_1 + V_2)/2$, which is exactly what we **don't** want in a difference amp. The matching ensures the **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is theoretically infinite.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** For a 2-input inverting summing amplifier with $R_1 = R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, find the output when $V_1 = 1\text{ V}$ and $V_2 = 2\text{ V}$.

**Solution:**

Using the inverting summer equation:
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = -20\text{k} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{10\text{k}} + \frac{2}{10\text{k}}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = -20\text{k} \cdot \left(\frac{3}{10\text{k}}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = -20 \cdot 0.3 = -6 \text{ V}$$

**Verification:** Each input gain = $R_f/R_i = 20/10 = 2$. Output = $-(2 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2) = -6$ V. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Design Matrix (for quick lab calculation)

| Desired Operation | Choose R₁, R₂, R_f | Resulting Gain | Hardware Realization |
|-------------------|--------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2)$ | $R_1 = R_2 = R_f = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ | Unity summing | $R_f = R_1 = R_2$ |
| $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2)$ | $R_1 = 5\text{k}, R_2 \approx 3.3\text{k}, R_f = 10\text{k}$ | Weighted 2 & 3 | Use E12 nearest value |
| $V_{out} = -0.5(V_1 + V_2)$ | $R_1 = R_2 = 20\text{k}, R_f = 10\text{k}$ | Attenuating summer | $R_f < R_i$ |
| $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$ | $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ | Unity difference | All four equal |
| $V_{out} = 5(V_2 - V_1)$ | $R_1 = R_3 = 10\text{k}, R_2 = R_f = 50\text{k}$ | Difference gain 5 | $R_f/R_1 = 5$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inverting Summing Amplifier

The mermaid diagram below maps the **signal flow** and **node interactions** of a 2-input inverting summer. Each block represents a circuit node/branch; arrows indicate current flow.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeV1["V1 INPUT SOURCE 1"] -- "through R1" --> nodeA["NODE A INVERTING INPUT VIRTUAL GROUND"]
    nodeV2["V2 INPUT SOURCE 2"] -- "through R2" --> nodeA
    nodeA -- "no current into opamp" --> nodeOP["OPAMP 741 INVERTING TERMINAL"]
    nodeOP -- "feedback through Rf" --> nodeA
    nodeOP --> nodeVOUT["Vout NEGATIVE SUM OUTPUT"]
    nodeGND["GROUND 0V"] --> nodeOP2["NON INVERTING TERMINAL"]
    nodeVCC["VCC POSITIVE 15V"] --> nodeOP3["PIN 7 POWER"]
    nodeVEE["VEE NEGATIVE 15V"] --> nodeOP4["PIN 4 POWER"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Two independent current paths from V₁ and V₂ converge at **NODE A** (the virtual ground).
- The op-amp enforces $V^{-} = 0$ at NODE A, so each source sees only its own resistor (no loading interaction).
- All current must exit via R_f, producing the inverted sum at Vout.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Difference Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeV1["V1 INPUT AT INVERTING"] -- "R1" --> nodeA["NODE A INVERTING V EQUALS V PLUS"]
    nodeV2["V2 INPUT AT NON INVERTING"] -- "R2" --> nodeB["NODE B NON INVERTING V PLUS"]
    nodeGND["GROUND"] -- "R3" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "Rf feedback" --> nodeVOUT["Vout SCALED DIFFERENCE OUTPUT"]
    nodeB -- "virtual short" --> nodeA
    nodeOP["OPAMP 741 CORE"] --> nodeVOUT
    nodeVCC["VCC 15V"] --> nodeOP
    nodeVEE["VEE MINUS 15V"] --> nodeOP
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The non-inverting branch (R₂, R₃) forms a voltage divider that establishes $V^{+}$ proportional to V₂.
- The inverting branch (R₁, R_f) senses V₁ and provides feedback.
- Virtual short ties both nodes together; KCL yields the difference equation derived in Section 3.2.

---

## 4.3 Pinout Reference Table (μA741 — for breadboard wiring)

| DIP Pin | Label | Function in This Experiment |
|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| **1** | OFFSET NULL | Connect 10 kΩ pot between pins 1 & 5, wiper to VEE for offset nulling |
| **2** | INVERTING IN (−) | Virtual ground node for summer; V₁ node for subtractor |
| **3** | NON-INVERTING IN (+) | Ground for summer; V₂ node for subtractor |
| **4** | V− (V_EE) | Connect to **−15 V** |
| **5** | OFFSET NULL | (other end of 10 kΩ pot) |
| **6** | OUTPUT | Vout (measure with CRO / DMM) |
| **7** | V+ (V_CC) | Connect to **+15 V** |
| **8** | NC | Leave unconnected |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab wiring tip:** Always switch OFF the power supply before inserting/removing the IC. Verify pin-1 orientation (notch or dot facing *left* when the breadboard is read top-to-bottom).

---

## 4.4 Expected Output Waveform (CRO Sketch Description)

When you feed two **sine waves of different frequencies** (say 1 kHz and 2 kHz, each 1 V peak) into the 2-input inverting summer with $R_f = R_1 = R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, the CRO will display:

- A complex periodic waveform that is the **negative instantaneous sum** of the two inputs.
- Peak amplitude = -(1+1) = −2 V at the points where both sines peak positively; +2 V when both peak negatively.
- If a **DC offset** is added to one input, the waveform shifts vertically.

> [!NOTE]
> **CRO measurement checklist:** Set CH1, CH2 in **DC coupling**, set timebase to view at least 5 cycles, set vertical scale so that peaks fill 60–80 % of the screen.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a summing amplifier. State its output equation for a 3-input inverting summer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **summing amplifier** is an op-amp circuit that produces an output voltage equal to the **weighted algebraic sum** of multiple input voltages. For a 3-input inverting summing amplifier with input resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$, inputs $V_1, V_2, V_3$ and feedback resistor $R_f$:

$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$$

(1 mark: definition, 2 marks: equation with all terms)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the condition for the difference amplifier to act as an ideal subtractor with unity gain?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a single-op-amp difference amplifier, the output is:
$$V_{out} = \left(\frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}\right)\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)V_2 - \frac{R_f}{R_1}V_1$$

The condition for **ideal unity-gain subtraction** is the **resistor matching condition**:

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}$$

**and** $R_f = R_1$ (i.e., $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f$). Under these conditions, $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$ with infinite CMRR.

(1 mark: matching condition, 2 marks: explanation)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Design an op-amp circuit using μA741 to realize the equation $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + 4V_3)$. Assume $R_f = 60\text{ k}\Omega$. Draw the circuit and verify with a calculation.

**(b) [7 marks)** With the same circuit, if $V_1 = 1.5\text{ V}$, $V_2 = 2.0\text{ V}$, and $V_3 = 0.5\text{ V}$, calculate Vout. State the maximum allowed input voltage range if the supply is ±15 V and saturation is at ±13 V.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design (7 marks):**

We need a 3-input inverting summing amplifier. From:
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$$

Comparing with target $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + 4V_3)$ and $R_f = 60\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \Rightarrow R_1 = \frac{60\text{k}}{2} = 30 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\frac{R_f}{R_2} = 3 \Rightarrow R_2 = \frac{60\text{k}}{3} = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\frac{R_f}{R_3} = 4 \Rightarrow R_3 = \frac{60\text{k}}{4} = 15 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Circuit diagram (describe):** μA741 with pin-2 receiving V₁ via 30 kΩ, V₂ via 20 kΩ, V₃ via 15 kΩ; pin-3 grounded; 60 kΩ feedback between pin-6 and pin-2; ±15 V on pins 7 and 4.

| Component | Value | Mark |
|-----------|-------|------|
| $R_f$ | 60 kΩ | (½) |
| $R_1$ | 30 kΩ | (½) |
| $R_2$ | 20 kΩ | (½) |
| $R_3$ | 15 kΩ | (½) |
| Op-amp IC | μA741 | (½) |
| Power supply | ±15 V | (½) |
| Circuit diagram | Drawn and labeled | (1) |
| Verification equation | Stated | (1) |
| Justification | Per-resistor calculation shown | (2) |

**Total: 7 marks**

---

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

Substituting into the equation:
$$V_{out} = -60\text{k} \cdot \left(\frac{1.5}{30\text{k}} + \frac{2.0}{20\text{k}} + \frac{0.5}{15\text{k}}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = -60\text{k} \cdot (0.05 + 0.10 + 0.0333)$$

$$V_{out} = -60\text{k} \cdot (0.1833)$$

$$V_{out} = -11.0 \text{ V}$$

**Maximum input range calculation (assuming worst case where all three inputs add constructively and the gain block saturates at ±13 V):**

For a single input at maximum $V_{max,i}$ with others zero:
$$V_{out,i,max} = -\frac{R_f}{R_i} \cdot V_{max,i}$$

Setting $|V_{out}| = 13$ V:
- For $V_1$: $V_{1,max} = \dfrac{13}{2} = 6.5$ V
- For $V_2$: $V_{2,max} = \dfrac{13}{3} \approx 4.33$ V
- For $V_3$: $V_{3,max} = \dfrac{13}{4} = 3.25$ V

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget that with **multiple non-zero inputs simultaneously**, saturation can occur at a much *lower* combined value. Always check: $|(2V_1 + 3V_2 + 4V_3)| \le 13$ V to avoid distortion in the output.

| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Equation substitution | (1) |
| Arithmetic for Vout = −11 V | (2) |
| Saturation condition stated | (1) |
| Individual max V calculations | (2) |
| Pitfall awareness / comment | (1) |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the output voltage expression for a single-op-amp **difference amplifier** with V₁ at the inverting input (via R₁, feedback R_f) and V₂ at the non-inverting input (via R₂, with R₃ to ground). Clearly state the matching condition required for it to act as an ideal subtractor.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the difference amplifier with $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, calculate Vout when $V_1 = 3.0$ V and $V_2 = 5.0$ V. Comment on whether the circuit behaves as an ideal subtractor.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

**Outline expected in answer:**

1. $V^{+}$ from voltage divider: $V^{+} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$ (1 mark)
2. Virtual short: $V^{-} = V^{+}$ (1 mark)
3. KCL at inverting node: $\dfrac{V_1 - V^{-}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f}$ (1 mark)
4. Solve for $V_{out}$ (algebraic manipulation, 2 marks)
5. Matching condition $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ stated (1 mark)
6. Final boxed equation $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ (1 mark)

---

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

Check matching condition:
$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k}} = 1$$
$$\frac{R_3}{R_2} = \frac{20\text{k}}{20\text{k}} = 1$$

The two ratios are **equal**, so the matching condition is satisfied. The circuit **does** behave as an ideal subtractor with unity gain.

Compute the constant:
$$\frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{20\text{k}}{20\text{k} + 20\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

The V₂ coefficient:
$$0.5 \times 2 = 1$$

The V₁ coefficient:
$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1$$

Therefore:
$$V_{out} = (1)(5.0) - (1)(3.0) = 2.0 \text{ V}$$

**Comment:** Since both gain paths are unity and the matching condition holds, the output is exactly the difference: $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1 = 5.0 - 3.0 = 2.0$ V. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2 — Most common mistake:** Students frequently assume that *any* choice of four equal resistors (e.g., all 10 kΩ) gives unity subtraction. The correct statement is: **all four resistors must be equal** AND **$R_f/R_1$ must equal $R_3/R_2$**. If resistors are mismatched, the common-mode signal leaks to the output and CMRR drops sharply. This costs 1–2 marks.

| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Matching condition check | (2) |
| V₂ coefficient calculation | (2) |
| V₁ coefficient calculation | (1) |
| Final Vout = 2.0 V | (1) |
| Comment on ideal subtractor behavior | (1) |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — General Warning:**
> The **most common mark-losing errors** in this experiment are:
> 1. Forgetting the **negative sign** in the inverting summer (cost: full 1 mark).
> 2. Confusing the inverting and non-inverting inputs in the subtractor (cost: 2–3 marks).
> 3. Failing to apply the matching condition in the difference amplifier (cost: 2 marks).
> 4. Not mentioning the **virtual ground** concept — examiners allocate 1 mark for it almost every year.
> 5. Not specifying the **saturation limit** (~±13 V for μA741) when the question asks about input voltage range.
> 6. Drawing the circuit without labeling pin numbers of the IC (cost: 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and keep it in your lab file.**

- ✅ **Virtual ground** exists only at the *inverting* node of an op-amp when the non-inverting terminal is grounded. This is what makes summing work.
- ✅ **No current flows into op-amp input terminals** — this is the KCL foundation for every derivation.
- ✅ Inverting summer: $V_{out} = -R_f \sum (V_i / R_i)$ — the negative sign is **mandatory**.
- ✅ If all input resistors are equal to R and $R_f = R/N$, the circuit becomes an **averaging amplifier**.
- ✅ Difference amplifier matching: $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ — without it, CMRR is poor and common-mode signals corrupt the output.
- ✅ Unity-gain subtractor: $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f$, giving $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$.
- ✅ For weighted sum of $V_{out} = -(aV_1 + bV_2 + cV_3)$, choose $R_i = R_f/a_i$ (i.e., $R_1 = R_f/a$, $R_2 = R_f/b$, $R_3 = R_f/c$).
- ✅ μA741 output saturates ≈ **2 V below the rails** → for ±15 V supply, $V_{out} \in [-13\text{ V}, +13\text{ V}]$.
- ✅ **Always mention the assumption of an ideal op-amp** (A_OL → ∞, R_in → ∞) at the start of every derivation.
- ✅ **Three op-amp instrumentation amplifier** uses one difference amp as the second stage — students should know it as an extension.
- ✅ Common lab ICs: **μA741** (classic, dual supply), **LM358** (single supply capable), **TL081** (FET input, low bias).
- ✅ Power supply decoupling: place a **0.1 μF** capacitor between each supply pin and ground, as close to the IC as possible, to prevent oscillation.
- ✅ Use a **10 kΩ potentiometer** between pins 1 and 5 (with wiper to V_EE) for **offset nulling** to remove the small output DC error.
- ✅ For viva: be ready to explain *why* the virtual ground ensures no interaction between input sources.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active integrator and differentiator circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Active integrator and differentiator circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **active integrator** is an op-amp based circuit that produces an output voltage waveform which is the **time integral** of the input voltage waveform, scaled by a constant factor. It is realized using an op-amp with an input resistor $R$ and a **feedback capacitor** $C$ that performs the mathematical integration of the input signal.

An **active differentiator** is an op-amp based circuit whose output is proportional to the **time derivative** of the input voltage waveform, scaled by a constant factor. It uses an op-amp with an **input capacitor** $C$ and a feedback resistor $R$ in the inverting configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Lab Focus):** In the lab course PCEBL407, the "active" qualifier refers to the use of an external energy source (the op-amp's $\pm V_{CC}$ supply rails) to overcome the loading, gain, and low-frequency roll-off limitations of their *passive* RC integrator/differentiator counterparts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Outcome Mapping:** This experiment directly maps to **CO2** — *Design and analyze op-amp based linear and non-linear applications* and reinforces the practical implementation of mathematical operations on analog signals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Integrator Analogy (The "Water Bucket"):**
Imagine a bucket collecting water. The **rate at which the bucket fills** depends on how fast water flows in. The **water level in the bucket** at any moment is the *integral* (cumulative sum) of the inflow over time. The active integrator behaves like a digital "water level meter": a constant input voltage (steady water flow) produces a *ramp* output (linearly rising water level), and a sine wave input (oscillating flow) produces a cosine output.

**Differentiator Analogy (The "Speedometer"):**
A car's speedometer does not display the distance traveled; it displays *how fast* the distance is changing. The differentiator is the **electronic speedometer of voltages** — it reports the instantaneous rate of change of the input signal, completely ignoring the steady (DC) level.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *Integration ACCUMULATES (ramp on square wave); Differentiation DETECTS CHANGES (spikes on square wave edges).*

## 1.3 Standard Operational Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Op-amp supply rails | $\pm V_{CC}$ | $\pm 12\ \text{V}$ or $\pm 15\ **\text{V}**$ |
| Time constant | $\tau = RC$ | $10\ \text{k}\Omega \times 0.01\ \mu\text{F} = 100\ \mu\text{s}$ |
| Useful integration frequency | $f > f_a$ | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ (Practical Integrator) |
| Input offset voltage | $V_{io}$ | $\le 1$ **mV** (op-amp dependent, e.g., $\mu$A741) |
| Ideal Closed-loop gain magnitude | $\vert A_{CL} \vert$ | $\dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ (Integrator) or $\omega RC$ (Differentiator) |

> [!NOTE]
> Both circuits operate in the **inverting configuration**; therefore the output is **180° out of phase** with the input and carries a leading minus sign in its transfer equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

### A. Active Integrator (Ideal)
1. The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied through resistor $R$ to the **inverting terminal** (Node $V^-$).
2. The **non-inverting terminal** ($V^+$) is grounded → $V^+ = 0\ \text{V}$.
3. The feedback element between output $V_o$ and node $V^-$ is a **capacitor $C$**.
4. Because of the **virtual ground** property of an ideal op-amp, $V^- \approx V^+ = 0\ \text{V}$.
5. The current through $R$ is $i_R = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$.
6. This current cannot flow into the op-amp's input; it must charge the feedback capacitor: $i_C = C\dfrac{d(V^- - V_o)}{dt} = -C\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}$.
7. Equating and integrating yields the governing equation.

### B. Active Differentiator (Ideal)
1. The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied through **capacitor $C$** to the inverting terminal.
2. The **feedback element** is a **resistor $R$**.
3. $V^+ = 0\ \text{V}$ → $V^- \approx 0\ \text{V}$ (virtual ground).
4. The current through the input capacitor: $i_C = C\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$.
5. This same current must flow through the feedback resistor: $i_R = \dfrac{0 - V_o}{R} = -\dfrac{V_o}{R}$.
6. Equating gives an output that is the *negative derivative* of the input.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Circuit Type | Output Equation (Time Domain) | Transfer Function (s-domain) | Gain Magnitude | Phase Shift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Integrator | $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_o(0)$ | $\dfrac{V_o(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$ | $\vert A \vert = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ | $-90°$ (constant) |
| Ideal Differentiator | $V_o(t) = -RC\dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$ | $\dfrac{V_o(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -sRC$ | $\vert A \vert = \omega RC$ | $+90°$ (constant) |
| Practical Integrator | $V_o(s) = -\dfrac{R_2 / R_1}{1 + sR_2 C_1}V_{in}(s)$ | Low-pass behaviour with $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\omega R_1 C_1}$ for $f \gg f_a$ | Approaches $-90°$ |
| Practical Differentiator | $V_o(s) = -\dfrac{s R_2 C_1}{(1 + sR_1C_1)(1 + sR_2C_2)}V_{in}(s)$ | High-pass with high-frequency roll-off at $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ | $\omega R_2 C_1$ for $f \ll f_b$ | Approaches $+90°$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **minus sign** in every transfer function confirms the **inverting** nature of these topologies. Examiners expect you to retain this polarity throughout the derivation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Analog Computers (Historical, 1960s–70s):** Solved differential equations governing flight dynamics, structural mechanics, and chemical process control before digital computers became inexpensive.
- **Signal Processing:** Active integrators form the **loop filter** of Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs) and **Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\Delta$) ADCs** — present inside every smartphone's audio codec and radio.
- **Waveform Generation:** The integrator stage inside a **function generator** converts a square wave into a triangular wave.
- **Differentiator Applications:** Edge detection in **biomedical instrumentation** (QRS detection in ECG), **PID controller D-term**, and **FM demodulators**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output of the Ideal Active Integrator

We begin from the basic inverting op-amp topology with $R$ as input impedance and $C$ as feedback impedance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: KCL at the inverting node } V^- \\
i_R(t) &= i_C(t) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Express the resistor current using virtual ground } (V^- = 0) \\
i_R(t) &= \frac{V_{in}(t) - V^-}{R} = \frac{V_{in}(t) - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}(t)}{R} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Express the capacitor current. } i_C = C\dfrac{dV_C}{dt}, \text{ where } V_C = V^- - V_o = -V_o \\
i_C(t) &= C \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(V^- - V_o(t)\bigr) = C \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(0 - V_o(t)\bigr) = -C\frac{dV_o(t)}{dt} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Equate the two currents} \\
\frac{V_{in}(t)}{R} &= -C \frac{dV_o(t)}{dt} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Rearrange to isolate } dV_o \\
dV_o(t) &= -\frac{1}{RC}V_{in}(t)\,dt \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: Integrate both sides from } 0 \text{ to } t \text{ (with initial condition } V_o(0)) \\
V_o(t) - V_o(0) &= -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau \\[4pt]
\text{Step 7: Final output equation} \\
\boxed{\,V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_o(0)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation of every term:**
- $\dfrac{1}{RC}$: scaling factor (reciprocal of time constant) with units of $\text{s}^{-1}$.
- The negative sign inverts the integrated waveform.
- $V_o(0)$: initial voltage on the capacitor at $t=0$ (acts as integration constant).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output of the Ideal Active Differentiator

The topology is mirrored: the **capacitor is at the input** and the **resistor is in the feedback path**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: KCL at the inverting node } V^- \\
i_C(t) &= i_R(t) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Current through the input capacitor} \\
i_C(t) &= C \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(V_{in}(t) - V^-\bigr) = C \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(V_{in}(t) - 0\bigr) = C\frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Current through the feedback resistor} \\
i_R(t) &= \frac{V^- - V_o(t)}{R} = \frac{0 - V_o(t)}{R} = -\frac{V_o(t)}{R} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Equate and solve for } V_o \\
C\frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt} &= -\frac{V_o(t)}{R} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Multiply both sides by } -R \\
\boxed{\,V_o(t) = -RC\frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $RC$: scaling factor with units of seconds.
- Output is **proportional to the slope** of the input signal.
- The minus sign indicates **phase inversion**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Response (Bode Magnitude) of the Ideal Integrator

Using the s-domain transfer function $H(s) = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$, substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(j\omega) &= -\frac{1}{j\omega RC} = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega} = -\frac{1}{RC}\cdot\left(\frac{-j}{\omega}\right) = j\cdot\frac{1}{\omega RC} \\[4pt]
\text{Magnitude: } \vert H(j\omega) \vert &= \frac{1}{\omega RC} \\[4pt]
\text{Phase: } \angle H(j\omega) &= +90^\circ \;\;\text{(the minus sign from inverting config contributes }180^\circ\text{, and } \frac{1}{j}\text{ adds } -90^\circ \Rightarrow +90^\circ \text{ net relative to input? — verify sign convention.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> With strict sign convention, the integrator output **lags** the input by 90° (plus the 180° inverting polarity = net 270° which equals $-90°$ modulo 360°). For KTU valuation, write: *Output lags input by 90° in the magnitude sense; the inverting sign simply flips polarity.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Frequency $f_a$ of the Practical Integrator

The practical integrator adds $R_2$ across $C_1$ to limit DC gain and prevent saturation due to input offset. The transfer function becomes:

$$
H(s) = -\frac{R_2/R_1}{1 + sR_2C_1}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For } \omega \gg \omega_a = \frac{1}{R_2C_1} &: \quad 1 + sR_2C_1 \approx sR_2C_1 \\[4pt]
H(s) &\approx -\frac{R_2/R_1}{sR_2C_1} = -\frac{1}{sR_1C_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The frequency at which integration begins to dominate:

$$
\boxed{\,f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_1}\,}
$$

**Design rule:** Choose $f_a \ll f_{signal}$ so the circuit behaves as a true integrator over the signal band.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define an active integrator and write its output equation for a sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = V_m\sin(\omega t)$.**
*(Model Answer – 3 marks)*

An active integrator is an op-amp circuit using an input resistor $R$ and a feedback capacitor $C$ that produces an output proportional to the integral of the input voltage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o(t) &= -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^t V_m \sin(\omega \tau)\,d\tau \\
&= -\frac{V_m}{RC}\left[-\frac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega}\right]_0^t \\
&= \frac{V_m}{\omega RC}\bigl[\cos(\omega t) - 1\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence a **sine input produces a cosine output scaled by $\dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$**, with a 90° phase lag. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. Why is the practical differentiator circuit preferred over the ideal differentiator in laboratory applications?**
*(Model Answer – 3 marks)*

The ideal differentiator amplifies high-frequency noise proportionally to $\omega$, and has an unbounded gain at high frequencies, making it unstable and susceptible to **RF interference**. The practical differentiator adds a small input resistor $R_1$ in series with $C_1$ and a feedback capacitor $C_2$ in parallel with $R_2$. This introduces a **high-frequency roll-off** above $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ and limits the closed-loop bandwidth, thereby **suppressing noise amplification** and ensuring stability. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model, PCEBL407]**

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**A (a)** Design an op-amp integrator to integrate an audio-frequency square wave of **$1\ \text{kHz}$** with peak amplitude $\pm 5\ \text{V}$. Choose $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and determine a suitable value of $C$. Sketch the expected input-output waveforms. (7 marks)

**A (b)** For the integrator designed in part (a), calculate the **triangular wave peak-to-peak output amplitude** at steady state. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — A(a)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } f &= 1\ \text{kHz}, \quad V_{in,p} = 5\ \text{V}, \quad R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega \\[4pt]
\text{For a square wave, during the positive half-cycle } (T/2 = 0.5\ \text{ms}) \\
V_{in} &= +5\ \text{V} \text{ (constant)} \\[4pt]
V_o(t) &= -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^{T/2} 5\,dt = -\frac{5}{RC}\cdot\frac{T}{2} = -\frac{5T}{2RC} \\[4pt]
\text{Choose } T/(2RC) \ll 1 \text{ to avoid saturation at } \pm 15\ \text{V supply rail} \\
\text{Let } RC &= 0.5\ \text{ms} \Rightarrow C = \frac{0.5\text{ ms}}{10\ \text{k}\Omega} = 50\ \text{nF} \\[4pt]
\text{Therefore: } C &= \mathbf{50\ \text{nF}} \\[4pt]
\text{Output during positive half-cycle: } \Delta V_o &= -\frac{5 \times 0.5\ \text{ms}}{0.5\ \text{ms}} = -5\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Waveform Sketch Description:** The input is a square wave of $\pm 5\ \text{V}$ at $1\ \text{kHz}$. The output is a triangular wave of peak-to-peak $10\ \text{V}$, with **negative slope** during positive input half-cycle and **positive slope** during negative input half-cycle. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — A(b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o,\text{peak}} &= \frac{V_{in,p} \cdot T}{2RC} = \frac{5 \times 1\times 10^{-3}}{2 \times (10\times 10^3)(50\times 10^{-9})} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5\times 10^{-3}}{1\times 10^{-3}} = 5\ \text{V (peak)} \\[4pt]
\therefore V_{o,\text{pp}} &= 2 \times 5 = \mathbf{10\ \text{V (peak-to-peak)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\pm 5\ \text{V}$ peak lies well within the $\pm 12\ \text{V}$ to $\pm 15\ \text{V}$ op-amp supply rails, the op-amp will **not saturate** and the triangular waveform will be linear and undistorted. (7 marks)

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks)

**B (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the **output voltage equation** of an op-amp differentiator. (7 marks)

**B (b)** An op-amp differentiator has $R = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$. Calculate the **output voltage** when the input is a **triangular wave** of peak amplitude $\pm 2\ \text{V}$ at $500\ \text{Hz}$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — B(a)

*(Refer to derivation in Section 3.2 for full step-by-step KCL. Key result reproduced here.)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Circuit: input capacitor } C \text{ to } V^-,\; \text{feedback resistor } R \text{ between } V_o \text{ and } V^- \\
\text{Applying KCL at the virtual-ground node:} \\
C\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} &= -\frac{V_o}{R} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,V_o(t) = -RC\frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a sinusoidal input $V_{in} = V_m\sin(\omega t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o(t) = -RC\cdot V_m\omega\cos(\omega t) = -\omega RC\, V_m\cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output leads the input by 90° (cosine)** and is scaled by the factor $\omega RC$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — B(b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } R &= 100\ \text{k}\Omega,\quad C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F},\quad f = 500\ \text{Hz},\quad V_{p} = 2\ \text{V} \\[4pt]
\text{Time period: } T &= \frac{1}{f} = 2\ \text{ms} \\[4pt]
\text{For a triangular wave, slope during rising edge: } \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = \frac{2V_p}{T/2} = \frac{4V_p}{T} \\[4pt]
\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} &= \frac{4 \times 2}{2\times 10^{-3}} = 4000\ \text{V/s} \\[4pt]
V_o &= -RC \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -(100\times 10^3)(0.1\times 10^{-6})(4000) \\[4pt]
V_o &= -10^{-2} \times 4000 = -40\ \mathbf{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reality Check:** Since the op-amp supply is typically $\pm 15\ \text{V}$, the theoretical $-40\ \text{V}$ **cannot be produced** — the output will be **clipped (saturated) at $-13\ \text{V}$ to $-14\ \text{V}$** in practice. This is a classic **KTU viva question** to test whether students understand the *practical* limits of the circuit. (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error (–1 mark):** Forgetting the negative sign from the inverting configuration is the most common deduction. Always retain $V_o = -RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ with the minus.
> 2. **Saturation Ignorance (–2 marks):** In Q-B(b), blindly writing $-40\ \text{V}$ as the answer loses marks. The correct professional answer is "theoretical $-40\ \text{V}$ **clipped to supply rail**, hence practically $\approx -13\ \text{V}$".
> 3. **Missing Initial Condition (–1 mark):** For the integrator, the constant $V_o(0)$ is the **initial capacitor voltage** at $t=0$, not an arbitrary constant.
> 4. **Wrong Phase Convention (–1 mark):** Integrator **lags** input by 90°; Differentiator **leads** input by 90° (in magnitude sense, ignoring 180° inversion).
> 5. **Units Mismatch (–1 mark):** Always express $RC$ in seconds; $1/RC$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$; the derivative $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ in $\text{V/s}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **Topology Rule:** Integrator = Resistor at input, **Capacitor in feedback**; Differentiator = **Capacitor at input**, Resistor in feedback.
- 🔹 **Governing Equation — Integrator:** $\boxed{V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_o(0)}$
- 🔹 **Governing Equation — Differentiator:** $\boxed{V_o(t) = -RC\dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}}$
- 🔹 **Transfer Functions:** Integrator $\dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}} = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$; Differentiator $\dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}} = -sRC$.
- 🔹 **Phase Relationship:** Integrator output **lags** input by 90° (magnitude); Differentiator output **leads** input by 90° (magnitude).
- 🔹 **Practical Integrator:** Adds $R_2$ across $C_1$ to define a low-frequency limit $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_1}$, preventing saturation from input offset voltage.
- 🔹 **Practical Differentiator:** Adds $R_1$ in series with $C_1$ and $C_2$ across $R_2$ to limit high-frequency gain and suppress noise — high-frequency pole at $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$.
- 🔹 **Square-wave → Triangular-wave** (integrator behavior) and **Triangular-wave → Square-wave** (differentiator behavior) are the **two most important lab observations** to record.
- 🔹 **Virtual ground** at $V^-$ is the **key assumption** enabling all derivations; if the op-amp is in saturation, virtual ground fails and the equations no longer hold.
- 🔹 **Slew Rate Limitation:** For high-frequency triangular inputs, the differentiator output may exceed the op-amp's **slew rate** (typically $0.5\ \text{V}/\mu\text{s}$ for $\mu$A741), causing **triangular-to-slew distortion** — a frequent viva topic.
- 🔹 **Default Lab Component Values to Memorise:** $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$ gives $\tau = 100\ \mu\text{s}$; suitable for audio-band ($20\ \text{Hz}$ to $20\ \text{kHz}$) experiments.
- 🔹 **Input Offset Compensation:** Use the **offset null** potentiometer pins (1 and 5 on $\mu$A741 8-pin DIP) to nullify $V_{io}$ before recording the integrator's output — critical for low-frequency DC integration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active first and second order high pass and low pass filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Active first and second order high pass and low pass filters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Active Filter** is a frequency-selective electronic network that uses an active component (typically an operational amplifier like **IC 741**) along with passive components (resistors and capacitors) to pass signals of desired frequency ranges while attenuating unwanted frequencies. Unlike passive filters, active filters provide **gain**, **impedance buffering**, and do not require inductors, making them ideal for low-frequency audio and instrumentation applications. In the KTU 2024 Scheme LIC Lab (PCEBL407), this experiment typically implements the following four filter topologies: | Filter Type | Function | Frequency Behavior | |-------------|----------|--------------------| | **1st Order Low Pass Filter (LPF)** | Passes low frequencies, blocks high | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | | **1st Order High Pass Filter (HPF)** | Passes high frequencies, blocks low | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | | **2nd Order Low Pass Filter** | Steeper roll-off (40 dB/decade) | Sallen-Key topology | | **2nd Order High Pass Filter** | Steeper roll-off (40 dB/decade) | Sallen-Key topology | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The order of a filter is determined by the number of energy-storing elements (capacitors/inductors) in the signal path. Each order adds **20 dB/decade** of roll-off rate beyond the cutoff frequency. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water filter system** connected to a tap: - A **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is like a sieve with small holes — it lets small particles (low frequency, slow signals like DC or bass notes) pass through, but blocks large debris (high frequency, fast signals like treble/hiss). - A **High Pass Filter (HPF)** is the opposite — like a coarse net that catches big stones (low frequency hum/rumble) but allows fine water spray (high frequency content) to pass. - Adding an **op-amp** to this "sieve" makes it *active* — meaning not only does it filter, but it can also **amplify** the wanted signal back to full strength, something a plain passive RC network cannot do efficiently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU Viva:** The "active" nature comes from the op-amp providing **gain** ($A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$). The "order" refers to the steepness of attenuation — **1st order = 20 dB/decade**, **2nd order = 40 dB/decade**. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard cutoff frequency** for lab design: $f_c = \textbf{1 kHz}$ or $\textbf{10 kHz}$ - **Standard capacitor** for audio filters: $C = \textbf{0.1 \mu F}$ or $\textbf{0.01 \mu F}$ - **Standard power supply for IC 741**: $\pm \textbf{15 V DC}$ - **Roll-off rate**: $\textbf{20 dB/decade}$ per filter order - **Gain-bandwidth product of IC 741**: $GBW = \textbf{1 MHz}$ --- ### 1.4 Block Diagram of a Generic Active Filter ``` Input Signal (Vin) → [RC Network] → [Op-Amp Stage] → Output Signal (Vout) ↓ ↓ Frequency Gain/Buffering Selection ``` ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Theory Breakdown #### A. First Order Active Low Pass Filter (LPF) The circuit consists of: - A **passive RC low-pass section** (R in series, C to ground) at the input - An op-amp wired as a **non-inverting amplifier** (voltage follower or with gain $A_v$) **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. The input signal $V_{in}$ passes through resistor $R$ and reaches the non-inverting terminal. 2. Capacitor $C$ connected from the input node to ground provides a **shunt path to ground** for high-frequency signals (since $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi fC}$ decreases with frequency). 3. At low frequencies, $X_C \to \infty$, so the signal passes unattenuated. 4. At high frequencies, $X_C \to 0$, so the signal is shorted to ground. 5. The op-amp buffers the output, providing **high input impedance** (no loading on RC network) and **low output impedance** (drives load easily). **Cutoff Frequency Derivation:** At cutoff, $\vert X_C \vert = R$, hence: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ --- #### B. First Order Active High Pass Filter (HPF) The circuit is the dual of LPF: - A **passive RC high-pass section** (C in series, R to ground) at the input - An op-amp in non-inverting configuration **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. Capacitor $C$ in series **blocks DC and low frequencies** ($X_C$ is high at low $f$). 2. Resistor $R$ to ground provides a return path; at high $f$, $X_C \to 0$, so signal passes. 3. Op-amp again provides gain and isolation. 4. At very low frequencies, $X_C \to \infty$, attenuating the signal. Same cutoff formula applies: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ --- #### C. Second Order Filters (Sallen-Key Topology) The **Sallen-Key** architecture uses **two RC sections** cascaded with a **non-inverting op-amp** providing positive feedback. This yields a sharper transition between passband and stopband. **For 2nd Order LPF (Sallen-Key):** - Components: $R_1, R_2, C_1, C_2$ with op-amp gain $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ - The two capacitors are at the output and feedback nodes - Transfer function has a **second-order denominator** **For 2nd Order HPF (Sallen-Key):** - Components: $C_1, C_2$ in series at input, $R_1, R_2$ to ground - Dual of LPF topology > [!NOTE] > **Roll-off Comparison:** 2nd order filters attenuate unwanted signals at **40 dB/decade**, which is **twice as steep** as 1st order. This makes them preferred in real-world applications requiring sharp selectivity. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | 1st Order LPF | 1st Order HPF | 2nd Order LPF (Sallen-Key) | 2nd Order HPF (Sallen-Key) | |-----------|---------------|---------------|----------------------------|----------------------------| | **Cutoff Frequency $f_c$** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | | **Passband Gain $A_v$** | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | | **Transfer Function $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$** | $\dfrac{A_v}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{A_v}{\sqrt{1 + (f_c/f)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{K}{\sqrt{(1 - (f/f_c)^2)^2 + (f/(Qf_c))^2}}$ | $\dfrac{K (f/f_c)^2}{\sqrt{(1 - (f/f_c)^2)^2 + (f/(Qf_c))^2}}$ | | **Roll-off Rate** | 20 dB/decade | 20 dB/decade | 40 dB/decade | 40 dB/decade | | **Quality Factor $Q$** | Not applicable | Not applicable | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3 - K}$ | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3 - K}$ | | **Phase Shift at $f_c$** | $-45°$ | $+45°$ | $-90°$ | $+90°$ | | **Damping Condition** | None | None | $K < 3$ (stable) | $K < 3$ (stable) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Design Rule of Thumb:** For equal component Sallen-Key design, set $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$, simplifying $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications - **LPF**: Anti-aliasing filter before ADC, noise removal in audio systems, smoothing PWM outputs - **HPF**: DC blocking in AC coupled amplifiers, hum removal (50/60 Hz) from audio, speech processing - **2nd Order Sallen-Key**: Crossover networks in loudspeakers, biomedical signal conditioning (ECG/EEG), instrumentation pre-filters ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of 1st Order Active LPF Transfer Function **Circuit Assumption:** Non-inverting op-amp with voltage divider RC network at input. **Step 1 — Voltage Divider at the Non-Inverting Input:** The capacitor $C$ is connected from the non-inverting terminal to ground. Resistor $R$ is in series from $V_{in}$ to the non-inverting input. Using voltage divider rule: $$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{X_C}{R + X_C}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute Capacitive Reactance:** $$X_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$ Therefore: $$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $j\omega C$:** $$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Op-Amp Non-Inverting Gain:** For a non-inverting op-amp configuration: $$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$$ Thus: $$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_+ = A_v \cdot V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 5 — Transfer Function $H(j\omega)$:** $$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A_v}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 6 — Magnitude Response:** $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{A_v}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Convert to Frequency Domain $f$:** Since $\omega = 2\pi f$: $$\boxed{\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{A_v}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}} \quad \text{where} \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ **Step 8 — Verification at $f = f_c$:** At cutoff frequency, $f = f_c$, so $f/f_c = 1$: $$\vert H(f_c) \vert = \frac{A_v}{\sqrt{1 + 1}} = \frac{A_v}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 A_v$$ This corresponds to **-3 dB point**, confirming the definition of cutoff. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of 1st Order Active HPF Transfer Function **Circuit Assumption:** Non-inverting op-amp with capacitor in series and resistor to ground. **Step 1 — Voltage Divider at the Non-Inverting Input:** The capacitor $C$ is in series from $V_{in}$, and resistor $R$ goes from the non-inverting input to ground: $$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + X_C}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $X_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$:** $$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $j\omega C$:** $$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Non-Inverting Op-Amp Gain:** $$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 5 — Transfer Function:** $$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A_v \cdot j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 6 — Magnitude Response:** $$\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{A_v}{\sqrt{1 + (f_c/f)^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Cutoff Frequency (Same as LPF):** $$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ **Step 8 — Behavior at Extremes:** - At $f \to 0$ (DC): $f_c/f \to \infty$, so $\vert H \vert \to 0$ (DC blocked ✓) - At $f \to \infty$: $f_c/f \to 0$, so $\vert H \vert \to A_v$ (high pass ✓) - At $f = f_c$: $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{A_v}{\sqrt{2}}$ (–3 dB point ✓) --- ### 3.3 Derivation of 2nd Order Sallen-Key LPF Transfer Function **Step 1 — Write KCL at Node A (between $R_1$ and $C_1$):** For the Sallen-Key LPF with equal components ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$), the standard transfer function is: $$H(s) = \frac{K}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + sRC(3 - K) + 1}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:** $$H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + j\omega RC(3 - K)}$$ **Step 3 — Compare to Standard 2nd Order Form:** Standard form: $H(s) = \dfrac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} s + \omega_0^2}$ Matching coefficients: $$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{R^2 C^2} \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$ $$\frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{3 - K}{RC} \implies Q = \frac{1}{3 - K}$$ **Step 4 — Cutoff Frequency:** $$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ **Step 5 — Special Cases for $K$:** - $K = 1$ (unity gain follower): $Q = 0.5$ (maximally flat, **Butterworth** response) - $K > 1$: $Q$ increases, response peaks near $f_c$ - $K \geq 3$: $Q \to \infty$ → **oscillation** (unstable) --- ### 3.4 Numerical Lab Design Example **Design Specification (Typical KTU Lab Question):** - Design a 1st order active LPF with $f_c = \textbf{1 kHz}$, $A_v = \textbf{2}$ - Choose $C = \textbf{0.1 \mu F}$ **Step 1 — Find R:** $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.5 \ \Omega \approx 1.59 \ k\Omega$$ **Step 2 — Find $R_f$ and $R_i$ for $A_v = 2$:** $$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 2 \implies \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 1$$ Choose $R_f = R_i = \textbf{10 k\Omega}$ (standard value) **Step 3 — Verification at $f_c$:** At $f = 1$ kHz, expected output = $0.707 \times V_{in} \times A_v = 0.707 \times V_{in} \times 2 = 1.414 V_{in}$ **Step 4 — 2nd Order Design with Same $f_c$:** For Sallen-Key LPF, equal component design: $R_1 = R_2 = 1.59 \ k\Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = 0.1 \ \mu F$, $K = 1$ (unity gain) → $Q = 0.5$ (Butterworth). --- ### 3.5 Frequency Response Bode Plot (Tabular Form) | Frequency $f$ | $\vert H \vert$ for 1st Order LPF | Gain in dB | |---------------|-----------------------------------|------------| | $0.01 f_c$ | $0.99995 A_v$ | $\approx 0$ dB | | $0.1 f_c$ | $0.995 A_v$ | $-0.04$ dB | | $f_c$ | $0.707 A_v$ | $-3$ dB | | $10 f_c$ | $0.0995 A_v$ | $-20$ dB | | $100 f_c$ | $0.01 A_v$ | $-40$ dB | > This confirms the **20 dB/decade** slope characteristic of 1st order filters. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define an active filter. List any two advantages of active filters over passive filters.* **Model Answer:** An **active filter** is a frequency-selective electronic circuit that uses an active device (such as an op-amp) in conjunction with passive components (resistors and capacitors) to achieve the desired frequency response with gain. **Two advantages over passive filters:** 1. **Gain:** Active filters can provide gain ($A_v > 1$), while passive filters always have gain $\leq 1$. 2. **No loading effect:** High input impedance of the op-amp prevents the RC network from being loaded, ensuring accurate cutoff frequency. 3. *Bonus:* No inductors required, making them compact and suitable for IC fabrication. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the roll-off rate of a 1st order and 2nd order Butterworth low pass filter.
