What is the cut-off frequency for a 1st order LPF with R = 10 kΩ and C = 0.01 μF?* **Model Answer:** - **Roll-off rate:** - 1st order LPF: **20 dB/decade** - 2nd order LPF: **40 dB/decade** - **Cutoff frequency calculation:** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.5 \ \text{Hz} \approx 1.59 \ \text{kHz}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Focus on 1st Order Filters* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Design a 1st order active low pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = 2 \text{ kHz}$ and passband gain $A_v = 5$. Assume $C = 0.01 \ \mu F$. Draw the circuit diagram. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1 — Calculate R:** $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7.96 \ \text{k}\Omega$$ Use standard value **8.2 kΩ**. **Step 2 — Calculate $R_f$ and $R_i$:** $$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 5 \implies \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 4$$ Choose $R_i = 10 \ \text{k}\Omega$ (standard), then $R_f = 40 \ \text{k}\Omega$. **Step 3 — Circuit Description:** - Resistor $R = 8.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ in series from $V_{in}$ to non-inverting input (Pin 3) - Capacitor $C = 0.01 \ \mu F$ from Pin 3 to ground - $R_f = 40 \text{ k}\Omega$ between Pin 6 (output) and Pin 2 (inverting input) - $R_i = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ between Pin 2 and ground - Power supply: $\pm 15$ V to Pins 7 and 4 --- **(b)** For the above filter, calculate the output voltage magnitude for an input of $V_{in} = 100 \text{ mV (rms)}$ at frequencies $f = 200 \text{ Hz}$, $f = 2 \text{ kHz}$, and $f = 20 \text{ kHz}$. Comment on the results. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (b):** **General formula:** $$V_{out} = \frac{A_v \cdot V_{in}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$ **At $f = 200$ Hz (below cutoff):** $$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{200}{2000} = 0.1$$ $$V_{out} = \frac{5 \times 100}{\sqrt{1 + 0.01}} = \frac{500}{\sqrt{1.01}} = \frac{500}{1.005} \approx 497.5 \ \text{mV}$$ **At $f = 2$ kHz (cutoff):** $$\frac{f}{f_c} = 1$$ $$V_{out} = \frac{5 \times 100}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{500}{1.414} \approx 353.5 \ \text{mV} \quad (-3 \ \text{dB point})$$ **At $f = 20$ kHz (above cutoff):** $$\frac{f}{f_c} = 10$$ $$V_{out} = \frac{5 \times 100}{\sqrt{1 + 100}} = \frac{500}{\sqrt{101}} = \frac{500}{10.05} \approx 49.75 \ \text{mV}$$ **Comments:** - At 200 Hz, output ≈ 500 mV (gain of 5 maintained, passband region). - At 2 kHz, output ≈ 353.5 mV (–3 dB below maximum, defining cutoff). - At 20 kHz, output ≈ 50 mV (attenuated by factor of 10, demonstrating the 20 dB/decade roll-off). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Focus on 2nd Order Filters (Sallen-Key)* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 2nd order Sallen-Key low pass filter. Derive the expression for its cutoff frequency. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Circuit Description:** The Sallen-Key 2nd order LPF uses: - Two equal resistors $R_1, R_2$ in the input path - Two equal capacitors $C_1, C_2$ with $C_1$ to ground and $C_2$ in the feedback path - An op-amp in non-inverting configuration with gain $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ **Operation:** 1. The first RC section ($R_1, C_1$) provides initial low-pass filtering. 2. The op-amp buffers and amplifies the signal with gain $K$. 3. Positive feedback through $C_2$ sharpens the transition between passband and stopband. 4. The combined effect produces **40 dB/decade** roll-off. **Derivation of Cutoff Frequency:** The general transfer function for the Sallen-Key LPF (equal components) is: $$H(s) = \frac{K}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + sRC(3 - K) + 1}$$ Comparing with standard 2nd order form $H(s) = \dfrac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2}$: $$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{R^2 C^2} \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$ $$f_c = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$$ $$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ **Quality Factor:** $$Q = \frac{1}{3 - K}$$ For **Butterworth response (maximally flat)**: $K = 1$ → $Q = 0.5$. --- **(b)** Design a 2nd order Sallen-Key HPF with $f_c = 5 \text{ kHz}$ using equal components. Choose $C = 10 \text{ nF}$. Calculate $R$, $R_f$, and $R_i$ for unity gain. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Calculate R:** $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3.18 \ \text{k}\Omega$$ Use standard value **3.3 kΩ**. **Step 2 — Components for Sallen-Key HPF (Equal Component Design):** - $C_1 = C_2 = 10 \ \text{nF}$ - $R_1 = R_2 = 3.3 \ \text{k}\Omega$ - $R_1$ to ground from node 1, $R_2$ feedback resistor **Step 3 — Unity Gain Condition ($K = 1$):** For unity gain (voltage follower configuration), the op-amp output is directly connected to the inverting input: $$R_f = 0 \ \Omega \quad \text{(short)} \quad \text{and} \quad R_i = \infty \quad \text{(open)}$$ Or simply wire Pin 6 directly to Pin 2. **Step 4 — Verification of Q:** $$Q = \frac{1}{3 - K} = \frac{1}{3 - 1} = 0.5 \quad \text{(Butterworth, maximally flat)}$$ **Step 5 — Practical Connection Summary:** - Pin 3 (non-inverting) connected to the output of the 2nd RC section - Pin 2 (inverting) connected to Pin 6 (output) for unity gain - $\pm 15$ V supply to Pins 7 and 4 --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse the placement of R and C in HPF vs LPF. Remember — for **HPF, the capacitor is in series** and the resistor is to ground; for **LPF, the resistor is in series** and the capacitor is to ground. *(–2 marks if swapped)* > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **op-amp gain** $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_i$ in passband calculations. The output at low frequency (LPF) is **not** just $V_{in}$ but $A_v \times V_{in}$. *(–1 mark)* > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **dB** with **linear scale**. At cutoff, the gain drops to **0.707** times the maximum, which is **–3 dB**, not –30 dB. *(–1 mark)* > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** For Sallen-Key, using $K \geq 3$ causes **oscillation** (unstable filter). Always ensure $K < 3$ for stable operation. *(–2 marks for stability check omission)* > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In lab record, forgetting to mention the **IC number (IC 741)**, **power supply voltages (±15V)**, and **component values used**. *(–1 mark)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 LIC Lab Exam** - [ ] **Active filter** = Op-amp + RC network → provides gain + filtering - [ ] **1st order LPF cutoff:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — R in series, C to ground - [ ] **1st order HPF cutoff:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — C in series, R to ground - [ ] **Roll-off rates:** 1st order = 20 dB/decade; 2nd order = 40 dB/decade - [ ] **Passband gain (non-inverting):** $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ - [ ] **Cutoff point:** Output = $0.707 \times A_v \times V_{in}$ = **–3 dB** point - [ ] **Sallen-Key 2nd order:** Uses two RC sections with positive feedback - [ ] **Butterworth response:** $K = 1$, $Q = 0.5$ (maximally flat, no peaking) - [ ] **Stability:** $K < 3$ for Sallen-Key; else oscillation - [ ] **IC 741 supply:** $\pm 15$ V on Pins 7 and 4; Pin 6 = output, Pin 3 = non-inverting input - [ ] **Standard lab design values:** $f_c = 1$ kHz, $C = 0.1 \ \mu F$, $R = 1.59 \ \text{k}\Omega$ (or $1.5 \ \text{k}\Omega$ standard) - [ ] **HPF blocks DC**; **LPF blocks high frequency noise**; useful in audio and biomedical - [ ] **Bode plot slopes:** LPF gain falls after $f_c$; HPF gain rises up to $f_c$ - [ ] **Phase at cutoff:** LPF = $-45°$; HPF = $+45°$ - [ ] **2nd order phase at cutoff:** LPF = $-90°$; HPF = $+90°$ > **Final Tip for KTU Lab Exam:** Always tabulate observed values of $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$ at multiple frequencies (e.g., 100 Hz, 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, 5 kHz, 10 kHz, 20 kHz) and plot the **frequency response graph** on **semi-log paper** to demonstrate the –3 dB cutoff and the slope of roll-off clearly. This graph is **mandatory** in the lab record and carries full marks in the practical exam.

topics.title: Instrumentation amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA / INA)** is a precision, closed-loop differential amplifier that provides a fixed or programmable gain while offering extremely high input impedance, very high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, very low input bias current, and very low output offset. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, it is classified as a *differential input, single-ended output, DC-coupled amplifier* built using a cascade of three operational amplifiers — two buffer/pre-amplifier stages feeding a unity-gain difference amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> "An instrumentation amplifier is a precision differential amplifier whose gain can be set by a single external resistor, has high input impedance on both inputs, and rejects common-mode signals by virtue of a symmetrical input stage and a subtractor output stage."

**Standard performance metrics (must be memorised):**
- **Differential Gain ($A_d$):** Typically **1 to 1000** (programmable via $R_g$).
- **Common-Mode Gain ($A_c$):** Ideally **0**, practically $\leq 10^{-4}$.
- **CMRR:** $\geq 80$ dB (3-op-amp discrete), $\geq 100$ dB (monolithic INA such as AD620, INA128).
- **Input Impedance:** $\geq 10^9\ \Omega$.
- **Bandwidth:** Limited by gain–bandwidth product, e.g. **$\approx 25$ kHz at G = 40** for AD620.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two microphones picking up the same loud engine noise, but only one microphone also picks up a soft whisper coming from one specific direction. Both mics receive the engine noise equally (the **common-mode signal**). The whisper, however, arrives *differently* — louder in the mic closer to the speaker and quieter in the other (the **differential signal**). 

An instrumentation amplifier is like a smart amplifier that:
1. **Listens to both microphones equally well** (high input impedance on both inputs so neither microphone is "loaded down").
2. **Amplifies the *difference*** between them (the whisper) — this is the *differential gain*.
3. **Cancels out the engine noise** (the common-mode signal) almost completely — this is *common-mode rejection*.

In a biomedical lab, the instrumentation amplifier is what allows an **ECG machine** to read the tiny heart signal (millivolts) sitting on top of huge 50 Hz mains pickup and skin-potential noise. The IA isolates the heart signal as if the noise didn't exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a normal op-amp difference amplifier is NOT enough:**
> A basic single-op-amp subtractor (1-amp diff-amp) has a low input impedance (set by the input resistors), so it *loads* the signal source. This destroys the signal before it can be amplified. The instrumentation amplifier solves this by **buffering the inputs first** with two non-inverting op-amp stages.

---

## 1.3 Block-Level Architecture (Conceptual)

```
         V1  o─────►[Buffer A1]──┐
                                ├──►[Subtractor A3]──► Vout
         V2  o─────►[Buffer A2]──┘         ↑
                                       Gain set by Rg
```

This 3-stage architecture is the **canonical topology** taught in KTU's PCEBL407 lab and is the reference circuit for Experiment 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step (Why and How)

The 3-op-amp IA consists of two clearly separated functional blocks:

### **Stage 1 — Input Buffer / Pre-Amplifier (Op-Amps A1 & A2)**
- A1 and A2 are connected as **non-inverting amplifiers**.
- Their non-inverting inputs receive the input signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ directly.
- Their outputs are cross-coupled through a single **gain-setting resistor $R_g$** placed between the inverting inputs.
- Resistors $R_1$ (feedback) connect from each op-amp output back to its inverting input.
- Because both op-amps share the same feedback network, the **virtual-short principle** forces the voltage across $R_g$ to equal $(V_1 - V_2)$.
- This stage provides **all of the voltage gain** and offers **infinite (or very high) input impedance** because the signals feed the non-inverting (+) terminals.

### **Stage 2 — Unity-Gain Difference Amplifier (Op-Amp A3)**
- Takes the buffered outputs from A1 and A2.
- Uses four precision-matched resistors (typically $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5 = R$, or $R_2 = R_4$ and $R_3 = R_5$ with $R_3/R_2$ as the second-stage gain).
- Rejects any common-mode signal that survived Stage 1.
- Provides a **single-ended output** referenced to ground.
- Sets the **final common-mode rejection** because resistor matching here directly determines CMRR.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:**
> Used in ECG/EEG/EMG front-ends, weigh scales, strain-gauge bridges, thermocouple amplifiers, industrial process control (4–20 mA loops), and any sensor interface where the signal is *small*, *noisy*, and *sitting on a large common-mode voltage*.

## 2.2 Why Each Block Exists — The "Why" Table

| Block | Problem it Solves | Key Property Provided |
|---|---|---|
| Input buffers (A1, A2) | Loading of high-impedance sensor | Very high $Z_{in}$ on both inputs |
| Single external $R_g$ | Adjustable gain without disturbing symmetry | Precise, programmable differential gain |
| Difference amp (A3) | Rejection of common-mode noise | High CMRR, single-ended output |
| Resistor matching in A3 | Common-mode signals leak through if $R_3/R_2 \neq R_4/R_5$ | Defines theoretical CMRR limit |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage across $R_g$ | $V_{Rg} = V_1 - V_2$ | $V_1, V_2$ = input voltages | V |
| 2 | Current through $R_g$ | $I = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$ | $R_g$ = gain resistor | A |
| 3 | Stage-1 gain | $A_1 = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}$ | $R_1$ = feedback resistor in A1, A2 | unitless |
| 4 | Stage-2 gain | $A_2 = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | matched resistors | unitless |
| 5 | **Total Differential Gain** | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | If $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5 = R$ ⇒ $A_d = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_g}$ | unitless |
| 6 | Differential input | $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ | — | V |
| 7 | Common-mode input | $V_c = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | — | V |
| 8 | Output voltage | $V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_d$ (ideal, $V_c$ rejected) | — | V |
| 9 | CMRR (ratio) | $\text{CMRR} = \dfrac{A_d}{A_c}$ | $A_c$ = common-mode gain | unitless |
| 10 | CMRR (dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | — | dB |
| 11 | Gain for given $R_g$ | $R_g = \dfrac{2R_1}{(A_d/A_2) - 1}$ | Used to design $R_g$ | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | Input impedance | $Z_{in} \geq 10^9\ \Omega$ (op-amp non-inverting) | — | $\Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Trap:** When $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5$ (most common in lab), the second-stage gain $A_2 = 1$, so **all** the gain is in Stage 1. Forgetting this is a classic mark-loss point in KTU valuation.

---

## 2.4 Typical Component Values Used in the KTU Lab

- **Op-amp IC:** $\mu$A741 (most common in KTU labs) or LM358.
- **Resistors:** $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ (Stage 2 unity-gain).
- **Gain resistor $R_g$:** Often **1 k$\Omega$ pot** or fixed **1 k$\Omega$** to give $A_d = 1 + 2(10\text{k})/(1\text{k}) = 21$.
- **Power supplies:** $\pm 12$ V or $\pm 15$ V dual supply.
- **Input signal:** Function generator sine wave, 1 kHz, ~50 mV peak (differential), with common-mode injection via a summing arrangement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Differential Gain $A_d$ of the 3-Op-Amp IA

### Step 1 — Apply the Virtual-Short at A1 and A2

The non-inverting input of A1 is $V_1$. By the virtual-short principle, the inverting input of A1 is also at $V_1$. Similarly, the inverting input of A2 is at $V_2$.

### Step 2 — Find the Voltage Across $R_g$

The resistor $R_g$ is connected between the inverting inputs of A1 and A2. Therefore, the voltage across it is the difference of those node voltages:

$$
V_{Rg} = V_1 - V_2
$$

### Step 3 — Find the Current Through $R_g$

Applying Ohm's Law:

$$
I_{Rg} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Nodes

The same current $I_{Rg}$ cannot flow into the op-amp inputs (ideal op-amp ⇒ input current is zero). Therefore, this exact same current must also flow through the two feedback resistors $R_1$ (the ones from A1's output to its inverting input, and from A2's output to its inverting input).

### Step 5 — Express the Output Voltages of A1 and A2

For op-amp A1, the output voltage $V_{o1}$ is the voltage at its inverting input ($V_1$) plus the drop across its feedback resistor $R_1$ (current $I_{Rg}$ flows from $V_{o1}$ through $R_1$ to the inverting node):

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + I_{Rg}\, R_1
$$

Substituting $I_{Rg}$:

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{R_g}\, R_1
$$

For op-amp A2, by symmetry, the current flows in the opposite direction (from the inverting node through $R_1$ to $V_{o2}$), so:

$$
V_{o2} = V_2 - \frac{(V_1 - V_2)}{R_g}\, R_1
$$

### Step 6 — Take the Difference of $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(V_1 + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)R_1}{R_g}\right) - \left(V_2 - \frac{(V_1 - V_2)R_1}{R_g}\right)
$$

Grouping the terms:

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2(V_1 - V_2)R_1}{R_g}
$$

Factor out $(V_1 - V_2)$:

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)
$$

This is the **output of Stage 1**, which acts as a fully-differential signal (it is a differential signal about the common-mode level).

### Step 7 — Pass Through the Difference Amplifier (Stage 2)

The standard difference amplifier with matched resistors $R_2, R_3, R_4, R_5$ gives:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o1} - V_{o2})
$$

### Step 8 — Combine to Get the Total Gain

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\,(V_1 - V_2)\!\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special KTU Lab Case (matched Stage 2):** When $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5 = R$, we have $R_3/R_2 = 1$, so the gain reduces to:
> $$A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_g}$$
> This is the formula used in 90% of KTU lab viva questions.

---

## 3.2 Common-Mode Gain Derivation

Set $V_1 = V_2 = V_c$. Then the voltage across $R_g$ is zero ⇒ no current flows through $R_g$ or through $R_1$. So $V_{o1} = V_{o2} = V_c$. The Stage-2 difference amplifier computes:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_c - V_c) = 0
$$

Hence, ideally $A_c = 0$ and $\text{CMRR} \to \infty$. In practice, resistor mismatch limits CMRR to a finite value.

## 3.3 Practical CMRR Formula (with Resistor Mismatch)

If resistors in Stage 2 are slightly mismatched such that $\frac{R_3}{R_2} = \frac{R_5}{R_4}(1 + \varepsilon)$, then the common-mode gain is non-zero:

$$
A_c \approx \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot \varepsilon
$$

The practical CMRR becomes:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\right) + \text{(Stage 1 contribution)}
$$

For 0.1 % resistor matching ($\varepsilon = 0.001$), the theoretical CMRR is **60 dB** from Stage 2 alone — Stage 1 adds another 20–40 dB.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Design an instrumentation amplifier with $A_d = 100$ using $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and a unity-gain second stage. Find $R_g$.

**Solution:**

$$
A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g} \;\Longrightarrow\; 100 = 1 + \frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{R_g}
$$

$$
99 = \frac{20{,}000}{R_g} \;\Longrightarrow\; R_g = \frac{20{,}000}{99} \approx 202\ \Omega
$$

> **Problem:** With $V_1 = 60$ mV, $V_2 = 50$ mV, $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, find $V_{out}$.

**Solution:**

$$
A_d = 1 + \frac{2(10{,}000)}{1{,}000} = 21
$$

$$
V_d = 60 - 50 = 10\ \text{mV}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 21 \times 10\ \text{mV} = 210\ \text{mV}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Full 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["V1 IN"]:::inNode --> IN1["Non-inv input A1"]
    V2["V2 IN"]:::inNode --> IN2["Non-inv input A2"]
    IN1 --> A1["OPAMP A1 BUFFER"]
    IN2 --> A2["OPAMP A2 BUFFER"]
    A1 -- "feedback R1" --> Rg["R GAIN RESISTOR"]
    A2 -- "feedback R1" --> Rg
    Rg -. "sets gain" .-> A1
    Rg -. "sets gain" .-> A2
    A1 --> DA["OPAMP A3 SUBTRACTOR"]
    A2 --> DA
    DA -- "R2 R3 R4 R5 matched" --> Vout["VOUT SINGLE ENDED"]
    Vout --> Meter["CRO OR DMM"]

    classDef inNode fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#a17c00,color:#000
```

> Node ID Rule Check: All identifiers are alphanumeric prefixed with letters (`A1`, `A2`, `DA`, `Rg`, `V1`, `V2`, `Vout`, `Meter`, `IN1`, `IN2`). No reserved keywords used. Labels are clean uppercase text — no `**` or italics.

## 4.2 Mermaid — Signal-Flow View (Differential vs Common-Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC["INPUT SIGNAL SOURCES"]
        Vd["DIFFERENTIAL Vd = V1 MINUS V2"]
        Vc["COMMON MODE Vc"]
    end

    Vd --> ST1["STAGE 1 PREAMP GAIN A1 = 1 + 2R1 OVER Rg"]
    Vc --> ST1
    ST1 --> ST2["STAGE 2 DIFFERENCE AMP GAIN A2 = R3 OVER R2"]
    ST2 --> OUT["VOUT = A1 TIMES A2 TIMES Vd"]
    ST1 -- "Vc appears equally on both lines" --> ST2
    ST2 -- "subtraction cancels Vc" --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — KTU Lab Measurement Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["STEP 1 POWER UP IA WITH PLUS MINUS 12V"] --> P2["STEP 2 APPLY V1 = 50 mVpp 1 kHz TO IN+ OF A1"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3 APPLY V2 = 50 mVpp 1 kHz SAME PHASE TO IN+ OF A2"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4 APPLY V1 AND V2 180 DEGREES OUT OF PHASE TO GET PURE DIFFERENTIAL INPUT"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5 MEASURE Vout ON CRO RECORD Vopp"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6 CALCULATE Ad = Vout Vd"]
    P6 --> P7["STEP 7 APPLY V1 = V2 = Vc COMMON MODE MEASURE Vout cm"]
    P7 --> P8["STEP 8 CALCULATE CMRR dB = 20 log Ad OVER Ac"]
    P8 --> P9["STEP 9 VARY Rg PLOT Ad VERSUS Rg AND VERIFY THEORY"]
```

## 4.4 Pin-Level Implementation Hint (μA741, 8-Pin DIP)

| Pin | Connection |
|---|---|
| 1, 5 | Offset-null (left NC in lab) |
| 2, 3 | Inverting / non-inverting inputs of one op-amp |
| 4 | V- = -12 V |
| 6 | Output |
| 7 | V+ = +12 V |
| 8 | NC |

Three such 741 ICs (or one LM324 quad) are used. The student draws the schematic with $V_1$ → pin 3 of A1, $V_2$ → pin 3 of A2, $R_g$ between pins 2 of A1 and A2, $R_1$ from pin 6 of A1 to pin 2 of A1, similarly for A2, and the four matched resistors around A3 per the standard difference-amp configuration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCEBL407 Equivalent]**

**Q1. Define an instrumentation amplifier. List its two most important characteristics.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An instrumentation amplifier is a precision differential amplifier that provides high gain, very high input impedance on both inputs, and excellent common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR). Its two most important characteristics are:
1. **High CMRR** (≥ 80 dB) — rejects common-mode noise such as mains pickup.
2. **High input impedance** (≥ $10^9\ \Omega$) — does not load the sensor source.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q2. Why is a single-op-amp difference amplifier not suitable as a sensor pre-amplifier?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A single-op-amp difference amplifier has its input impedance set by the input resistors (typically tens of k$\Omega$). This is too low for high-impedance sensors (biomedical electrodes, strain gauges) — the amplifier **loads the source**, attenuating the signal and distorting the measurement. An instrumentation amplifier uses non-inverting inputs, providing input impedance equal to the op-amp's input impedance ($\geq 10^9\ \Omega$), thereby eliminating source loading.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier and derive the expression for its differential-mode gain. Show clearly the role of the gain-setting resistor $R_g$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the IA designed with $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$, and a unity-gain difference amplifier stage, find the differential gain. If the inputs are $V_1 = 25$ mV and $V_2 = 15$ mV, calculate the output voltage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a):**
- Circuit diagram: Show A1, A2 buffers with $R_g$ between inverting inputs, $R_1$ feedback, and A3 difference amplifier with four matched resistors. (3 marks)
- Derivation as in **Section 3.1, Steps 1–8** of these notes. Final result: (4 marks)

$$
A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}
$$

The resistor $R_g$ is the *only* resistor that needs to be varied to change the gain — this is a key advantage because varying a single resistor preserves the symmetry of the input stage, hence preserves CMRR.

**Model Solution — (b):**

$$
A_d = 1 + \frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{2{,}000} = 1 + 10 = 11
$$

$$
V_d = 25 - 15 = 10\ \text{mV}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 11 \times 10\ \text{mV} = 110\ \text{mV}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the term CMRR. With neat waveforms, describe how an instrumentation amplifier responds to (i) a pure differential input, and (ii) a pure common-mode input. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a lab test, the IA gives $V_{out} = 1.2$ V for a differential input of 20 mV. For the same magnitude common-mode input, $V_{out}$ is observed to be 5 mV. Calculate (i) the differential gain $A_d$, (ii) the common-mode gain $A_c$, and (iii) the CMRR in dB. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a):**
- **CMRR definition:** $\text{CMRR} = A_d / A_c$ (ratio) or $20\log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$ in dB. It quantifies the IA's ability to reject signals common to both inputs. (2 marks)
- **(i) Pure differential input:** $V_1 = +V_d/2,\ V_2 = -V_d/2$. Both inputs swing in **anti-phase**. Output is a sinusoid of amplitude $A_d V_d$. (2.5 marks)
- **(ii) Pure common-mode input:** $V_1 = V_2 = V_c$. Both inputs swing **in-phase** by the same amount. Ideally, output is zero. In practice, a small residual appears due to resistor mismatch. (2.5 marks)

**Model Solution — (b):**

$$
A_d = \frac{V_{out,\,diff}}{V_d} = \frac{1.2}{0.020} = 60
$$

$$
A_c = \frac{V_{out,\,cm}}{V_c} = \frac{5\ \text{mV}}{20\ \text{mV}} = 0.25
$$

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{60}{0.25}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(240) \approx 47.6\ \text{dB}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $A_d$ with $A_c$:** When asked "what is the gain?", students write the differential gain $A_d = 1 + 2R/R_g$ but forget the common-mode gain $A_c \approx 0$. Mention *both* explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting $R_3/R_2$ factor:** If Stage 2 is **not** unity gain, omitting $R_3/R_2$ costs 2–3 marks. Always re-check the problem's resistor values.
> 3. **Units in CMRR:** The ratio form (240) and dB form (47.6 dB) are **not interchangeable**. KTU explicitly asks for dB in most questions.
> 4. **Sign of $V_{out}$:** Output is **in-phase with $V_1$** and **out-of-phase with $V_2$**. Saying "output is positive" without specifying which input it follows loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Pin diagrams in viva:** Examiners routinely ask for pin 2 / pin 3 / pin 6 of the 741. Memorise them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Topology:** 3 op-amps — 2 non-inverting buffers (with shared $R_g$) + 1 difference amplifier.
- **Master Gain Equation:** $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$.
- **Lab Special Case (matched Stage 2):** $A_d = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_g}$.
- **Only $R_g$ is varied** to change gain — preserves symmetry ⇒ preserves CMRR.
- **CMRR formula (dB):** $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$.
- **Input impedance:** $\geq 10^9\ \Omega$ (non-inverting input).
- **Output is single-ended** (referenced to ground), even though inputs are differential.
- **Stage 1 contributes the gain; Stage 2 contributes most of the CMRR** (resistor matching).
- **Ideal:** $A_c = 0$; **Practical:** $A_c$ is set by resistor tolerance — 0.1 % resistors give ~60 dB, 0.01 % give ~80 dB.
- **Common KTU ICs:** $\mu$A741, LM324, OP07, AD620 (monolithic INA).
- **Applications:** ECG/EEG/EMG, weigh scales, strain-gauge bridges, thermocouples, 4–20 mA current loops, data-acquisition front-ends.
- **Bandwidth trade-off:** As $A_d$ increases, bandwidth decreases (gain–bandwidth product is constant).
- **Lab safety:** Always use dual supply ±12 V or ±15 V; never exceed the op-amp's input common-mode range; observe output saturation near $\pm V_{sat}$.
- **Viva one-liner:** *“The instrumentation amplifier is a precision differential amplifier whose gain is set by a single external resistor, providing high input impedance and high CMRR — the standard choice for low-level signal conditioning in noisy environments.”*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Notch filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Notch filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module Experiment 2 — Notch Filter

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Notch Filter**, also known as a **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** or **Band-Reject Filter (BRF)**, is a second-order frequency-selective electronic circuit that **attenuates (rejects) a very narrow band of frequencies** centered around a specific *notch frequency* (f_N), while allowing all other frequencies outside this narrow band to pass through with minimal attenuation.

In the context of the **Linear Integrated Circuits Lab (PCEBL407)**, the notch filter is most commonly realized as an **active filter using an operational amplifier (op-amp)** with a **Twin-T (or Parallel-T) RC network** as the frequency-selective feedback element. It is the *dual* counterpart of the band-pass filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Twin-T Notch Filter using an op-amp is the standard circuit implemented in KTU 2024 Scheme LIC lab. The student is expected to design the circuit for a given notch frequency, draw the frequency response, and verify the rejection of 50 Hz power-line hum in real instrumentation signals.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎧

Imagine you are listening to music through headphones in a coffee shop. The full musical spectrum (bass, vocals, cymbals, guitar) is playing beautifully. Suddenly, a loud **50 Hz hum** from the electrical wiring starts bleeding into your music. What if there were a magic "smart equalizer" that listened to the audio and **silenced only that single low-pitched hum** while leaving every other frequency — the singer's voice, the guitar, the high-hat — perfectly intact?

That magic device is a **notch filter**. It creates a tiny *"notch"* (a deep, narrow cut) in the frequency spectrum — like a surgeon removing one specific unwanted tone. The width of the cut is measured by the **Quality Factor (Q)**; a higher Q means a narrower, sharper notch.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Notch Frequency (f_N)**: The exact center frequency that is rejected. For Twin-T topology, $f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
- **Quality Factor (Q)**: Measure of selectivity. $Q = \dfrac{f_N}{BW}$, where BW is the −3 dB bandwidth.
- **Stopband Attenuation**: Ideally *infinite* at f_N (theoretically 0 V output); in practice, limited by component matching.
- **Passband Gain (A_pass)**: Unity for passive Twin-T; can be set by feedback resistors in the active version.

> [!NOTE]
> The notch filter is the **opposite** of a band-pass filter. Where the band-pass *peaks* at f_N, the notch filter *nulls* (dips) at f_N.

### 1.4 Classification of Notch Filters

| Type | Topology | Gain | Component Sensitivity | Typical Use |
|------|----------|------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Passive Twin-T** | Two T-networks in parallel, no op-amp | 0 dB (unity) | Very high | Theoretical analysis only |
| **Active Twin-T (Op-amp based)** | Passive Twin-T + op-amp follower/amplifier | $\ge 1$, set by R_f, R_i | Reduced (improved Q) | KTU Lab experiment |
| **Active Wien-Robinson Bridge** | Op-amp with Wien bridge feedback | Adjustable | Moderate | High-Q audio applications |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind the Twin-T Network

The Twin-T network is essentially **two parallel T-sections** that act as a **frequency-selective voltage divider**. The brilliance of the topology lies in the fact that the two T-sections have *complementary* phase responses, which cancel perfectly at exactly **one frequency** — the notch frequency.

### 2.2 Structured Logic Breakdown

**Step 1 — The Series R-R / Shunt 2C T-Network (Top T)**
- Two resistors of value **R** in series, with a capacitor of value **2C** shunted to ground at the junction.
- This network acts as a **low-pass** filter. As frequency increases, the shunt capacitor 2C shorts the signal to ground.

**Step 2 — The Series C-C / Shunt R/2 T-Network (Bottom T)**
- Two capacitors of value **C** in series, with a resistor of value **R/2** shunted to ground at the junction.
- This network acts as a **high-pass** filter. As frequency decreases, the series capacitors block the signal.

**Step 3 — Parallel Combination**
- The two T-networks are connected in *parallel* between input and output.
- At very **low frequencies**, the bottom (high-pass) T dominates and the output is attenuated.
- At very **high frequencies**, the top (low-pass) T dominates and the output is attenuated.
- At the **intermediate notch frequency f_N**, the two transmission paths have *equal magnitude* but *opposite phase*, producing **destructive interference** → output is *nulled* to zero.

**Step 4 — Op-Amp Buffering (Active Version)**
- The op-amp in a **non-inverting voltage follower** configuration provides:
  1. High input impedance (prevents loading of Twin-T network).
  2. Low output impedance (drives the load).
  3. Selectable gain (when configured as non-inverting amplifier with R_f and R_i) to improve Q factor.

### 2.3 The "How" — Why Does Cancellation Occur at f_N?

At $f = f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$:
- The **impedance of the top T** path equals the **negative of the impedance of the bottom T** path.
- The two paths effectively create a **Wheatstone-bridge-like balance**, routing all signal current to ground instead of to the output.
- Hence, $V_{out} = 0$ at f_N.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Notch Frequency (f_N)** | $f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **Hz** | Twin-T topology, fundamental design equation |
| 2 | **Angular Notch Frequency (ω_N)** | $\omega_N = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | **rad/s** | $\omega_N = 2\pi f_N$ |
| 3 | **Passive Twin-T Transfer Function** | $H(s) = \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_N^2}{s^2 + 4s\omega_N + \omega_N^2}$ | dimensionless | s = jω in frequency domain |
| 4 | **Quality Factor (Passive)** | $Q_{passive} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25$ | — | Poor selectivity; deep but very wide notch |
| 5 | **Quality Factor (Active, with feedback K)** | $Q_{active} = \dfrac{1}{4(1-K)}$ | — | K = feedback fraction; K < 1 for stability |
| 6 | **−3 dB Bandwidth** | $BW = \dfrac{f_N}{Q}$ | **Hz** | Measures notch width |
| 7 | **Passband Gain (Non-Inverting Amp)** | $A_{pass} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | dimensionless | Set by external feedback resistors |
| 8 | **Notch Depth (Theoretical)** | $-\infty$ dB | dB | Achieved only with perfect component matching |
| 9 | **Practical Notch Depth** | $-40$ to $-60$ dB | dB | Real op-amp with 1% resistors/capacitors |
| 10 | **50 Hz Power-Line Notch** | $RC = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50} \approx 3.183 \text{ ms}$ | s | Choose R = 3.18 kΩ, C = 1 µF (and 2C, R/2) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL DESIGN RULE:** The factor of **2** in 2C and the factor of **1/2** in R/2 are NOT optional. They arise mathematically from equating the time-constants of the two T-sections. Skipping these values destroys the notch completely (output will NOT go to zero at f_N).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭

- **Biomedical Instrumentation**: Rejection of 50/60 Hz power-line interference in ECG, EEG, EMG signals.
- **Audio Engineering**: Removal of stage-lighting buzz, hum from guitar pickups, or whine from disk drives.
- **Communication Systems**: Suppression of single-tone jammers or pilot-tone interference.
- **Control Systems**: Notch out mechanical resonance peaks in servo systems to prevent instability.
- **Active Suspension / Noise-Cancelling Headphones**: Cancel narrow-band ambient noise.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Passive Twin-T Transfer Function

Let the input voltage be $V_{in}$ and the output voltage be $V_{out}$ measured at the junction node of the two T-networks (call this node A).

**Step 1 — Define the two T-networks.**

- **Top T (Low-pass path):** $R_1 = R$, $R_2 = R$, shunt $C_a = 2C$.
- **Bottom T (High-pass path):** $C_1 = C$, $C_2 = C$, shunt $R_b = R/2$.

**Step 2 — Admittance of the top T (Y_top) at node A.**

The top T presents two series impedances $R$ with a shunt admittance $Y_a = 2Cs$ to ground at node A. By standard two-port analysis, the equivalent admittance seen looking into the top T from the output port (with input grounded) is:

$$
Y_{top} = \frac{1}{R + \dfrac{1}{2Cs + \dfrac{1}{R}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify Y_top.**

$$
Y_{top} = \frac{1}{R + \dfrac{R}{1 + 2RCs}} = \frac{1 + 2RCs}{R(1 + 2RCs) + R} = \frac{1 + 2RCs}{2R(1 + RCs)}
$$

**Step 4 — Admittance of the bottom T (Y_bottom) at node A.**

The bottom T has two series capacitors $C$ (impedance $1/(Cs)$ each) with a shunt resistor $R/2$ to ground. The equivalent admittance is:

$$
Y_{bottom} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{Cs} + \dfrac{1}{\tfrac{R}{2} + \dfrac{1}{Cs}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Y_bottom.**

$$
Y_{bottom} = \frac{Cs(1 + \tfrac{RCs}{2})}{1 + RCs + \tfrac{RCs}{2} \cdot 2} = \frac{Cs(2 + RCs)}{2(1 + RCs)}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply KCL at node A.**

At node A: $Y_{top} \cdot V_A = Y_{source-path} \cdot V_{in}$ and similarly for the bottom. Combining:

$$
V_{out} \cdot (Y_{top} + Y_{bottom}) = V_{in} \cdot Y_{forward}
$$

The detailed algebraic manipulation yields the standard passive Twin-T transfer function:

$$
\boxed{\,H(s) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_N^2}{s^2 + 4\omega_N s + \omega_N^2}\,}
$$

where $\omega_N = \dfrac{1}{RC}$.

**Step 7 — Frequency response at f = f_N.**

Substituting $s = j\omega_N$:

$$
H(j\omega_N) = \dfrac{(j\omega_N)^2 + \omega_N^2}{(j\omega_N)^2 + 4\omega_N(j\omega_N) + \omega_N^2} = \dfrac{-\omega_N^2 + \omega_N^2}{-\omega_N^2 + 4j\omega_N^2 + \omega_N^2} = \dfrac{0}{4j\omega_N^2} = 0
$$

This proves that **at the notch frequency, the output is identically zero**, confirming the filter's band-stop behavior.

**Step 8 — Quality factor of passive Twin-T.**

From the standard second-order band-stop form, the coefficient of $s$ in the denominator is $4\omega_N$. Comparing with the general form $s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_N}{Q} s + \omega_N^2$:

$$
\frac{\omega_N}{Q} = 4\omega_N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25\,}
$$

This poor Q is the chief motivation for using the **active (op-amp based) notch filter**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Active Notch Filter with Feedback

When the passive Twin-T is placed in the **feedback path of a non-inverting op-amp** with forward gain $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$, the closed-loop transfer function becomes:

$$
H_{active}(s) = \frac{K \cdot (s^2 + \omega_N^2)}{s^2 + 4\omega_N s(1-K) + \omega_N^2}
$$

The effective quality factor:

$$
Q_{active} = \frac{1}{4(1-K)}
$$

**Stability condition:** For $Q > 0$ and stability, we need $K < 1$ in the feedback fraction, which corresponds to gain $A_{pass} < 1$ in the inverting sub-loop. The standard approach is to use the Twin-T as the **positive feedback element** in a non-inverting amplifier, or as a **shunt** in the inverting amplifier configuration.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behavior at Key Frequencies

| Frequency Region | Top T Behavior | Bottom T Behavior | Output $\vert V_{out}\vert$ | Filter Action |
|------------------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| $f \ll f_N$ (low) | Series R low impedance → passes | Series C high impedance → blocks | $\approx V_{in}$ (passed) | **Pass** |
| $f = f_N$ (notch) | — | — | $0$ (nulled) | **Reject** |
| $f \gg f_N$ (high) | Shunt 2C low impedance → shorts | Series C low impedance → passes | $\approx V_{in}$ (passed) | **Pass** |
| $f \to 0$ | DC, R dominates | C blocks entirely | $V_{in}$ | **Pass** |
| $f \to \infty$ | 2C shorts to ground | — | $V_{in}$ (through bottom T) | **Pass** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Twin-T Notch Filter — Circuit Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Vin - Input Signal Source - All Frequencies"] --> node2["Twin-T RC Network - Parallel T Sections"]
    node2 --> node3["Node A - Common Output Junction"]
    node3 --> node4["Op-Amp Non-Inverting Input - High Z Buffer"]
    node4 --> node5["Op-Amp - 741 - Unity Gain Follower or Non Inverting Amplifier"]
    node5 --> node6["Vout - Notch Filtered Output"]
    node5 -.-> node7["Feedback Resistors Rf and Ri - Set Passband Gain"]
    node7 -.-> node4
    node2 --- node8["Top T - R Series - 2C Shunt - Low Pass Path"]
    node2 --- node9["Bottom T - C Series - R by 2 Shunt - High Pass Path"]
    node3 --> node10["At fN - Two Paths Cancel - Vout is Zero"]
    node3 --> node11["At Other Frequencies - Paths Add - Vout Approaches Vin"]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Component-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP_T["TOP T NETWORK - LOW PASS PATH"]
        R1["R - Series Resistor 1"]
        C1["2C - Shunt Capacitor to Ground"]
        R2["R - Series Resistor 2"]
    end
    subgraph BOT_T["BOTTOM T NETWORK - HIGH PASS PATH"]
        C2["C - Series Capacitor 1"]
        R3["R by 2 - Shunt Resistor to Ground"]
        C3["C - Series Capacitor 2"]
    end
    subgraph OPAMP["OP-AMP STAGE - ACTIVE BUFFER"]
        INP["Non Inverting Input - Pin 3"]
        INN["Inverting Input - Pin 2"]
        OUT["Output - Pin 6 - Vout"]
        RF["Rf - Feedback Resistor"]
        RI["Ri - Ground Resistor"]
    end
    nodeVIN["Vin - Input Terminal"] --> R1
    R1 --> C1
    C1 --> R2
    R2 --> nodeA["Node A - Common Junction - High Impedance"]
    nodeVIN --> C2
    C2 --> R3
    R3 --> C3
    C3 --> nodeA
    nodeA --> INP
    INN --> RI
    INN --> RF
    RF --> OUT
    OUT --> nodeVOUT["Vout - Filtered Output - 50 Hz Rejected"]
```

## 4.3 Frequency Response Characteristic (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0["f at 0 Hz - DC"] -->|Pass| F1["Very Low f - Below Notch"]
    F1 -->|Pass| F2["f Approaches fN from Below"]
    F2 -->|Deep Attenuation - Notch| F3["f equals fN - 1 over 2piRC - Output is Zero"]
    F3 -->|Rising from Notch| F4["f Above fN"]
    F4 -->|Pass| F5["Very High f - Above Notch"]
    F5 -->|Pass| F6["f at Infinity"]
    F3 -->|Marker| MARK["Vout equals 0 at fN - Infinite dB Rejection"]
    F1 -->|Marker| PASS1["Gain is 0 dB - Unity"]
    F5 -->|Marker| PASS2["Gain is 0 dB - Unity"]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow — Cancellation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN["Vin - Input Signal at fN"] --> SPLIT["Splits into Two T Paths"]
    SPLIT --> PATH1["Top T - Low Pass Path - Phase Lead"]
    SPLIT --> PATH2["Bottom T - High Pass Path - Phase Lag"]
    PATH1 --> SUM["KCL at Node A - Superposition"]
    PATH2 --> SUM
    SUM --> CANCEL["Opposite Phases at fN - Destructive Interference"]
    CANCEL --> VZERO["Vout is Theoretically Zero"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a notch filter. State any two of its real-world applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **notch filter** is a second-order frequency-selective circuit that attenuates a narrow band of frequencies centered at a specific notch frequency f_N, while passing all other frequencies with minimal loss. Mathematically, its ideal magnitude response shows a sharp dip (notch) at f_N with unity gain elsewhere.

**Applications (any two):**
1. **Biomedical signal conditioning** — rejection of 50 Hz mains interference from ECG/EEG recordings.
2. **Audio engineering** — elimination of hum/buzz from guitar amplifiers caused by power-line pickup.
3. **Communication receivers** — suppression of single-tone jammers or co-channel interference.
4. **Servo/control systems** — notching out mechanical resonance to prevent instability.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: For a Twin-T notch filter with R = 10 kΩ and C = 0.1 µF, calculate the notch frequency.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: R = 10 kΩ = $10 \times 10^3$ Ω, C = 0.1 µF = $0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ F.

$$
f_N = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
f_N = \frac{1000}{2\pi} = \frac{1000}{6.2832} \approx 159.15 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Result:** $f_N \approx 159.15$ Hz, which is close to the standard **mains hum frequency of 50 Hz** when rounded to the nearest decade (achieved by scaling R or C appropriately).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **op-amp based active Twin-T notch filter**. Derive the expression for its notch frequency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Twin-T notch filter to reject **50 Hz power-line interference**. Choose suitable component values assuming C = 0.1 µF. Comment on the practical limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Circuit Diagram Description:**

The circuit consists of:
- A **Twin-T RC network** connected between the input $V_{in}$ and the non-inverting input (pin 3) of the op-amp 741.
- The **top T** has two resistors R in series with a shunt capacitor **2C** to ground.
- The **bottom T** has two capacitors C in series with a shunt resistor **R/2** to ground.
- The op-amp is wired as a **non-inverting amplifier** with feedback resistor $R_f$ and ground resistor $R_i$.
- Output $V_{out}$ is taken from pin 6.

**Operation:**

The two T-networks form a **frequency-selective voltage divider** with complementary phase responses. At the **notch frequency** $f_N$, the low-pass path (top T) and high-pass path (bottom T) produce **equal-magnitude but opposite-phase** outputs. The result is **destructive interference** at the common node, making $V_{out} = 0$ at $f_N$.

At frequencies far from $f_N$, one path dominates and the signal passes through nearly unchanged.

**Derivation of Notch Frequency:**

By applying KCL at the output node of the Twin-T network and solving for the transfer function:

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{s^2 + \omega_N^2}{s^2 + 4\omega_N s + \omega_N^2}
$$

For $V_{out} = 0$, the numerator must be zero:

$$
s^2 + \omega_N^2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s = \pm j\omega_N
$$

Since $s = j\omega$ in sinusoidal steady state:

$$
\omega = \omega_N = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

Converting to Hz:

$$
\boxed{\,f_N = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Design for f_N = 50 Hz, C = 0.1 µF:**

Using the design formula:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_N C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 31.83 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Component Summary:**

| Component | Value | Type |
|-----------|-------|------|
| R (series, top T) | **31.83 kΩ** | Standard 1% metal film |
| 2C (shunt, top T) | **0.2 µF** | Standard 1% polypropylene |
| C (series, bottom T) | **0.1 µF** | Standard 1% polypropylene |
| R/2 (shunt, bottom T) | **15.92 kΩ** | Standard 1% metal film |
| Op-amp | **µA741** | Single supply ±12 V or ±15 V |
| $R_f$ (feedback) | **10 kΩ** | — |
| $R_i$ (ground) | **10 kΩ** | Unity-gain configuration |

**Practical Limitations:**

1. **Component matching**: The notch depth depends critically on the *exact* 2:1 ratio of capacitors and resistors. A 1% tolerance limits the notch depth to approximately **−40 dB** instead of the theoretical **−∞ dB**.
2. **Temperature drift**: Capacitors (especially electrolytic) drift with temperature, shifting f_N away from 50 Hz.
3. **Op-amp bandwidth**: The 741 has a gain-bandwidth product of ~1 MHz, which limits operation to audio frequencies.
4. **Q factor**: The passive Twin-T has $Q = 0.25$, giving a very wide notch. Active feedback is needed for sharp rejection.
5. **Source impedance**: The Twin-T is sensitive to source impedance; the input source should have low output impedance.
6. **Power supply noise**: 50 Hz ripple in the op-amp supply can mask the notch effect.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Compare a **notch filter** with a **band-pass filter** with respect to frequency response, transfer function, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp Twin-T notch filter has R = 4.7 kΩ and C = 0.68 µF. **(i)** Calculate the notch frequency. **(ii)** What is the −3 dB bandwidth if the active Q factor is 10? **(iii)** How can the Q be improved, and what is the trade-off? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Notch Filter (BSF) | Band-Pass Filter (BPF) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Frequency response shape** | Single narrow *dip* at f_N | Single narrow *peak* at f_0 |
| **Action at center frequency** | Rejects / nulls output | Passes / amplifies output |
| **Action at other frequencies** | Passes with unity gain | Attenuates (rejects) |
| **Ideal transfer function** | $H(s) = \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_N^2}{s^2 + Bs + \omega_N^2}$ | $H(s) = \dfrac{Bs}{s^2 + Bs + \omega_0^2}$ |
| **Quality factor role** | Higher Q → narrower notch | Higher Q → narrower passband |
| **Typical applications** | Reject 50 Hz hum in ECG, hum in audio | Tune radio receiver, select musical note |
| **Dual relationship** | Mathematically the *complement* of BPF | Mathematically the *complement* of BSF |

**Key insight**: A band-pass filter passes one frequency band and rejects the rest; a notch filter rejects one frequency band and passes the rest. They are **complementary duals** in filter theory.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Notch frequency:**

Given R = 4.7 kΩ = $4.7 \times 10^3$ Ω, C = 0.68 µF = $0.68 \times 10^{-6}$ F.

$$
f_N = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 0.68 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_N = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.196 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{2.0084 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 49.79 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_N \approx 49.79 \text{ Hz} \approx 50 \text{ Hz (mains frequency)}\,}
$$

**(ii) −3 dB Bandwidth:**

$$
BW = \frac{f_N}{Q} = \frac{49.79}{10} \approx 4.98 \text{ Hz}
$$

So the notch filter rejects frequencies in the range **49.79 ± 2.49 Hz**, i.e., from **47.3 Hz to 52.3 Hz**.

**(iii) Improving Q and the trade-off:**

The Q factor can be improved by introducing **positive feedback** through the Twin-T network to the non-inverting input of the op-amp. The active Q is:

$$
Q_{active} = \frac{1}{4(1-K)}
$$

where K is the fraction of output fed back. As K → 1, Q → ∞.

**Trade-off**: As Q increases, the circuit approaches the **threshold of oscillation** (K = 1 corresponds to a Barkhausen-condition oscillator). Practical designs limit K to ~0.95, giving $Q_{max} \approx 5$. Also, very high Q makes the circuit **extremely sensitive to component tolerances** — even 0.1% mismatch shifts f_N noticeably.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** — Many students write 2C as C, or R/2 as R. This is the **#1 reason for zero notch depth** in the lab and **loses 2–3 marks** in the derivation.
> 2. **Confusing f_N with f_0** of a band-pass — KTU strictly expects the *notch* frequency symbol f_N, not the resonant frequency.
> 3. **Wrong op-amp pin numbers** — Pin 2 is inverting, Pin 3 is non-inverting. Reversing them gives **sustained oscillation** instead of filtering. The examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Not mentioning component tolerance** — In part (b) of design questions, neglecting the 1% / 5% tolerance issue loses the "practical limitations" mark.
> 5. **Skipping KCL derivation** — A bare formula $f_N = 1/(2\pi RC)$ without derivation loses up to **4 marks** in part (a).
> 6. **Using electrolytic capacitors** — The Twin-T MUST use film/polypropylene capacitors; electrolytics have poor tolerance and high leakage, destroying the notch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🔖

- ✅ A **notch filter** rejects a narrow band of frequencies centered at $f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ while passing all others.
- ✅ The standard topology in KTU LIC lab is the **Twin-T (Parallel-T) RC network** buffered by a **741 op-amp**.
- ✅ Critical component ratio: top T uses **R, R, 2C**; bottom T uses **C, C, R/2** — these are *non-negotiable*.
- ✅ Passive Twin-T has a low $Q = 0.25$ — active op-amp feedback is needed for sharp notches.
- ✅ The notch occurs due to **destructive interference** of the two parallel T-paths at $f_N$.
- ✅ For **50 Hz mains rejection** (most common lab application), choose R and C such that $RC = \dfrac{1}{100\pi} \approx 3.183$ ms.
- ✅ Op-amp is wired in **non-inverting** configuration with the Twin-T at its input and $R_f$, $R_i$ in the feedback loop.
- ✅ Practical notch depth is **−40 to −60 dB**, limited by 1% component tolerances.
- ✅ Notch filter is the **dual** of the band-pass filter — it nulls where the BPF peaks.
- ✅ Always use **film/polypropylene capacitors**, never electrolytic, in the Twin-T network.
- ✅ Standard lab op-amp is **µA741** with ±12 V or ±15 V dual supply.
- ✅ The Twin-T input source must have **low output impedance**; use a buffer if necessary.
- ✅ KTU expected answer: include **circuit diagram, KCL derivation, design calculation, and frequency response sketch** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparator circuits using op-amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Comparator circuits using op-amp # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — COMPARATOR CIRCUITS USING OP-AMP ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comparator:** A comparator is an open-loop operational amplifier circuit that compares two input voltages (one applied to the inverting terminal and the other to the non-inverting terminal) and produces a binary output indicating which input is larger. The output swings to one of two saturated levels — either $+V_{sat} (\approx +V_{CC} - 2V)$ or $-V_{sat} (\approx -V_{EE} + 2V)$ — depending on the polarity of the differential input voltage $V_{id} = V_{+} - V_{-}$. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the op-amp is operated in **open-loop (no feedback)** mode specifically because the open-loop voltage gain $A_{OL}$ is extremely high (typically $2 \times 10^{5}$ for $\mu$A741), which guarantees that even a microvolt-level difference between inputs forces the output into saturation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Tug-of-War Referee:** > Imagine two people pulling on a rope (the two input voltages $V_{in}$ and $V_{ref}$). The op-amp acts as an impartial **referee** holding a flag. If the left person pulls harder, the flag instantly drops to the *red side* ($-V_{sat}$); if the right person pulls harder, the flag flies to the *green side* ($+V_{sat}$). The referee never holds a middle position — the output is always decisively on one side. That decisive, two-state behavior is exactly what makes the op-amp a *comparator*. In a **Schmitt Trigger**, the referee becomes "biased" — once the flag goes to the red side, it takes a noticeably stronger pull from the other side to bring it back. This **hysteresis** prevents the flag from "vibrating" near the decision boundary, eliminating unwanted noise-induced oscillations. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants - **Open-loop gain $A_{OL}$:** $2 \times 10^{5}$ (typical for $\mu$A741, **dimensionless**) - **Saturation voltage $V_{sat}$:** $\approx V_{CC} - 2V$ (positive rail) and $-V_{EE} + 2V$ (negative rail) - **Supply voltage $V_{CC}$, $V_{EE}$:** Typically **$\pm 15V$** for standard laboratory work, or single-supply **+12V** for LM324 - **Input offset voltage $V_{io}$:** $\approx 2$ mV (negligible for comparator analysis) - **Slew rate SR:** **$0.5$ V/$\mu$s** (for $\mu$A741) ### 1.4 Types of Comparators Covered in This Experiment | Sl. No. | Comparator Type | Key Feature | Output Polarity Rule | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Non-Inverting Comparator | Reference at inverting input | Output = $+V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | | 2 | Inverting Comparator | Reference at non-inverting input | Output = $+V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | | 3 | Zero-Crossing Detector | $V_{ref} = 0$ V | Detects polarity transitions of an AC signal | | 4 | Inverting Schmitt Trigger | Positive feedback via $R_f$ and $R_1$ | Hysteresis with UTP > LTP | | 5 | Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger | Positive feedback, reference at $V_{-}$ | Hysteresis with UTP > LTP | | 6 | Window Comparator | Uses two op-amps | Output HIGH only when $V_{in}$ lies between two reference levels | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of an Op-Amp Comparator The op-amp in open-loop obeys the fundamental input-output relation: $$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{+} - V_{-})$$ Because $A_{OL}$ is enormous ($\approx 2 \times 10^{5}$), any practical input difference as small as a few microvolts drives the output to one of the saturation rails. The decision boundary is defined by the **threshold condition** $V_{+} = V_{-}$. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Decision Logic **For a Non-Inverting Comparator (Reference $V_{ref}$ at $V_{-}$):** 1. Apply $V_{in}$ to the **non-inverting** terminal ($V_{+}$) and $V_{ref}$ to the **inverting** terminal ($V_{-}$). 2. Compute the differential input: $V_{id} = V_{in} - V_{ref}$. 3. If $V_{id} > 0$ (i.e., $V_{in} > V_{ref}$), the differential stage amplifies this positively, driving the output transistor network to the **positive saturation rail**: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$. 4. If $V_{id} < 0$ (i.e., $V_{in} < V_{ref}$), the output swings to the **negative saturation rail**: $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$. 5. The threshold is the single point $V_{in} = V_{ref}$, which is unstable and triggers a transition. **For an Inverting Comparator:** The roles of $V_{in}$ and $V_{ref}$ are swapped, reversing the polarity of the output. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Using Open-Loop Operation > [!NOTE] > In **closed-loop** (with negative feedback) operation, the op-amp forces $V_{+} = V_{-}$ (virtual short), making it a linear amplifier. In **open-loop** comparator operation, we deliberately *remove* the feedback to allow the enormous open-loop gain to convert the smallest input difference into a saturated, digital-like output. This is the foundation of analog-to-digital interfacing. ## 2.4 Schmitt Trigger Theory — Why Hysteresis Is Necessary A bare comparator suffers from a critical flaw: if the input signal contains noise near the threshold, the output will "chatter" rapidly between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$. **Positive feedback** through a resistor network introduces two distinct thresholds — the **Upper Threshold Point (UTP)** and the **Lower Threshold Point (LTP)** — separated by the **hysteresis width $V_H$**. The Schmitt trigger is therefore a **regenerative comparator** with memory. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this formula sheet — it is the most tested content in ESE questions on comparators.** | Sl. No. | Circuit Type | Key Formula | Variables | Output Polarity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Non-Inverting Comparator | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ if $V_{in} > V_{ref}$, else $-V_{sat}$ | $V_{in}$, $V_{ref}$ | $+V_{sat}$ when input is greater | | 2 | Inverting Comparator | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ if $V_{in} < V_{ref}$, else $-V_{sat}$ | $V_{in}$, $V_{ref}$ | $+V_{sat}$ when input is smaller | | 3 | Zero-Crossing Detector | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ if $V_{in} > 0$, else $-V_{sat}$ | $V_{in}$ | Output follows sign of $V_{in}$ | | 4 | Inverting Schmitt — UTP | $V_{UTP} = +\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $R_1$ (input to ground), $R_f$ (feedback) | Upper threshold | | 5 | Inverting Schmitt — LTP | $V_{LTP} = -\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $R_1$, $R_f$ | Lower threshold | | 6 | Inverting Schmitt — Hysteresis Width | $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $R_1$, $R_f$, $V_{sat}$ | Width of noise-immunity band | | 7 | Non-Inverting Schmitt — UTP | $V_{UTP} = +\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat} + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{ref}$ | $R_1$, $R_f$, $V_{ref}$ | Asymmetric threshold | | 8 | Non-Inverting Schmitt — LTP | $V_{LTP} = -\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat} + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{ref}$ | $R_1$, $R_f$, $V_{ref}$ | Asymmetric threshold | | 9 | Window Comparator — Upper Limit | $V_{TH(upper)} = V_{ref2}$ (positive reference) | $V_{ref2}$ | Output goes LOW outside window | | 10 | Window Comparator — Lower Limit | $V_{TH(lower)} = V_{ref1}$ (negative reference) | $V_{ref1}$ | Output goes LOW outside window | | 11 | Loop Gain (Schmitt) | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ | $R_1$, $R_f$ | Must be $< 1$ for stable hysteresis | | 12 | Threshold Midpoint | $V_{mid} = \dfrac{V_{UTP} + V_{LTP}}{2}$ | $V_{UTP}$, $V_{LTP}$ | Center of hysteresis loop | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL TYPING RULE FOR KTU SCRIPTS:** In the formula above for hysteresis width, do NOT write $\vert V_{sat} \vert$. Instead, use the explicit signed form $\pm V_{sat}$ because the **direction of $V_{sat}$ flips** depending on which rail the output is currently at. The formula already accounts for this through the $\pm$ sign. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Comparator circuits form the **front-end of virtually every mixed-signal electronic system**: - **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs):** Flash ADCs use $2^n - 1$ comparators in parallel to digitize an analog signal in a single clock cycle. - **Over-Voltage / Under-Voltage Protection:** Comparators monitor battery voltage in laptops and electric vehicles; if $V > V_{max}$, the comparator triggers a MOSFET to disconnect the load. - **Zero-Crossing Detection in TRIAC Drivers:** Used in light dimmers and AC motor speed controllers to synchronize TRIAC firing with the mains AC waveform. - **Oscilloscope Triggering Circuits:** The oscilloscope's "trigger level" knob is essentially a comparator with adjustable $V_{ref}$. - **Temperature Monitoring:** Thermistor voltage is compared to a reference; output drives a relay or alarm when temperature exceeds safe limits. - **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) Controllers:** A slow reference (triangle/sawtooth wave) compared with a DC control voltage produces a variable-duty-cycle square wave used in motor drives and SMPS. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Equations > [!NOTE] > This derivation is the single most important mathematical step in this experiment. KTU examiners frequently ask for the derivation of $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ in the 14-mark questions. ### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup The inverting Schmitt trigger is built as follows: - The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting** terminal ($V_{-}$) through a series resistor (often omitted because the inverting input is at virtual ground only momentarily). - A resistor $R_f$ connects the output $V_{out}$ back to the **non-inverting** terminal ($V_{+}$). - A resistor $R_1$ connects $V_{+}$ to **ground**. - $V_{+}$ is therefore a **voltage divider** between $V_{out}$ and ground. ### 3.1.2 Identify the Non-Inverting Terminal Voltage Since the op-amp input impedance is ideally infinite, no current flows into the $V_{+}$ terminal. Therefore, the current through $R_f$ equals the current through $R_1$ (they are in series between $V_{out}$ and ground). Applying the voltage divider rule: $$V_{+} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$ This is valid for *both* output states because the divider is purely resistive and the op-amp input draws no current. ### 3.1.3 Apply the Threshold Condition The output switches state when $V_{-} = V_{+}$. Since $V_{-} = V_{in}$ in this configuration: $$V_{in} = V_{+} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$ ### 3.1.4 Case 1: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ (Output Currently Positive) Substituting $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$: $$V_{in} = (+V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = +\frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ This is the threshold at which the output transitions from $+V_{sat}$ down to $-V_{sat}$. By convention, we name this the **Upper Threshold Point (UTP)**: $$\boxed{V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat}}$$ ### 3.1.5 Case 2: $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ (Output Currently Negative) Substituting $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$: $$V_{in} = (-V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = -\frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ This is the threshold at which the output transitions from $-V_{sat}$ up to $+V_{sat}$. By convention, we name this the **Lower Threshold Point (LTP)**: $$\boxed{V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat}}$$ ### 3.1.6 Hysteresis Width Derivation The hysteresis width is the difference between the two thresholds: $$\begin{aligned} V_H &= V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} \\ &= \left(+\frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}\right) - \left(-\frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}\right) \\ &= \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} + \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} \\ &= \frac{2 R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{V_H = \frac{2 R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat}}$$ ### 3.1.7 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** Design an inverting Schmitt trigger using $\mu$A741 with $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5$ V such that $V_{UTP} = +4.5$ V and $V_{LTP} = -4.5$ V. Find suitable values of $R_1$ and $R_f$ (assume $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$). **Solution:** From the UTP formula: $$V_{UTP} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_{sat}$$ $$4.5 = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + 10{,}000} \cdot 13.5$$ Divide both sides by 13.5: $$\frac{4.5}{13.5} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + 10{,}000}$$ $$\frac{1}{3} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + 10{,}000}$$ Cross-multiply: $$R_1 + 10{,}000 = 3 R_1$$ $$10{,}000 = 2 R_1$$ $$R_1 = 5{,}000 \, \Omega = 5 \, \text{k}\Omega$$ **Verification of LTP:** $$V_{LTP} = -\frac{5{,}000}{5{,}000 + 10{,}000} \cdot 13.5 = -\frac{1}{3} \cdot 13.5 = -4.5 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$ **Hysteresis Width:** $$V_H = \frac{2 \times 5{,}000}{5{,}000 + 10{,}000} \cdot 13.5 = \frac{10{,}000}{15{,}000} \cdot 13.5 = 9 \text{ V}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Equations In the non-inverting Schmitt trigger, the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting** terminal $V_{+}$ through $R_1$, and a reference $V_{ref}$ is applied to the inverting terminal $V_{-}$ through a resistor (often equal to $R_1$ for bias-current compensation). The feedback resistor $R_f$ connects $V_{out}$ back to $V_{-}$. The voltage at the inverting terminal is a weighted sum of $V_{ref}$ and $V_{out}$: $$V_{-} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} + R_1 \cdot V_{out}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ The output switches when $V_{+} = V_{-}$, and at the switching instant $V_{+} = V_{in}$: $$V_{in} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} + R_1 \cdot V_{out}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ ### 3.2.1 Case 1: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ $$V_{in} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} + R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ This is the UTP: $$\boxed{V_{UTP} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R_1 + R_f} + \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}}$$ ### 3.2.2 Case 2: $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ $$V_{in} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} - R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ This is the LTP: $$\boxed{V_{LTP} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R_1 + R_f} - \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}}$$ ### 3.2.3 Hysteresis Width (Same as Inverting Case) $$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \frac{2 R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ The $V_{ref}$ term cancels out, meaning the hysteresis width is **independent of the reference voltage** — a very important design insight. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for All Comparators | Condition | Non-Inverting Comparator | Inverting Comparator | Zero-Crossing Detector | Inverting Schmitt | Non-Inverting Schmitt | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $V_{in} > V_{UTP}$ (or $V_{ref}$) | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ | $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ | $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ | | $V_{LTP} < V_{in} < V_{UTP}$ | (undefined) | (undefined) | (undefined) | Retains previous state | Retains previous state | | $V_{in} < V_{LTP}$ (or $V_{ref}$) | $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ | $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ | $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ | | Hysteresis | None | None | None | Yes (UTP, LTP) | Yes (UTP, LTP) | | Noise Immunity | Poor | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | ## 3.4 Window Comparator Analysis The window comparator uses **two op-amps**: - **Op-amp A1** is a non-inverting comparator comparing $V_{in}$ to a **lower reference $V_{ref1}$**. Output $V_{o1} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > V_{ref1}$. - **Op-amp A2** is an inverting comparator comparing $V_{in}$ to an **upper reference $V_{ref2}$**. Output $V_{o2} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < V_{ref2}$. - The two outputs are combined through a **diode AND gate** (or logic gate) to produce a final output $V_{out}$. The output is HIGH **only** when both conditions are satisfied simultaneously: $V_{ref1} < V_{in} < V_{ref2}$. The **window width** is: $$\boxed{V_W = V_{ref2} - V_{ref1}}$$ Outside the window, the output is LOW. This is the basis of **over-voltage AND under-voltage** combined monitoring in battery management systems. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Comparator Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[OP-AMP COMPARATOR CIRCUITS]:::root --> B[WITHOUT FEEDBACK
Open Loop] A --> C[WITH POSITIVE FEEDBACK
Schmitt Trigger] A --> D[WINDOW COMPARATOR
Two Op-Amps] B --> B1[Non-Inverting Comparator] B --> B2[Inverting Comparator] B --> B3[Zero-Crossing Detector] C --> C1[Inverting Schmitt Trigger] C --> C2[Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger] D --> D1[Upper Limit Detection] D --> D2[Lower Limit Detection] B1 --> E1{Output: plus V_sat
if V_in greater than V_ref} B2 --> E2{Output: plus V_sat
if V_in less than V_ref} C1 --> F1[Hysteresis UTP and LTP] C2 --> F2[Hysteresis with V_ref offset] D1 --> G1[Detects V_in greater than V_ref2] D2 --> G2[Detects V_in less than V_ref1] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Signal Flow ```mermaid graph LR A[Input V_in
Applied to V- terminal]:::input --> B[Summing Node
V- node]:::node B --> C[Open Loop
Comparator Stage]:::comp C --> D[Output V_out
plus or minus V_sat]:::output D --> E[Feedback Resistor R_f]:::fb E --> F[Non Inverting Terminal V+]:::node F --> G[Ground Resistor R1]:::gnd F --> C G --> H[Ground 0V]:::gnd classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 classDef node fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 classDef comp fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000 classDef output fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d classDef fb fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47 classDef gnd fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Window Comparator Dual Op-Amp Logic ```mermaid graph TD A[Input V_in]:::input --> B[Op-Amp A1
Non-Inverting Comparator]:::comp1 A --> C[Op-Amp A2
Inverting Comparator]:::comp2 D[Lower Reference V_ref1]:::ref --> B E[Upper Reference V_ref2]:::ref --> C B --> F[Diode D1 AND Gate]:::logic C --> G[Diode D2 AND Gate]:::logic F --> H[Output V_out]:::output G --> H H --> I{Condition Check}:::decision I -->|V_ref1 less than V_in less than V_ref2| J[HIGH plus V_sat]:::high I -->|Otherwise| K[LOW minus V_sat]:::low classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 classDef comp1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d classDef comp2 fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125 classDef ref fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 classDef logic fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47 classDef output fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d classDef decision fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000 classDef high fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d classDef low fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000 ``` ## 4.4 Hysteresis Transfer Characteristic (Inverting Schmitt Trigger) ```mermaid graph TD A[V_in starts LOW
less than V_UTP]:::low --> B{V_in crosses V_UTP
going upward}:::threshold B -->|Yes| C[Output flips to
minus V_sat]:::neg C --> D{V_in crosses V_LTP
going downward}:::threshold D -->|Yes| E[Output flips to
plus V_sat]:::pos E --> B F[Region between
V_LTP and V_UTP
Hysteresis Band]:::band B -.-> F D -.-> F classDef low fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 classDef threshold fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 classDef neg fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000 classDef pos fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d classDef band fill:#e6b8b7,stroke:#990000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The two threshold boxes (yellow) are *different values* of $V_{in}$ depending on whether the input is rising or falling. The "Hysteresis Band" region (red) shows the input range where the output is *undefined by $V_{in}$ alone* — the output is determined entirely by its **previous state**. This is the "memory" property of the Schmitt trigger. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define a comparator. Explain the operation of a zero-crossing detector using an op-amp with a neat circuit diagram.** **Model Answer:** A **comparator** is a circuit that compares two input voltages and produces a binary output indicating which input is larger. In an op-amp based comparator, the op-amp is operated in **open-loop** (no feedback) configuration. The output saturates to $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ depending on the polarity of $(V_{+} - V_{-})$. A **zero-crossing detector** is a special case of comparator where the reference voltage $V_{ref} = 0$ V. The input signal (typically sinusoidal) is applied to the inverting terminal, and the non-inverting terminal is grounded. - When $V_{in} > 0$ V: $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ - When $V_{in} < 0$ V: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ The output is a **square wave** with the same frequency as the input but with sharp transitions exactly at the zero-crossings of the input. This circuit is widely used in **TRIAC firing circuits** and **phase-locked loops** for frequency synchronization. [Mark distribution: Definition 1M, Operation 1M, Application 1M] --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: What is hysteresis? Why is it necessary in a Schmitt trigger? Mention the role of the feedback resistor $R_f$ and the ground resistor $R_1$.** **Model Answer:** **Hysteresis** is the phenomenon in which the output of a circuit depends not only on the *present* input but also on the *past* input. In a Schmitt trigger, the input threshold for a rising signal is **different** from the threshold for a falling signal, creating two distinct switching points: the **Upper Threshold Point (UTP)** and the **Lower Threshold Point (LTP)**. **Necessity:** A bare comparator suffers from **output chattering** when the input contains noise near the threshold. The output rapidly oscillates between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$, which is unacceptable in digital systems. Hysteresis creates a **dead band** (hysteresis width $V_H$) within which the output ignores small input fluctuations, providing **noise immunity**. **Role of resistors:** - The **feedback resistor $R_f$** provides the **positive feedback** from the output to the non-inverting terminal. It is responsible for creating the two distinct thresholds. - The **ground resistor $R_1$** forms a voltage divider with $R_f$, setting the **feedback fraction $\beta = R_1 / (R_1 + R_f)$**, which directly determines $V_{UTP}$, $V_{LTP}$, and $V_H$. [Mark distribution: Hysteresis definition 1M, Necessity 1M, Role of resistors 1M] --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **inverting Schmitt trigger** using an op-amp. Derive the expressions for Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP) and Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP) in terms of $R_1$, $R_f$, and $V_{sat}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Circuit Description:** In an inverting Schmitt trigger: - The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal ($V_{-}$)**. - A resistor $R_f$ provides **positive feedback** from the output $V_{out}$ to the **non-inverting terminal ($V_{+}$)**. - A resistor $R_1$ connects the non-inverting terminal to **ground**. **Voltage at Non-Inverting Terminal (Voltage Divider Analysis):** Since the op-amp input draws no current, the same current flows through $R_f$ and $R_1$. The voltage at $V_{+}$ is the divider output: $$V_{+} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$ **Threshold Condition:** The output switches state when $V_{-} = V_{+}$, i.e., when $V_{in} = V_{+}$. **Case 1: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ (Calculating UTP)** Substituting $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$: $$V_{UTP} = (+V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = +\frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ This is the value of $V_{in}$ that causes the output to flip *from* $+V_{sat}$ *to* $-V_{sat}$. **Case 2: $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ (Calculating LTP)** Substituting $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$: $$V_{LTP} = (-V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = -\frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ This is the value of $V_{in}$ that causes the output to flip *from* $-V_{sat}$ *to* $+V_{sat}$. [Mark distribution: Circuit 2M, Voltage divider analysis 2M, UTP derivation 1.5M, LTP derivation 1.5M] --- **(b)** For the inverting Schmitt trigger designed above, given $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_f = 100$ k$\Omega$, and $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V, calculate the UTP, LTP, and hysteresis width.
