If a triangular wave of amplitude $\pm 8$ V is applied at the input, sketch the output waveform and explain the operation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_f = 100$ k$\Omega$, $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V. **Step 1: Calculate UTP** $$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} = +\frac{10{,}000 \cdot 13}{10{,}000 + 100{,}000} = +\frac{130{,}000}{110{,}000} = +1.18 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Calculate LTP** $$V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} = -\frac{10{,}000 \cdot 13}{110{,}000} = -1.18 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Calculate Hysteresis Width** $$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 1.18 - (-1.18) = 2.36 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4: Output Waveform Analysis** Since the input triangular wave has amplitude $\pm 8$ V, which is **greater** than both UTP (+1.18 V) and LTP (-1.18 V), the input will cross both thresholds during every cycle. **Operation sequence (assume output starts at $+V_{sat}$):** 1. As $V_{in}$ rises from -8 V, when it crosses $V_{UTP} = +1.18$ V (going upward), the output flips from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ (-13 V). 2. As $V_{in}$ falls from +8 V, when it crosses $V_{LTP} = -1.18$ V (going downward), the output flips from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$ (+13 V). **Sketch of Waveform:** ``` V_out | +13 |- - - - - - - - - - - - - + + + + + - - - - - - - - - | ^ ^ | | | 0 |__________________________|___________|_______________ time | | | -13 |+ + + + + - - - - - - - - + + - - - - - - + | ^ | | | | | V_UTP=+1.18V V_LTP=-1.18V V_in (triangular, ±8V) | +8 |- - - /\ - - /\ - - /\ - - | / \ / \ / \ 0 |----/------\/------\/------\------ time | / \/ \/ \ -8 |-/\- - - - - - - - - - - - - - ``` The output is a **rectangular square wave** that switches precisely at the UTP and LTP crossings. Note that the switching occurs at the *same* voltage level (in absolute value) due to the symmetry of $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ about zero. [Mark distribution: UTP 1M, LTP 1M, V_H 1M, Operation 1.5M, Sketch 1.5M, Explanation 1M] --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Explain the operation of a **non-inverting Schmitt trigger** using an op-amp with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expressions for UTP and LTP. Show that the hysteresis width is independent of the reference voltage. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Circuit Description:** In a non-inverting Schmitt trigger: - The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal ($V_{+}$)** through resistor $R_1$. - A reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal ($V_{-}$)** through a series resistor (often $R_1$ for bias compensation). - A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output $V_{out}$ back to the inverting terminal $V_{-}$. **Voltage at the Inverting Terminal (Superposition Analysis):** Applying superposition with the inverting node summing $V_{ref}$ and $V_{out}$ through their respective resistors: $$V_{-} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} + R_1 \cdot V_{out}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ **Threshold Condition:** The output switches when $V_{+} = V_{-}$. At the switching instant, $V_{+} = V_{in}$: $$V_{in} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} + R_1 \cdot V_{out}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ **Case 1: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ (UTP)** $$V_{UTP} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} + R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R_1 + R_f} + \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ **Case 2: $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ (LTP)** $$V_{LTP} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} - R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} = \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R_1 + R_f} - \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$$ **Hysteresis Width:** $$\begin{aligned} V_H &= V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} \\ &= \left[\frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R_1 + R_f} + \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}\right] - \left[\frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R_1 + R_f} - \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}\right] \\ &= \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R_1 + R_f} - \frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R_1 + R_f} + \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} + \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} \\ &= \frac{2 R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** The hysteresis width $V_H$ contains **no $V_{ref}$ term**. Therefore, the hysteresis width is **completely independent of the reference voltage** and depends only on $R_1$, $R_f$, and $V_{sat}$. Changing $V_{ref}$ simply shifts the *midpoint* of the hysteresis loop, but the *width* of the loop remains constant. [Mark distribution: Circuit 1.5M, $V_{-}$ expression 1.5M, UTP 1M, LTP 1M, Hysteresis width derivation 1.5M, Conclusion 0.5M] --- **(b)** Design a **window comparator** using two op-amps to detect input voltages in the range $V_{ref1} = 2$ V to $V_{ref2} = 5$ V. Draw the circuit and explain the operation. What is the window width? Mention two practical applications of window comparators. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Circuit Description:** A window comparator uses two op-amps: - **Op-amp A1**: Configured as a **non-inverting comparator** with reference $V_{ref1} = 2$ V at the inverting input and input signal $V_{in}$ at the non-inverting input. - **Op-amp A2**: Configured as an **inverting comparator** with reference $V_{ref2} = 5$ V at the non-inverting input and input signal $V_{in}$ at the inverting input. - The two outputs are combined using a **diode AND gate** (D1, D2, and a pull-up resistor $R$) to produce the final output $V_{out}$. **Operation:** - **Op-amp A1 output** $V_{o1}$: $V_{o1} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > 2$ V; $V_{o1} = -V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < 2$ V. - **Op-amp A2 output** $V_{o2}$: $V_{o2} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < 5$ V; $V_{o2} = -V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > 5$ V. The diode AND gate produces $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ **only when both** $V_{o1} = +V_{sat}$ **and** $V_{o2} = +V_{sat}$. This is satisfied when: $$2 \text{ V} < V_{in} < 5 \text{ V}$$ For any $V_{in}$ outside this window, at least one diode is reverse-biased, forcing $V_{out}$ LOW ($-V_{sat}$). **Window Width:** $$\boxed{V_W = V_{ref2} - V_{ref1} = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ V}}$$ **Two Practical Applications:** 1. **Battery Voltage Monitoring System:** Used in laptops and electric vehicles to monitor whether the battery voltage is within a safe operating range. If the voltage falls below the lower threshold (indicating over-discharge) or rises above the upper threshold (indicating over-charge), the window comparator triggers a protection circuit to disconnect the battery from the load. 2. **Industrial Level Sensors:** In a liquid-level measurement system, a window comparator can detect whether a sensor output (e.g., from a pressure transducer) lies within an acceptable "normal" range. Out-of-range signals trigger alarms or automatic shut-off valves, preventing equipment damage. [Mark distribution: Circuit 1.5M, A1 operation 1M, A2 operation 1M, Diode AND logic 1M, Window width 0.5M, Applications 1M, Diagram-quality 1M] --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Valuations:** > > 1. **Sign Error in UTP/LTP Formulae:** Students frequently write $V_{UTP} = +V_{sat}$ instead of $+V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$. The voltage divider is mandatory — the full $V_{sat}$ appears at the output, but only a **fraction** appears at the non-inverting terminal. > > 2. **Confusing Inverting and Non-Inverting Configurations:** The input is at $V_{-}$ in the inverting Schmitt but at $V_{+}$ in the non-inverting Schmitt. Mixing these up reverses the entire threshold logic. > > 3. **Forgetting to Define $V_{sat}$:** Examiners deduct marks if you use $V_{sat}$ in calculations without stating its value (typically $\pm 13$ V for $\mu$A741 with $\pm 15$ V supplies). > > 4. **Drawing Circuit Without Polarity Marks:** The op-amp symbol must clearly show the '+' and '-' terminals on inputs 1 and 2. Failure to mark these is a common reason for losing 1 mark. > > 5. **Skipping Units in Numerical Answers:** $V_{UTP} = +1.18$ (no unit) is treated as incomplete. Always write "V" or "volts". > > 6. **Misnaming the Threshold Points:** UTP is the threshold for the **rising** input (output transitions from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ in inverting configuration), and LTP is for the **falling** input. Reversing this is a classic 1-mark deduction. > > 7. **Forgetting Bias-Current Compensation Resistor:** In practical designs, a resistor of value $R_1 \parallel R_f$ should be placed in series with the inverting input to compensate for input bias currents. Examiners appreciate this mention for full marks. > > 8. **Window Comparator Diode Polarity:** The diodes in the AND gate must be oriented such that they conduct when **both** op-amp outputs are positive. Drawing them in the wrong direction is a common error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.** ### **Core Definitions (Must Memorize)** - A **comparator** operates the op-amp in **open-loop**; output is always saturated. - $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 2V$ for the positive rail and $-V_{EE} + 2V$ for the negative rail. - A **zero-crossing detector** is a comparator with $V_{ref} = 0$ V. - A **Schmitt trigger** is a comparator with **positive feedback** introducing **hysteresis**. - **UTP** = threshold for rising input; **LTP** = threshold for falling input. - **Hysteresis width** $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$ — the noise-immunity band. ### **Key Formulae (Commit to Memory)** - Inverting Schmitt: $V_{UTP} = +V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ - Inverting Schmitt: $V_{LTP} = -V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ - Hysteresis: $V_H = \dfrac{2 R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$ (independent of $V_{ref}$) - Non-Inverting Schmitt: $V_{UTP} = \dfrac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} + R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$ - Non-Inverting Schmitt: $V_{LTP} = \dfrac{R_f \cdot V_{ref} - R_1 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_f}$ - Window width: $V_W = V_{ref2} - V_{ref1}$ ### **Output Polarity Rules (Common Exam Trap)** - Non-Inverting Comparator: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ ✓ - Inverting Comparator: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ ✓ - Zero-Crossing Detector: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > 0$ V ✓ - Window Comparator: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{ref1} < V_{in} < V_{ref2}$ ✓ ### **Component Values (Standard Lab Kit)** - Op-amp IC: **$\mu$A741** (or LM324 for single-supply operation) - Typical $R_1$ range: **1 k$\Omega$ to 10 k$\Omega$** - Typical $R_f$ range: **10 k$\Omega$ to 100 k$\Omega$** - Power supply: **$\pm 15$ V dual supply** for symmetric $\pm V_{sat}$ - Function generator: sine/triangular wave, 1 kHz, amplitude 1–10 V peak ### **Practical Lab Procedure Checklist** 1. Verify the op-amp power supply connections ($V_{CC} = +15$ V on pin 7, $V_{EE} = -15$ V on pin 4 for $\mu$A741). 2. Connect the input and reference as per the circuit diagram. 3. Use a CRO (Cathode Ray Oscilloscope) to observe both input and output simultaneously in **dual-trace mode**. 4. For Schmitt trigger, slowly vary the input amplitude and observe the output transitioning cleanly between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$. 5. Measure $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ using a multimeter by sweeping the DC input and noting the output transition voltages. 6. Compare measured values with theoretical predictions; tolerance within $\pm 10\%$ is acceptable. ### **One-Sentence Final Summary** > A comparator is an op-amp operated open-loop to produce a two-level saturated output indicating which of two inputs is greater, and by adding positive feedback we convert it into a Schmitt trigger with noise-immune hysteresis, the two threshold voltages $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ being set by the resistor ratio $R_1/(R_1 + R_f)$ multiplying the saturation voltage.

topics.title: Window comparator using op-amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Window comparator using op-amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Window Comparator** is a dual-threshold voltage detection circuit constructed using two operational amplifiers configured as voltage comparators. It determines whether an unknown input voltage $V_{in}$ lies **inside** or **outside** a predefined voltage band, called a "window," bounded by an **Upper Threshold Voltage ($V_{UTH}$)** and a **Lower Threshold Voltage ($V_{LTH}$)**. The circuit is the functional equivalent of a *two-sided limit detector* and is a fundamental building block in signal conditioning, battery monitoring, and over/under-voltage protection systems.

In the canonical KTU lab implementation, the IC **LM741** (or equivalent) is deployed in a dual-OPAMP configuration with a passive resistor network generating the two reference thresholds and steering diodes (typically **1N4148**) implementing the output logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A window comparator is essentially *two open-loop comparators with a shared input and a diode-OR/logic-AND output stage*. The output is asserted ($+V_{sat}$) **only when $V_{LTH} \le V_{in} \le V_{UTH}$**.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Toll Booth Gate"

Imagine a **two-tiered toll plaza** on a highway:

- The **first gate** opens only for cars whose height is **above** a certain minimum (this is *Op-Amp A2* checking the **lower limit**).
- The **second gate** opens only for cars whose height is **below** a certain maximum (this is *Op-Amp A1* checking the **upper limit**).
- The toll signal turns **GREEN** only when **both gates are open simultaneously** — i.e., the car is neither too short nor too tall.

If a vehicle is too short (analogous to $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$) or too tall (analogous to $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$), one of the gates stays shut and the signal stays **RED**. The "passage window" is exactly the allowed height range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Anchor:** Just as a window lets in only light of the right size, the window comparator lets through only voltages of the right magnitude. The *width* of the window is $V_W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH}$.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Op-amp supply rails | $\pm V_{CC}$ | $\pm 12$ to $\pm 15$ | V (DC) |
| Op-amp saturation output | $\pm V_{sat}$ | $\pm 13$ to $\pm 14$ | V |
| Forward diode drop (1N4148) | $V_F$ | $\mathbf{0.7}$ | V |
| Reference supply | $V_{REF}$ | $5$ to $15$ | V |
| Reference resistor (typical) | $R_1, R_2, R_3$ | $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $\Omega$ |
| Window width | $V_W$ | Application-defined | V |

> [!NOTE]
> Always note that the **LM741 input common-mode range** excludes the supply rails by $\approx 2\text{ V}$. Hence $V_{REF}$ should be chosen at least **2 V below** $V_{CC}$ to avoid input-stage saturation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Architecture & Operational Logic

The window comparator is built from **three functional blocks**:

1. **Reference Voltage Divider Network** — three resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ in series across $V_{REF}$ generate two precise threshold voltages.
2. **Dual Op-Amp Comparator Bank** — Op-amp A1 (Upper Limit Detector) and Op-Amp A2 (Lower Limit Detector).
3. **Diode Steering / Logic Output Stage** — two signal diodes $D_1, D_2$ and a pull-down resistor $R_L$.

The reference network forms a node at the junction of $R_1$–$R_2$ and a second node at the junction of $R_2$–$R_3$. The full reference chain is $V_{REF} \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow \text{Node-A} \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow \text{Node-B} \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow \text{GND}$.

### 2.1.1 Threshold Voltage Derivation (Voltage Divider Principle)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the series chain, with no current drawn into op-amp inputs (ideal $I_B = 0$):

$$V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \quad \text{(Voltage at Node-A)}$$

$$V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \quad \text{(Voltage at Node-B)}$$

The **Window Width** is therefore:

$$V_W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **wider** the middle resistor $R_2$, the **wider** the detection window for a fixed $V_{REF}$.

### 2.1.2 Comparator Action — Why Two Op-Amps Are Needed

A single op-amp can only detect **one** threshold (over-voltage *or* under-voltage). To detect that a signal is **simultaneously above one limit and below another**, we need:

- **Op-Amp A1 (Upper Detector):** Non-inverting input $(+)$ tied to **$V_{in}$**, inverting input $(-)$ tied to **$V_{UTH}$**.
  - Output $V_{o1} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$ (over-voltage condition).
- **Op-Amp A2 (Lower Detector):** Non-inverting input $(+)$ tied to **$V_{UTH}$ node... wait — tied to $V_{in}$**, inverting input $(-)$ tied to **$V_{LTH}$**.
  - Output $V_{o2} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$ (under-voltage condition).

The diode logic then **inverts** this behaviour: the output is **HIGH only when $V_{in}$ is inside the window** (both comparators output $-V_{sat}$, both diodes reverse-biased OFF, and $V_{out}$ is pulled to $+V_{sat}$ through the pull-up action — or vice-versa depending on diode orientation).

> [!NOTE]
> The two output configurations are **Active-HIGH-Inside** and **Active-LOW-Inide**. The KTU lab typically demonstrates **Active-HIGH-Inside**: $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{LTH} < V_{in} < V_{UTH}$.

---

## 2.2 Truth Table & Logic Behaviour

| Condition on $V_{in}$ | $V_{o1}$ (A1 Out) | $V_{o2}$ (A2 Out) | Diode $D_1$ | Diode $D_2$ | $V_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$ (Below window) | $-V_{sat}$ | $+V_{sat}$ | ON | OFF | $-V_{sat}$ |
| $V_{LTH} < V_{in} < V_{UTH}$ (Inside window) | $-V_{sat}$ | $-V_{sat}$ | OFF | OFF | $+V_{sat}$ |
| $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$ (Above window) | $+V_{sat}$ | $-V_{sat}$ | OFF | ON | $-V_{sat}$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2 + R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$ | Upper threshold voltage | V |
| 2 | $V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$ | Lower threshold voltage | V |
| 3 | $V_W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$ | Window width | V |
| 4 | $V_{REF} = V_{UTH} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2 + R_3}\right)$ | Reference voltage re-arranged | V |
| 5 | $I_{divider} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$ | Current through reference chain | A |
| 6 | $P_{R1} = I_{divider}^{2} \cdot R_1$ | Power dissipated in $R_1$ | W |
| 7 | $V_{out}^{HIGH} \approx +V_{sat} - V_F$ | Output high with diode drop | V |
| 8 | Slew Rate Limit: $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$ | Maximum input sine frequency | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Tip:** In the KTU exam, the most frequently asked relation is $V_W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH}$. Memorize the threshold expressions as **fractions of $V_{REF}$** weighted by the resistor *below* the corresponding node.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Window comparators are deployed in:

- **Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Detect over-charge and over-discharge in Li-ion packs.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Monitor tank levels, pressure transducers, and temperature sensors for *out-of-range* alarms.
- **Automotive Electronics:** Verify that the 12 V supply rail stays within 9 V – 16 V.
- **Analog-to-Digital Signal Slicing:** Recovery of digital pulses buried in noise (Schmitt-trigger variants).
- **Medical Instrumentation:** Pulse-oximetry and ECG signal-level validation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Threshold Voltages

We consider the standard KTU lab window comparator reference network drawn below. A single DC source $V_{REF}$ is connected across a series chain of three resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_3$. The non-inverting terminal of op-amp A2 is tapped at the junction between $R_2$ and $R_3$ (Node-B), and the inverting terminal of op-amp A1 is tapped at the junction between $R_1$ and $R_2$ (Node-A).

### Step 1 — Total Resistance in the Divider Chain

Since all three resistors are in series, the total equivalent resistance is:

$$R_{T} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$$

### Step 2 — Divider Current (No Load Condition)

Because the inputs of an ideal op-amp draw **zero current**, no current branches away from the chain. The current through the chain is purely ohmic:

$$I_{T} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R_{T}} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

**Logic:** This is simply Ohm's Law applied to the entire series chain. Because the op-amp inputs are voltage-sensing devices with theoretically infinite input impedance, the divider is unloaded — preserving linearity.

### Step 3 — Voltage at Node-A (the Upper Threshold)

The voltage at the junction between $R_1$ and $R_2$ is the voltage developed across the series combination of $R_2$ and $R_3$ (because the chain runs from $V_{REF}$ down to GND, and Node-A sits "above" $R_2 + R_3$):

$$V_{A} = I_{T} \cdot (R_2 + R_3)$$

Substituting $I_T$:

$$V_{A} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \cdot (R_2 + R_3)$$

$$\boxed{V_{UTH} = V_{A} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}}$$

**Logic:** The factor $\frac{R_2 + R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$ is the standard voltage-divider ratio giving the *fraction* of $V_{REF}$ appearing at Node-A.

### Step 4 — Voltage at Node-B (the Lower Threshold)

The voltage at the junction between $R_2$ and $R_3$ is the voltage developed across $R_3$ alone:

$$V_{B} = I_{T} \cdot R_3$$

Substituting $I_T$:

$$V_{B} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \cdot R_3$$

$$\boxed{V_{LTH} = V_{B} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}}$$

**Logic:** $R_3$ is the *lowest* resistor in the chain, so the voltage at its top terminal (Node-B) is naturally the smallest of the two thresholds. Hence $V_{LTH} < V_{UTH}$, as required.

### Step 5 — Window Width Computation

Subtracting the two threshold expressions:

$$V_W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH}$$

$$V_W = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} - V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

Factoring the common denominator:

$$V_W = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{(R_2 + R_3) - R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

$$V_W = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

$$\boxed{V_W = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}}$$

**Logic:** The middle resistor $R_2$ *alone* controls the window width. Choosing $R_2$ large makes the window wide; choosing $R_2$ small makes the window narrow.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** Design a window comparator using op-amp LM741 for $V_{UTH} = +4\,\text{V}$ and $V_{LTH} = +2\,\text{V}$ with $V_{REF} = +12\,\text{V}$. Choose standard $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistors.

### Step 1 — Identify Window Width

$$V_W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH} = 4 - 2 = 2\,\text{V}$$

### Step 2 — Form Ratios from $V_{REF}$

$$\frac{V_{UTH}}{V_{REF}} = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_2 + R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\frac{V_{LTH}}{V_{REF}} = \frac{2}{12} = \frac{1}{6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} = \frac{1}{6}$$

### Step 3 — Assume $R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 6\,\text{k}\Omega$ (Total Standard Value)

Then $R_3 = \frac{1}{6} \cdot 6 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 + R_3 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 6 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, so $R_2 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$. Finally, $R_1 = 6 - 1 - 1 = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$.

### Step 4 — Final Selected Values

| Resistor | Value | E12 Standard |
|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ | $4\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $3.9\,\text{k}\Omega$ (closest) |
| $R_2$ | $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $1.0\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| $R_3$ | $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $1.0\,\text{k}\Omega$ |

### Step 5 — Verification

$$V_{UTH} = 12 \cdot \frac{1 + 1}{4 + 1 + 1} = 12 \cdot \frac{2}{6} = 4\,\text{V} \;\checkmark$$

$$V_{LTH} = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{4 + 1 + 1} = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{6} = 2\,\text{V} \;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** In the KTU lab, use **E12 series** standard values (1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.7, 5.6, 6.8, 8.2 k$\Omega$). Slight deviations are acceptable as long as $V_{UTH}$ and $V_{LTH}$ remain within $\pm 5\%$ of the design target.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Constraints Summary

| Constraint | Equation | Typical Limit |
|---|---|---|
| $R_3 \le R_1$ for $V_{LTH} < V_{UTH}$ | $R_3 < R_2 + R_3$ | Always satisfied |
| Divider current bounded | $I_T \le 5\,\text{mA}$ | $R_T \ge V_{REF}/5\,\text{mA}$ |
| Op-amp output current limit | $I_{out} \le 20\,\text{mA}$ | $R_L \ge V_{sat}/20\,\text{mA}$ |
| Pull-up resistor (active-low version) | $R_{PU} \approx 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Standard |
| Pull-down resistor (active-high version) | $R_{PD} \approx 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Standard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Window Comparator

The diagram below is a **functional block architecture** mapping the signal flow, threshold generation, and decision logic. This is a Mermaid-safe representation (all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords are used; all labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VrefNode["V REF DC SOURCE"]
    Rone["RESISTOR R ONE"]
    Rtwo["RESISTOR R TWO"]
    Rthree["RESISTOR R THREE"]
    GndNode["GROUND"]
    ViNode["V IN INPUT SIGNAL"]
    VoNode["V OUT FINAL OUTPUT"]
    OpAone["OP AMP A ONE UPPER DETECTOR"]
    OpAtwo["OP AMP A TWO LOWER DETECTOR"]
    DiodeOne["DIODE D ONE"]
    DiodeTwo["DIODE D TWO"]
    PullR["PULL RESISTOR R L"]
    NodeA["NODE A V UTH"]
    NodeB["NODE B V LTH"]

    VrefNode --> Rone
    Rone --> NodeA
    NodeA --> Rtwo
    Rtwo --> NodeB
    NodeB --> Rthree
    Rthree --> GndNode

    ViNode --> OpAone
    NodeA --> OpAone
    OpAone --> DiodeOne
    DiodeOne --> VoNode

    ViNode --> OpAtwo
    NodeB --> OpAtwo
    OpAtwo --> DiodeTwo
    DiodeTwo --> VoNode

    VoNode --> PullR
    PullR --> GndNode
```

---

## 4.2 Decision-Flow Diagram of the Comparator

The diagram below captures the **logical decision flow** that the op-amps and diodes execute for every value of $V_{in}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartNode["START COMPARE V IN"]
    Qone["IS V IN GREATER THAN V UTH"]
    Qtwo["IS V IN LESS THAN V LTH"]
    CaseA["A ONE OUTPUT PLUS V SAT D ONE CONDUCTS V OUT MINUS V SAT"]
    CaseB["A ONE AND A TWO OUTPUT MINUS V SAT BOTH DIODES OFF V OUT PLUS V SAT INSIDE WINDOW"]
    CaseC["A TWO OUTPUT PLUS V SAT D TWO CONDUCTS V OUT MINUS V SAT"]
    StopNode["END WINDOW DECISION COMPLETE"]

    StartNode --> Qone
    Qone -- YES --> CaseA
    Qone -- NO --> Qtwo
    Qtwo -- YES --> CaseC
    Qtwo -- NO --> CaseB
    CaseA --> StopNode
    CaseB --> StopNode
    CaseC --> StopNode
```

---

## 4.3 Annotated Pin Connections (LM741 Dual-Supply Pinout)

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Connection in Window Comparator |
|---|---|---|
| 1, 5 | Offset Null | Left open (no offset trim used) |
| 2 | Inverting Input ($-$) of A1 | Connected to **Node-A ($V_{UTH}$)** |
| 3 | Non-Inverting Input ($+$) of A1 | Connected to **$V_{in}$** |
| 4 | $V_{CC-}$ | Connected to **$-12$ V** |
| 6 | Output of A1 | Connected to **Anode of $D_1$** |
| 7 | $V_{CC+}$ | Connected to **$+12$ V** |
| 2 (A2) | Inverting Input ($-$) of A2 | Connected to **$V_{in}$** |
| 3 (A2) | Non-Inverting Input ($+$) of A2 | Connected to **Node-B ($V_{LTH}$)** |
| 6 (A2) | Output of A2 | Connected to **Anode of $D_2$** |
| Cathode of $D_1$ & $D_2$ | Common Node | Connected to **$V_{out}$** & $R_L$ to GND |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** Always connect pin-8 (NC) to nothing. A bypass capacitor of **0.1 $\mu$F** between pins 7 and 4 is recommended to suppress supply-rail noise. A second LM741 is needed for A2 since each IC has only one op-amp.

---

## 4.4 Component List for KTU Lab Implementation

| Component | Specification | Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| IC LM741 | Op-amp, 8-pin DIP | 2 |
| Resistor $R_1$ | 1/4 W, 5% (value as per design) | 1 |
| Resistor $R_2$ | 1/4 W, 5% (value as per design) | 1 |
| Resistor $R_3$ | 1/4 W, 5% (value as per design) | 1 |
| Resistor $R_L$ | 10 k$\Omega$, 1/4 W | 1 |
| Diode 1N4148 | Signal diode | 2 |
| DC Source $V_{REF}$ | 0 – 15 V variable | 1 |
| DC Source $V_{in}$ | 0 – 15 V variable (or function gen) | 1 |
| Breadboard, connecting wires, CRO | Standard lab kit | 1 set |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define a window comparator. Mention any two applications.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A window comparator is a circuit using two op-amp comparators that detects whether an input voltage $V_{in}$ lies within a defined voltage band bounded by an upper threshold $V_{UTH}$ and a lower threshold $V_{LTH}$.
> **Applications:** (i) Battery over-charge/over-discharge protection in BMS, (ii) Industrial process control for monitoring tank-level or pressure out-of-range alarms. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark first app + 1 mark second app = 3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** What is the role of the diode network in a window comparator circuit?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ implement the **output logic combination** of the two op-amp outputs. They allow only the *more positive* output to appear at $V_{out}$ (diode-OR logic), and crucially, they permit a clean transition between the inside-window and outside-window states. When *both* op-amps output $-V_{sat}$ (i.e., $V_{in}$ is inside the window), neither diode conducts, and $V_{out}$ is pulled up to $+V_{sat}$ through $R_L$ or a separate pull-up — producing the active-high-inside behaviour. *(1 mark identification + 1 mark logic role + 1 mark active-high-inside behaviour = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a window comparator using op-amp. Derive the expressions for upper and lower threshold voltages. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design a window comparator to detect voltages in the range **+3 V to +7 V** using a reference supply $V_{REF} = +12$ V. Choose appropriate resistor values. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Working & Threshold Derivation [7 marks]

**Circuit Description [2 marks]:**
A window comparator uses two op-amps (A1, A2), a three-resistor reference divider ($R_1, R_2, R_3$), and two signal diodes ($D_1, D_2$). $V_{in}$ is applied to:
- The **non-inverting** input of A1 (upper-limit detector) and to the **inverting** input of A2 (lower-limit detector).
- The **inverting** input of A1 is tied to Node-A ($V_{UTH}$), and the **non-inverting** input of A2 is tied to Node-B ($V_{LTH}$).

**Working [3 marks]:**

- When $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$: A2's $(+)$ input is below its $(-)$ input $\rightarrow V_{o2} = -V_{sat}$. A1's $(+)$ input is also below its $(-)$ input $\rightarrow V_{o1} = -V_{sat}$. Both diodes are reverse-biased, $V_{out}$ is pulled LOW (outside window, low output for active-high-inside configuration; inverted for active-low-inside).
- When $V_{LTH} \le V_{in} \le V_{UTH}$: A2's $(+) > (-)$ $\rightarrow V_{o2} = +V_{sat}$; A1's $(+) < (-)$ $\rightarrow V_{o1} = -V_{sat}$. The diode from A2 conducts, raising $V_{out}$ to $+V_{sat}$ minus $V_F$. **Output is HIGH** — signal is *inside* the window.
- When $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$: Both op-amps reverse state, $V_{o1} = +V_{sat}$ but $V_{o2} = -V_{sat}$ — output is again LOW.

**Threshold Derivation [2 marks]:** (as in Section 3.1)

$$V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

#### Part (b) — Design [7 marks]

**Given:** $V_{UTH} = 7$ V, $V_{LTH} = 3$ V, $V_{REF} = 12$ V.

**Step 1 — Compute Window Width:**
$$V_W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH} = 7 - 3 = 4\,\text{V} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 2 — Form Ratios:**
$$\frac{V_{UTH}}{V_{REF}} = \frac{7}{12} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{V_{LTH}}{V_{REF}} = \frac{3}{12} = \frac{1}{4} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 3 — Choose $R_T$ and Solve:** Let $R_T = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 12$ k$\Omega$ (clean integer math).

$$R_3 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 12 = 3\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$R_2 + R_3 = \frac{7}{12} \cdot 12 = 7\,\text{k}\Omega \;\Rightarrow\; R_2 = 4\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$R_1 = 12 - 7 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**
$$V_{UTH} = 12 \cdot \frac{4 + 3}{5 + 4 + 3} = 12 \cdot \frac{7}{12} = 7\,\text{V} \;\checkmark$$
$$V_{LTH} = 12 \cdot \frac{3}{12} = 3\,\text{V} \;\checkmark \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 5 — Final Values:**

| Resistor | Designed Value | E12 Standard |
|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ | $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| $R_2$ | $4\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $3.9\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| $R_3$ | $3\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $3.3\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| $R_L$ | $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ | $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ |

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*
>
> **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a window comparator using op-amp LM741. Explain the role of the diode network in generating the output. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a window comparator, $V_{REF} = 10$ V, $R_1 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 3\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $R_3 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate $V_{UTH}$, $V_{LTH}$, and the window width. State the conditions under which the output is HIGH. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Circuit & Diode Role [7 marks]

**Circuit Diagram [3 marks]:** Draw LM741 A1 and A2 with the reference divider, input, and diode output stage as shown in Section 4.1.

**Role of Diode Network [4 marks]:** The two signal diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ form a **diode-OR gate** at the output. Their cathodes are tied together at the output node, and their anodes connect to the outputs of A1 and A2. Only the op-amp that outputs the higher voltage forward-biases its diode, allowing that signal to reach $V_{out}$. The diode network thus:

1. Combines the two comparator outputs into a single logic output.
2. Prevents one op-amp from feeding current into the other.
3. Allows the inside-window state to be uniquely identified (when *both* diodes are OFF, $V_{out}$ is pulled to the desired level by $R_L$).
4. Introduces a forward voltage drop $V_F \approx 0.7$ V, which must be considered in high-precision applications.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{UTH}$ [2 marks]:**
$$V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2 + R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} = 10 \cdot \frac{3 + 5}{2 + 3 + 5} = 10 \cdot \frac{8}{10} = 8\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{LTH}$ [2 marks]:**
$$V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} = 10 \cdot \frac{5}{10} = 5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Window Width [1 mark]:**
$$V_W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH} = 8 - 5 = 3\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Output HIGH Condition [2 marks]:** The output is HIGH ($+V_{sat}$) when the input voltage lies **within the window**:
$$V_{LTH} \le V_{in} \le V_{UTH} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5\,\text{V} \le V_{in} \le 8\,\text{V}$$

Outside this range, the output is LOW ($-V_{sat}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention $V_{in}$ ranges (not just $> V_{LTH}$ or $< V_{UTH}$):** Many students write "$V_{out}$ is HIGH when $V_{in} > V_{LTH}$" — this is **WRONG**. The HIGH condition is the **simultaneous satisfaction of both bounds**, i.e., the inside-window condition. Deduct **1 mark** for this ambiguity.
> 2. **Mixing up the two threshold formulae:** $V_{UTH}$ uses the resistor *below* Node-A ($R_2 + R_3$), while $V_{LTH}$ uses the resistor *below* Node-B ($R_3$). Reversing them is a classic error. Deduct **2 marks** if reversed.
> 3. **Ignoring the diode forward drop:** When asked to compute $V_{out}^{HIGH}$, students often write $+V_{sat}$ without subtracting $V_F \approx 0.7$ V. Deduct **1 mark** for this omission in high-precision questions.
> 4. **No current drawn from divider:** Students sometimes try to include op-amp input bias current in the divider analysis. For LM741 ($\pm 50$ nA typical), this is negligible — state "ideal op-amp inputs draw zero current" explicitly. Deduct **0.5 mark** otherwise.
> 5. **Pin-numbering errors in the circuit diagram:** Pin 3 is non-inverting, pin 2 is inverting — swap them and lose **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Window Comparator**

- **Definition:** A two-threshold detector that asserts output when $V_{in}$ lies between $V_{LTH}$ and $V_{UTH}$.
- **Two op-amps required** because two independent reference comparisons are needed.
- **Reference divider:** $R_1$–$R_2$–$R_3$ series chain; **$R_2$ alone controls window width**.
- **Threshold formulae:**
  - $V_{UTH} = V_{REF} \cdot (R_2 + R_3) / (R_1 + R_2 + R_3)$
  - $V_{LTH} = V_{REF} \cdot R_3 / (R_1 + R_2 + R_3)$
- **Window width:** $V_W = V_{REF} \cdot R_2 / R_1 + R_2 + R_3$
- **Diode logic:** Diode-OR with pull-down $R_L$ for **active-high-inside**; diode-AND with pull-up for **active-low-inside**.
- **Output is HIGH when $V_{LTH} \le V_{in} \le V_{UTH}$** — not just above $V_{LTH}$.
- **Diode drop $V_F \approx 0.7$ V** must be subtracted from $+V_{sat}$ in $V_{out}^{HIGH}$ for precision work.
- **Op-amp:** LM741, used **open-loop** (no feedback) — saturation is the desired operating region.
- **Common-mode limit:** $V_{in}$ must lie between $(V_{CC-} + 2\,\text{V})$ and $(V_{CC+} - 2\,\text{V})$.
- **Slew rate limit:** LM741 SR = 0.5 V/$\mu$s limits the maximum input frequency to $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$.
- **Design rule of thumb:** Choose $R_T = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$ such that $I_T \le 5$ mA to keep power dissipation low.
- **Application domains:** BMS, automotive rail monitoring, process control alarms, signal slicing.
- **Lab caution:** Always use bypass capacitors (0.1 $\mu$F) across op-amp supply pins to suppress oscillation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital to Analog Converter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Digital to Analog Converter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an essential mixed-signal integrated circuit (IC) device that converts a finite-precision digital input code (typically a binary word consisting of N bits) into a discrete analog output signal, which is represented as a continuous voltage or current level proportional to the input digital value. In the context of linear integrated circuit laboratory work, the DAC serves as the fundamental bridge between the digital processing domain and the continuous-time analog world, enabling reconstructed analog signals for audio playback, instrumentation control, function generation, and precision actuator driving.

The transfer function of an ideal N-bit DAC is mathematically expressed as:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} b_i \cdot 2^i / 2^N = V_{ref} \times \frac{D}{2^N}$$

where $D$ is the decimal equivalent of the binary input word and $V_{ref}$ is the stable precision reference voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The laboratory experiment is centered on the design, construction, and performance verification of the **Binary Weighted Resistor DAC** and the **R-2R Ladder DAC** using op-amp summing configurations. Students are expected to practically measure Full-Scale Output Voltage ($V_{FS}$), resolution (LSB size), linearity, and percentage error for both architectures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water tap connected to a precise digital weighing scale**. You have a set of fixed-size water containers labeled with binary weights: one container of size **1 unit**, one of size **2 units**, one of **4 units**, and one of **8 units** (this is essentially a 4-bit system). When you send a digital code like `1010` (which equals 10 in decimal), the system "turns on" exactly the 8-unit container and the 2-unit container, while the 4-unit and 1-unit containers remain closed. The total water that flows out is exactly **10 units**, which can then be measured by the analog scale.

The DAC works exactly like this — except instead of water, it is **electric current (or voltage)** flowing through precisely selected resistors. The binary bits "switch on" or "switch off" specific current paths, and the sum of all these currents is converted into a single analog output voltage by an operational amplifier configured as a summing amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Takeaway:** A DAC essentially performs a mathematical **weighted summation** of the input bits, where the weight of each bit is determined by its positional value in the binary code ($2^0, 2^1, 2^2, \ldots, 2^{N-1}$). The analog output is this weighted sum scaled by a reference voltage.

## 1.3 Critical Performance Metrics and Standard Definitions

The following physical constants and standard metrics govern DAC operation and are mandatory for KTU lab viva voce:

- **Resolution (Step Size or LSB):** The smallest change in the analog output corresponding to a 1-LSB change in the digital input. For an N-bit DAC, Resolution = $V_{FS} / (2^N - 1)$ or $V_{ref} / 2^N$.
- **Full-Scale Output Voltage ($V_{FS}$):** The maximum output voltage obtained when all input bits are HIGH (i.e., input code = $111\ldots1$).
- **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The maximum excursion of the output, generally equal to $V_{ref}$.
- **Percentage Resolution:** $\frac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100\%$
- **Offset Error:** The deviation of the actual output from the ideal output when the digital input is all zeros.
- **Gain Error:** The deviation of the slope of the actual transfer function from the ideal slope.
- **Linearity Error (INL & DNL):** Integral Non-Linearity (INL) and Differential Non-Linearity (DNL) measure deviation from the ideal straight-line transfer characteristic.
- **Monotonicity:** A DAC is monotonic if its output never decreases when the input code increases monotonically.
- **Settling Time:** Time required for the output to settle within a specified error band (typically $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB) after a digital input change.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture — Why and How

A DAC fundamentally executes a **weighted summation** of binary bits. To understand *why* this works, consider that any binary number $D$ of N bits can be expressed in positional notation:

$$D = b_{N-1} \cdot 2^{N-1} + b_{N-2} \cdot 2^{N-2} + \ldots + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_0 \cdot 2^0$$

If we could make each bit $b_i$ produce an analog contribution proportional to $2^i$, then summing all contributions would yield an analog value directly proportional to the digital input $D$. The two most common architectures to achieve this are:

### 2.1.1 Binary Weighted Resistor DAC
- **How it works:** Each bit is connected to the inverting input of an op-amp through a resistor whose value is inversely proportional to the bit weight. The MSB gets the smallest resistor ($R$), the next bit gets $2R$, then $4R$, $8R$, and so on. When a bit is HIGH, current flows through its resistor into the virtual ground of the op-amp summing junction.
- **Why this works:** The op-amp's virtual ground maintains the summing node at 0V, ensuring each bit's current is independent of the state of other bits (KCL holds at the summing junction).
- **Drawback:** The resistor spread is $2^{N-1}$:1, which for a 12-bit DAC is 2048:1, making it extremely difficult to fabricate with precision on a single IC chip.

### 2.1.2 R-2R Ladder DAC
- **How it works:** Uses only two resistor values ($R$ and $2R$) arranged in a repeating ladder network. Each bit is switched between $V_{ref}$ and ground. The Thevenin equivalent at each node creates the binary-weighted division automatically.
- **Why this works:** The ladder network's recursive structure inherently produces a binary voltage divider. At each rung, the equivalent resistance looking into the ladder is $2R$, ensuring the voltage at each node is exactly halved as we move from the MSB to the LSB.
- **Advantage:** Easy to manufacture in IC form, excellent matching, and easily scalable to high resolutions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 lab examination. All notations follow IEEE and KTU standard convention.

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Voltage (Binary Weighted) | $V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R} V_{ref} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$ | Volts (V) | Negative sign indicates inverting configuration |
| Output Voltage (R-2R Ladder) | $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{N-i}}$ | Volts (V) | LSB contributes $-V_{ref}/2^N$ |
| Full-Scale Output ($V_{FS}$) | $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2^N - 1}{2^N}$ | Volts (V) | When all bits are HIGH |
| Resolution / LSB Size | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ | Volts (V) | Smallest analog step change |
| Percentage Resolution | $\% \text{ Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100$ | Percent | For N-bit DAC |
| Offset Binary Transfer | $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \left[ b_3 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right]$ | Volts (V) | For 4-bit DAC ($b_3$ = MSB) |
| Sum of Currents (at summing node) | $I_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} I_i$ | Amperes (A) | KCL at virtual ground |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examiner's Note:** The KTU 2024 lab manual and model answer key for PCEBL407 consistently uses $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ or $V_{ref} = 10\text{ V}$ in the design calculations. Students must explicitly **mention the value of $V_{ref}$** in their calculations. Skipping this step is a common cause of full-mark deduction.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

DACs are foundational components across numerous engineering disciplines:

- **Audio Engineering:** Every smartphone, music player, and home theater uses high-resolution DACs (often 24-bit / 192 kHz) to reconstruct analog audio from compressed digital audio streams (MP3, FLAC, AAC).
- **Industrial Process Control:** Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) use DACs to drive analog actuators such as motor speed controllers, valve positioners, and proportional temperature controllers.
- **Automatic Test Equipment (ATE):** Function generators and arbitrary waveform generators rely on high-speed DACs to synthesize complex analog waveforms.
- **Telecommunications:** Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS) chips use DACs at their output stage to generate precisely tunable sinusoidal carriers for modulators and signal generators.
- **Embedded Systems & IoT:** Microcontroller boards (e.g., Arduino, STM32) use internal or external DACs to drive analog sensors, control lighting, and interface with legacy analog systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Binary Weighted Resistor DAC Output Equation

### Step 1 — Circuit Topology Definition

Consider a 4-bit binary weighted resistor DAC built around an ideal inverting summing amplifier. The four input resistors connected to the inverting node are $R$, $2R$, $4R$, and $8R$, corresponding to bits $b_0$ (LSB), $b_1$, $b_2$, and $b_3$ (MSB), respectively. The feedback resistor is $R_f$. The non-inverting input is grounded.

### Step 2 — Apply the Virtual Short Concept

Because the op-amp is ideal and configured with negative feedback, the inverting input ($V^-$) is held at the same potential as the non-inverting input ($V^+$). Since $V^+ = 0\text{ V}$ (grounded), the inverting input is at a **virtual ground**, meaning $V^- = 0\text{ V}$.

This virtual ground is critical because it ensures that the voltage across each input resistor is entirely determined by its corresponding input bit voltage, independent of the state of all other bits.

### Step 3 — Write Individual Branch Currents

The current flowing through each input resistor toward the virtual ground is given by Ohm's Law:

$$I_0 = \frac{b_0 \cdot V_{ref} - 0}{2^{0} R} = \frac{b_0 \cdot V_{ref}}{R}$$

$$I_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot V_{ref} - 0}{2^{1} R} = \frac{b_1 \cdot V_{ref}}{2R}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{b_2 \cdot V_{ref} - 0}{2^{2} R} = \frac{b_2 \cdot V_{ref}}{4R}$$

$$I_3 = \frac{b_3 \cdot V_{ref} - 0}{2^{3} R} = \frac{b_3 \cdot V_{ref}}{8R}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the Summing Node

Since the ideal op-amp draws no input current, all currents flowing into the summing node from the input branches must flow through the feedback resistor $R_f$:

$$I_f = I_0 + I_1 + I_2 + I_3$$

### Step 5 — Express Output Voltage in Terms of Currents

The output voltage $V_{out}$ is the voltage at the inverting input (0 V) plus the voltage drop across the feedback resistor. Since the current flows *from* the virtual ground *into* the output through $R_f$, the output is at a negative potential:

$$V_{out} = 0 - I_f \cdot R_f = -I_f \cdot R_f$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the Current Sum

$$V_{out} = -R_f \left[ \frac{b_0 \cdot V_{ref}}{R} + \frac{b_1 \cdot V_{ref}}{2R} + \frac{b_2 \cdot V_{ref}}{4R} + \frac{b_3 \cdot V_{ref}}{8R} \right]$$

### Step 7 — Factor Out Common Terms

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f \cdot V_{ref}}{R} \left[ b_0 + \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} \right]$$

### Step 8 — Choose Standard Component Values for Lab Use

In the KTU 2024 lab, the standard practice is to select $R_f = R$, which simplifies the equation. Substituting $R_f = R$:

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \left[ b_0 + \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} \right]$$

This is the **canonical 4-bit Binary Weighted DAC transfer equation** used throughout the KTU lab manual.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Lab Standard Case

**Given:** A 4-bit binary weighted resistor DAC with $V_{ref} = 8\text{ V}$ and $R_f = R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$. Compute the output voltage for input code $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1100_2$ (i.e., $b_3 = 1, b_2 = 1, b_1 = 0, b_0 = 0$).

### Solution:

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \left[ b_0 + \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} \right]$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the bit values:

$$V_{out} = -8 \left[ 0 + \frac{0}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} \right]$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the fractions inside the bracket:

$$\frac{1}{4} = 0.25, \quad \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

**Step 3:** Sum the bracket:

$$0 + 0 + 0.25 + 0.125 = 0.375$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $-V_{ref}$:

$$V_{out} = -8 \times 0.375 = -3.0 \text{ V}$$

**Verification using the decimal equivalent:** $1100_2 = 12_{10}$. The analog equivalent is $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \times \frac{D}{2^N} = -8 \times \frac{12}{16} = -6\text{ V}$? 

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction Notice:** The decimal form $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^N$ assumes bits are weighted as MSB = $2^{N-1}$. The expanded form explicitly uses $b_0 = 2^0$ weight. **Always double-check which convention your KTU lab manual uses.** When bits are labeled as $b_0$ (LSB), the formula is the explicit weighted sum form. When labeled as $b_3$ (MSB) with $b_3 \cdot 2^3$ weight, the formula becomes $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{b_3 \cdot 8 + b_2 \cdot 4 + b_1 \cdot 2 + b_0 \cdot 1}{16}\right) = -8 \times \frac{12}{16} = -6\text{ V}$.

The correct answer for input $1100_2$ is therefore $\boxed{V_{out} = -6.0 \text{ V}}$.

## 3.3 R-2R Ladder DAC — Analytical Matrix

For a 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC with $V_{ref} = 8\text{ V}$:

| Digital Input $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ | Decimal $D$ | $V_{out}$ (Volts) |
|---|---|---|
| 0000 | 0 | $0.000$ |
| 0001 | 1 | $-0.500$ |
| 0010 | 2 | $-1.000$ |
| 0011 | 3 | $-1.500$ |
| 0100 | 4 | $-2.000$ |
| 0101 | 5 | $-2.500$ |
| 0110 | 6 | $-3.000$ |
| 0111 | 7 | $-3.500$ |
| 1000 | 8 | $-4.000$ |
| 1001 | 9 | $-4.500$ |
| 1010 | 10 | $-5.000$ |
| 1011 | 11 | $-5.500$ |
| 1100 | 12 | $-6.000$ |
| 1101 | 13 | $-6.500$ |
| 1110 | 14 | $-7.000$ |
| 1111 | 15 | $-7.500$ |

**Resolution (LSB):** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^N = 8 / 16 = 0.5\text{ V}$.
**Full-Scale Output:** $V_{FS} = 7.5\text{ V}$ (when input = 1111).
**Percentage Resolution:** $\frac{1}{15} \times 100\% = 6.67\%$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Binary Weighted Resistor DAC — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vref[Vref DC Supply] --> SwitchMSB
    b3[Bit b3 MSB] --> SwitchMSB
    SwitchMSB[Solid State Switch] --> ResMSB[Resistor R]
    b2[Bit b2] --> Switch2
    Switch2[Solid State Switch] --> Res2[Resistor 2R]
    b1[Bit b1] --> Switch1
    Switch1[Solid State Switch] --> Res1[Resistor 4R]
    b0[Bit b0 LSB] --> Switch0
    Switch0[Solid State Switch] --> Res0[Resistor 8R]
    ResMSB --> SummingNode
    Res2 --> SummingNode
    Res1 --> SummingNode
    Res0 --> SummingNode
    SummingNode[Summing Junction Virtual Ground] --> OpAmpIC
    Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> OpAmpIC
    Ground[Analog Ground] --> OpAmpIC
    OpAmpIC[Op Amp IC 741 Summing Amplifier] --> OutputNode
    OutputNode[Analog Output Vout] --> Meter[DMM or CRO]
```

## 4.2 R-2R Ladder DAC — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputStage[Digital Input Word b3 b2 b1 b0] --> LadderInput
    VrefTwo[Vref Source] --> LadderInput
    LadderInput[Ladder Network R and 2R Resistors Only] --> NodeB3
    NodeB3[Node 3 Voltage b3 times Vref divided by 2] --> NodeB2
    NodeB2[Node 2 Voltage b2 times Vref divided by 4] --> NodeB1
    NodeB1[Node 1 Voltage b1 times Vref divided by 8] --> NodeB0
    NodeB0[Node 0 Voltage b0 times Vref divided by 16] --> SummingOpAmp
    RfTwo[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> SummingOpAmp
    SummingOpAmp[Op Amp IC 741 Current to Voltage Converter] --> OutputStage
    OutputStage[Analog Output Voltage Vout] --> DmmReadout[DMM or CRO Display]
```

## 4.3 DAC Operational State Diagram (Bit Cycling)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Input0000
    Input0000 --> Input0001: Increment Counter
    Input0001 --> Input0010: Increment Counter
    Input0010 --> Input0011: Increment Counter
    Input0011 --> Input0100: Increment Counter
    Input0100 --> Input0101: Increment Counter
    Input0101 --> Input0110: Increment Counter
    Input0110 --> Input0111: Increment Counter
    Input0111 --> Input1000: Increment Counter
    Input1000 --> Input1001: Increment Counter
    Input1001 --> Input1010: Increment Counter
    Input1010 --> Input1011: Increment Counter
    Input1011 --> Input1100: Increment Counter
    Input1100 --> Input1101: Increment Counter
    Input1101 --> Input1110: Increment Counter
    Input1110 --> Input1111: Increment Counter
    Input1111 --> [*]: Full Scale Reached
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSU1[DC Regulated Power Supply plus minus 15V] --> Board
    PSU2[Reference Voltage Source Vref] --> Board
    LogicSwitches[Logic Switches 4 Bits Logic 0 and Logic 5V] --> Board
    Board[Digital to Analog Converter Test Board with IC 741 and Resistor Network] --> Dmm
    Dmm[Digital Multimeter to Measure Vout] --> Result
    CRO[Cathode Ray Oscilloscope for Waveform Observation] --> Result

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Reading Guide:** In all the Mermaid architecture diagrams, the **"summing junction virtual ground"** node is the central point where all input currents converge. The op-amp's role is to maintain this node at 0V (virtual ground) and to convert the total incoming current into a proportional output voltage.
