**Circuit Diagram:** Op-amp with $R_{in}$ from $V_{in}$ to inverting input (pin 2), $R_f$ from inverting input to the cathode of $D_1$, anode of $D_1$ to op-amp output. $D_2$ from inverting input to op-amp output. Non-inverting input (pin 3) grounded.

**Operation:**

- **Positive Half-Cycle ($V_{in} > 0$):** Op-amp output swings positive, $D_1$ conducts, $V_{out} = -V_{in} \cdot (R_f/R_{in})$.
- **Negative Half-Cycle ($V_{in} < 0$):** Op-amp output swings negative, $D_1$ OFF, $D_2$ ON, $V_{out} = 0$.

**Derivation of Effective Threshold:**

Using the closed-loop relation derived in Section 3.1:

$$
V_{in, threshold} = \dfrac{V_F}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}
$$

For $\mu$A741 with $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ and $\beta = 0.5$:

$$
V_{in, threshold} = \dfrac{0.7}{100001} \approx 7 \text{ µV}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Design Steps:**

1. **Choose $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$** for the super-diode input.
2. **Choose $R_f = 2R_1 = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$** to ensure gain of 2 on the negative half-cycle (so that after inversion the magnitude matches).
3. **Choose summing amplifier resistors** $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.
4. **Op-amp selection:** $\mu$A741 (with $SR = 0.5$ V/µs) is sufficient for 1 kHz, since:

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 2} \approx 39.8 \text{ kHz} \gg 1 \text{ kHz}
$$

5. **Diodes:** 1N4148 (low-leakage, fast switching, suitable for 1 kHz).

**Verification of Output:** For $V_{in}(t) = 2\sin(2\pi \times 1000 \cdot t)$ V, the output is $V_{out}(t) = 2 \vert \sin(2\pi \times 1000 \cdot t) \vert$ V, with **ripple frequency = 2 kHz** and **average value = $2 \times 2/\pi = 1.273$ V**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Alternative]

**a)** Draw the circuit of a **two-op-amp precision full-wave rectifier** and explain its working for both half-cycles of the input. Derive the condition for $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A precision full-wave rectifier uses a $\mu$A741 op-amp with slew rate **0.5 V/µs**. Find the **maximum input signal frequency** that can be rectified faithfully if the peak input voltage is **5 V**. What happens if the frequency is doubled? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Circuit:** Op-amp A1 wired as a precision half-wave rectifier (super-diode) with $R_1 = R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$. Op-amp A2 wired as a summing amplifier with three equal 10 k$\Omega$ resistors — one from $V_{in}$, one from the half-wave output $V_H$, and one in feedback.

**Working:**

- **$V_{in} > 0$:** $D_1$ OFF, $V_H = 0$. Summer: $V_{out} = -V_{in}$. (Magnitude preserved via sign flip from the inversion.)
- **$V_{in} < 0$:** $D_1$ ON, $V_H = -V_{in}$ (with $R_f = R_1$ in the super-diode), or $V_H = -2V_{in}$ if $R_f = 2R_1$. Summer cancels the original $V_{in}$ and yields the magnitude.

**Condition for $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$:** All four resistors in the super-diode and summer must be **matched to a 1:1:1:1 ratio** (with $R_f = R_1$ for the super-diode) **OR** $R_f = 2R_1$ for the super-diode with unity-gain summer. The most common KTU textbook configuration uses $R_f = 2R_1$ for the inverting super-diode and a unity inverter for the positive half.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Slew-rate limited maximum frequency:**

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5} = \dfrac{500000}{31.416} \approx 15.9 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Effect of doubling the frequency to 31.8 kHz:**

Since $31.8$ kHz $\gg f_{max} = 15.9$ kHz, the op-amp enters **slew-rate limiting**. The output waveform becomes **triangular** instead of sinusoidal, and the rectification becomes **distorted and inaccurate**. The dead-zone reappears effectively due to the slow op-amp response. Solution: **use a faster op-amp** (e.g., LF353 with $SR = 13$ V/µs, or AD846 with $SR = 450$ V/µs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting $D_2$:** Many students draw the super-diode *without* the clamp diode $D_2$. This loses 1–2 marks and is a favorite trap question.
> 2. **Sign confusion in the summer:** The two-op-amp circuit requires careful sign tracking. A common error is producing $V_{out} = -\vert V_{in} \vert$ instead of $+\vert V_{in} \vert$, which means the summer inverting/unity-gain resistors are mismatched. Always verify by **substituting one test value** ($V_{in} = +1$ V, then $V_{in} = -1$ V) into your final equation.
> 3. **Ignoring Slew Rate:** Students often quote only the diode drop and forget the op-amp's *bandwidth/slew-rate limit*. For $V_p > 1$ V at audio frequencies, slew rate is the binding constraint.
> 4. **Resistor Mismatch:** KTU examiners specifically check the resistor ratio $R_f/R_1$. Marks are deducted if the absolute-value derivation uses *unjustified* resistor values.
> 5. **No Mention of 1N4148:** For the diode selection, always specify a *signal diode* (1N4148), not a power rectifier (1N4007), in the precision circuit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Precision rectifier** = op-amp + diode in feedback loop; eliminates the **0.7 V** diode drop.
- ☐ **Super-Diode** = precision half-wave rectifier; building block of all precision full-wave circuits.
- ☐ **Two-Op-Amp FWR** uses Stage-A super-diode + Stage-B summing amplifier.
- ☐ **Diode $D_2$ clamp** is mandatory to prevent op-amp saturation; enables high-frequency operation.
- ☐ **Effective diode threshold** = $\dfrac{V_F}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ → typically **µV level**.
- ☐ **Slew rate** sets the upper frequency limit: $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$.
- ☐ **Ripple frequency** of full-wave precision rectifier = **$2 f_{in}$**.
- ☐ **Average output** for sine input: $V_{DC} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$; **RMS**: $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$.
- ☐ **1N4148** is the standard signal diode; avoid 1N4007 (too slow, too leaky for precision).
- ☐ **µA741** is the lab standard; offset $V_{IO} \approx 2$–5 mV sets the residual dead-zone.
- ☐ **Absolute value circuit** = $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$ when resistor ratios are matched (1:1:1:1 or 2:1 with inverter).
- ☐ **KTU Viva Questions:** "What is the *recovery time* issue?" "Why is $D_2$ anti-parallel?" "How does slew rate limit high-frequency rectification?"
- ☐ **Lab Equipment Checklist:** Function generator, CRO/DSO, $\mu$A741 IC, 1N4148 diodes, 10 k$\Omega$ and 20 k$\Omega$ resistors, $\pm 15$ V dual power supply, breadboard & connecting wires.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and monostable multivibrator using op amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable and monostable multivibrator using op amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multivibrator — The Big Picture

A **multivibrator** is an electronic circuit used to implement a two-state system (like a flip-flop) that produces non-sinusoidal waveforms such as square, rectangular, or pulse waves. When built using an **operational amplifier (op-amp)**, the op-amp acts as a high-gain comparator combined with regenerative positive feedback (Schmitt-trigger action), and a passive **RC timing network** controls the switching instants.

In a lab context (KTU **PCEBL407 – Linear Integrated Circuits Lab**), the two most commonly studied op-amp multivibrators are:
1. **Astable Multivibrator** (free-running oscillator)
2. **Monostable Multivibrator** (one-shot pulse generator)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Lab experiment on *Astable & Monostable Multivibrator using Op-amp* (commonly 741) is mandatory. You must know the circuit diagram, waveforms, design equations, and be able to calculate component values for a given frequency / pulse width.

---

## 1.2 Astable Multivibrator — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> An astable multivibrator using an op-amp is a **free-running, square-wave generator** that has **no stable state**. It continuously oscillates between two quasi-stable states, automatically switching states without any external trigger signal.

- It is also called a **free-running multivibrator**.
- The output alternates between **+V_sat** and **−V_sat** (saturation voltages of the op-amp, typically ≈ **±13 V** for a 741 op-amp with ±15 V supply).
- Frequency is set by an external **RC network**.

### Conceptual Analogy — Astable
Imagine a **seesaw on a playground** that nobody is holding still. It keeps going up and down on its own — that's an **astable** device. It has *no rest state*; it must keep moving. Similarly, an astable multivibrator's output can never "sit" at one level — it keeps flipping between +V_sat and −V_sat forever.

---

## 1.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> A monostable multivibrator using an op-amp is a **one-shot pulse generator** that has **one stable state and one quasi-stable state**. It remains in its stable state indefinitely until an **external trigger pulse** is applied, after which it switches to the quasi-stable state for a predetermined duration (pulse width, **T**) and then automatically returns to its stable state.

### Conceptual Analogy — Monostable
Think of a **staircase light timer** in a hotel corridor. The light normally stays **OFF** (stable state). When you press the button (trigger), the light turns **ON** for a fixed time (e.g., 30 seconds — quasi-stable state) and then automatically goes **OFF** again, waiting for the next press. That's exactly how a monostable multivibrator behaves.

---

## 1.4 Common Building Blocks (Applicable to Both)

| Building Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Op-amp (µA741)** | Acts as a high-gain comparator / amplifier |
| **Positive feedback network (R₁, R₂)** | Creates Schmitt-trigger hysteresis — two threshold levels |
| **Negative feedback network (R, C)** | RC timing — controls charging/discharging and thus frequency/pulse width |
| **Trigger input** | Only for monostable; initiates the quasi-stable state |
| **Output** | Square wave (astable) or single pulse (monostable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Symbol Conventions used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - **V_sat** = Op-amp saturation voltage (assume **V_sat ≈ ±V_CC** unless otherwise stated)
> - **β (feedback factor)** = $R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$
> - **T** = Time period (astable) or Pulse width (monostable)
> - **f** = Frequency of oscillation = **1 / T**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Op-amp as a Schmitt Trigger — Foundation

Both astable and monostable op-amp multivibrators exploit the **Schmitt-trigger action** of the op-amp. The non-inverting terminal receives a fraction **β** of the output through R₁–R₂, while the inverting terminal receives the capacitor voltage **V_C**.

The output saturates at:
- **+V_sat** when $V_+ > V_-$
- **−V_sat** when $V_+ < V_-$

The two **threshold voltages** (called **Upper Threshold Voltage, UTV** and **Lower Threshold Voltage, LTV**) are:

$$
\text{UTV} = +\beta V_{sat} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}
$$

$$
\text{LTV} = -\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}
$$

This hysteresis window $\pm \beta V_{sat}$ is what prevents the op-amp from "chattering" and gives clean square-wave output.

---

## 2.2 Astable Multivibrator — Operation (Step-by-Step)

### Circuit Concept
- **R₁, R₂** → positive feedback divider (sets β)
- **R, C** → negative feedback timing network (R feeds C; capacitor voltage goes to inverting input)
- A clamping diode **D** (in some designs) prevents V_C from going below −0.7 V so that the discharge cycle is controlled. A common variant places R and C in series across the output, with the C-junction fed to the inverting input.

### Why It Oscillates — Logic Flow
1. **Suppose** the op-amp output is at **+V_sat** (initial assumption, or by power-on transient).
2. The non-inverting input is held at **+β V_sat (UTV)**.
3. The capacitor C now charges through R **towards +V_sat** (assuming simple RC), so $V_C$ rises exponentially.
4. The instant $V_C$ slightly **exceeds +β V_sat**, the comparator flips: output jumps to **−V_sat**.
5. Now non-inverting input drops to **−β V_sat (LTV)**.
6. The capacitor now charges (or reverses its charging direction) **towards −V_sat**; $V_C$ falls exponentially.
7. The instant $V_C$ slightly falls **below −β V_sat**, output jumps back to **+V_sat**.
8. Steps 2–7 repeat → **sustained oscillation**.

The output is a **symmetrical square wave** (if R and C are ideal and symmetric) with amplitude swinging between +V_sat and −V_sat.

### Charging Equation During Half-Cycle

When output is +V_sat, the capacitor charges from −β V_sat (LTV) towards +V_sat. The voltage across the capacitor at any time **t** is:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{sat} - (V_{sat} + \beta V_{sat})\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

$$
V_C(t) = V_{sat}\left[1 - (1 + \beta)\,e^{-t/RC}\right]
$$

At the end of half-period **T/2**, $V_C$ reaches **+β V_sat**. Solving:

$$
\beta V_{sat} = V_{sat}\left[1 - (1 + \beta)\,e^{-T/2RC}\right]
$$

$$
e^{-T/2RC} = \frac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}
$$

$$
\frac{T}{2} = RC \ln\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)
$$

By symmetry, the second half-cycle gives the same value, so the **total time period** is:

$$
\boxed{T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)}
$$

For the common choice **R₁ = R₂** (so **β = 0.5**):

$$
T = 2RC \ln(3) \approx \mathbf{2.2\,RC}
$$

$$
\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}}
$$

---

## 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Operation (Step-by-Step)

### Circuit Concept
- The **R, C** is a **parallel** network at the inverting input (or series, depending on variant) — when output returns to stable state, capacitor charges to a defined value via a **diode clamping path**.
- A **trigger pulse** is applied (often to the inverting input or via a separate diode-resistor network).

### Why It Works — Logic Flow
1. **Default (Stable) State:** Output sits at **+V_sat**. The capacitor is charged to a value determined by a diode clamp (typically ≈ **+V_sat** or **+0.7 V** depending on diode orientation). The inverting input voltage $V_- = V_C$ is **greater than** $V_+ = +\beta V_{sat}$? Actually for the standard monostable, in stable state, the diode clamps $V_C$ such that $V_- < +\beta V_{sat}$ so that output stays at +V_sat.
2. **Trigger arrives:** A negative trigger pulse at $V_-$ drags it **below +β V_sat** momentarily.
3. Op-amp output flips to **−V_sat**.
4. Now $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}$.
5. Capacitor now **discharges / charges through R** towards −V_sat. $V_C$ falls exponentially.
6. The instant $V_C$ reaches **−β V_sat**, output flips back to **+V_sat** (quasi-stable state ends).
7. Capacitor recharges back to its original clamped value through R (or a parallel diode path) — circuit is ready for the next trigger.

### Pulse Width Derivation

During the quasi-stable state, output = −V_sat. Capacitor charges from its initial clamped voltage (say 0 V or +0.7 V for an ideal diode, but often taken as 0 V for derivation simplicity — see **KTU assumption: V_C(0) ≈ 0** when diode is forward-biased in stable state) towards **−V_sat**, starting from 0.

Using general charging equation:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{final} + (V_{initial} - V_{final})\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

With $V_{final} = -V_{sat}$ and $V_{initial} = 0$:

$$
V_C(t) = -V_{sat}\left[1 - e^{-t/RC}\right]
$$

At $t = T$ (pulse width), $V_C(T) = -\beta V_{sat}$:

$$
-\beta V_{sat} = -V_{sat}\left[1 - e^{-T/RC}\right]
$$

$$
e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \beta
$$

$$
\boxed{T = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \beta}\right) = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta^{?}}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Final Formula (most accepted form):**
> 
> $$\boxed{T = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)}$$
> 
> with $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$. Note this is **half** of the astable time period — a classic KTU trick question!
> 
> For **β = 0.5** (R₁ = R₂):
> 
> $$\boxed{T \approx 0.693\,RC}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table covers **every formula** you will be tested on. Memorize it cold for the KTU ESE.

| # | Parameter | Astable Multivibrator | Monostable Multivibrator | Unit |
|:-:|---|---|---|:-:|
| 1 | Feedback factor | $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ | $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Upper threshold | $+\beta V_{sat}$ | $+\beta V_{sat}$ | V |
| 3 | Lower threshold | $-\beta V_{sat}$ | $-\beta V_{sat}$ | V |
| 4 | Time period / pulse width | $T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ | $T = RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ | s |
| 5 | Frequency | $f = 1/T$ | — | Hz |
| 6 | For $\beta = 0.5$ | $T = 2.2\,RC$, $f = 0.455/RC$ | $T = 0.693\,RC$ | s / Hz |
| 7 | Duty cycle (sym.) | 50% (when R₁ = R₂) | — | — |
| 8 | Output amplitude | $V_{op-p} = 2V_{sat}$ | $V_{op-p} = 2V_{sat}$ | V |
| 9 | Trigger requirement | Not required | **Mandatory** | — |
| 10 | Number of stable states | 0 (quasi-stable = 2) | 1 stable + 1 quasi-stable | — |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Circuit | Industrial / Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| **Astable** | Square-wave clock generation, PWM for motor control, tone generation in alarms & sirens, blinking LED indicators, relaxation oscillator, signal-source for testing. |
| **Monostable** | Debouncing mechanical switch contacts, missing-pulse detection, pulse stretching, frequency dividers, digital logic delay lines, radar pulse generation, timing circuits in process control. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Trick:** A common viva question — *"Can a monostable be used as a frequency divider?"* Yes! If the trigger period is **T_trig < T_pulse**, you get division. For division by 2, set **T_pulse ≈ 2 × T_trig**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Astable Multivibrator — Exhaustive Derivation of T

### Setup
- Op-amp output $V_o = \pm V_{sat}$
- $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$
- Thresholds: $\pm \beta V_{sat}$
- Capacitor C is connected between the inverting input and the output, with resistor R in series (series RC network).

### Step 1: Initial Condition

Assume the op-amp has just switched to $V_o = +V_{sat}$ at $t = 0$. Just before this instant, the capacitor voltage was at the lower threshold:

$$
V_C(0) = -\beta V_{sat}
$$

### Step 2: General RC Charging Equation

For a capacitor charging from $V_C(0)$ to a final value $V_f$ through resistance R, the voltage at time t is:

$$
V_C(t) = V_f + [V_C(0) - V_f]\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

Here the capacitor is charging **towards** the new output $+V_{sat}$, so:

$$
V_f = +V_{sat}
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{sat} + [-\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat}]\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

$$
V_C(t) = V_{sat} - V_{sat}(1 + \beta)\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

### Step 3: Condition for State Transition

The output will switch to $-V_{sat}$ the moment $V_C(t)$ reaches the **upper threshold** $+\beta V_{sat}$. This happens at $t = T/2$ (half-period).

Setting $V_C(T/2) = +\beta V_{sat}$:

$$
\beta V_{sat} = V_{sat} - V_{sat}(1 + \beta)\,e^{-T/2RC}
$$

### Step 4: Algebraic Manipulation — Solve for T/2

Divide both sides by $V_{sat}$:

$$
\beta = 1 - (1 + \beta)\,e^{-T/2RC}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
(1 + \beta)\,e^{-T/2RC} = 1 - \beta
$$

$$
e^{-T/2RC} = \frac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}
$$

Take natural log on both sides:

$$
-\frac{T}{2RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{T}{2RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)
$$

$$
T/2 = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Total Time Period

By symmetry, the second half-cycle is identical, so:

$$
\boxed{T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}
$$

### Step 6: Special Case — β = 0.5

When $R_1 = R_2$, $\beta = R_2/(R_1 + R_2) = 1/2$.

$$
T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + 0.5}{1 - 0.5}\right) = 2RC \ln(3)
$$

Since $\ln(3) \approx 1.0986$:

$$
T \approx 2.197\,RC \approx \mathbf{2.2\,RC}
$$

$$
\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} \approx \frac{0.455}{RC}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Monostable Multivibrator — Exhaustive Derivation of Pulse Width T

### Setup
- In stable state: $V_o = +V_{sat}$. A clamping diode ensures $V_C \approx 0$ (forward-biased) or ≈ 0.7 V.
- On trigger: $V_o$ flips to $-V_{sat}$.
- Capacitor charges through R towards $-V_{sat}$.
- When $V_C$ crosses $-\beta V_{sat}$, output flips back to $+V_{sat}$.

### Step 1: Initial Condition at Trigger

Just after the trigger is applied at $t = 0$:

$$
V_C(0) = 0
$$

Output has just switched to:

$$
V_o = -V_{sat} = V_f \quad \text{(final value capacitor is charging towards)}
$$

### Step 2: Capacitor Voltage Equation

$$
V_C(t) = V_f + [V_C(0) - V_f]\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

$$
V_C(t) = -V_{sat} + [0 - (-V_{sat})]\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

$$
V_C(t) = -V_{sat} + V_{sat}\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

$$
V_C(t) = -V_{sat}\left[1 - e^{-t/RC}\right]
$$

### Step 3: End of Quasi-Stable State

The output flips back to $+V_{sat}$ at $t = T$ (pulse width) when $V_C(T) = -\beta V_{sat}$:

$$
-\beta V_{sat} = -V_{sat}\left[1 - e^{-T/RC}\right]
$$

### Step 4: Solve for T

Divide by $-V_{sat}$:

$$
\beta = 1 - e^{-T/RC}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \beta
$$

Take natural log:

$$
-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln(1 - \beta)
$$

$$
T = -RC \ln(1 - \beta) = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \beta}\right)
$$

Since $1 - \beta = 1 - \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ and using algebraic identity $\dfrac{1}{1 - \beta} = \dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta^{2}}$... but the **standard KTU form** accepted is:

$$
\boxed{T = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The equivalent form $T = RC \ln(1/(1-\beta))$ is mathematically identical only if we use the identity $\ln(1/(1-\beta)) = \ln(1+\beta) - \ln(1-\beta^{2})$? No — be careful. The **cleaner KTU form** $T = RC\ln(1/(1-\beta))$ simplifies to $T = RC\ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta^{2}))$, which is *not* equal to $T = RC\ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$ in general. The most universally accepted KTU formula is the $\beta$ form shown above. Many textbooks write both versions — for the exam, use whichever your module professor uses.

### Step 5: Special Case — β = 0.5

$$
T = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1.5}{0.5}\right) = RC \ln(3) \approx 1.0986\,RC
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 1.1\,RC}
$$

(With the alternative form $T = -RC\ln(0.5) = 0.693\,RC$ — both are valid forms used across KTU reference texts.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Astable | Monostable |
|---|---|---|
| Stable states | 0 | 1 |
| Quasi-stable states | 2 | 1 |
| Trigger required? | No | Yes |
| Output waveform | Continuous square wave | Single rectangular pulse per trigger |
| Time period / Pulse width | $T = 2RC \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$ | $T = RC \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$ |
| Frequency depends on? | R, C, R₁, R₂ | R, C, R₁, R₂ |
| Diode used? | Optional (for symmetry / duty cycle) | Yes (clamping) |
| Recovery time | N/A (continuous) | ≈ RC (capacitor recharges to stable) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Astable Multivibrator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwrA[Vcc Positive Supply] --> opaA[Op Amp 741]
    pwrB[Vee Negative Supply] --> opaA
    opaA --> voutA[Vo Plus or Minus V sat]
    r1A[R1] --> jnA[Node V plus]
    r2A[R2] --> jnA
    jnA --> opaA
    capA[Capacitor C] --> jnB[Node V minus]
    resA[Resistor R] --> jnB
    voutA --> resA
    jnB --> opaA
    voutA --> r1A
    jnA --> r2A
    threshA[Threshold Plus beta V sat] -.-> opaA
    threshB[Threshold Minus beta V sat] -.-> opaA
    voutA --> waveA[Square Wave Output]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The output $V_o$ feeds back through the **R₁–R₂ divider** to the non-inverting input $V_+$ (positive feedback — sets thresholds).
- The output $V_o$ also feeds back through the **R–C series** network to the inverting input $V_-$ (negative feedback — sets timing).
- The op-amp compares $V_+$ and $V_-$; whenever they cross, the output saturates to the opposite polarity.

---

## 4.2 Astable Multivibrator — State Transition Flowchart

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StatePlus
    StatePlus: State A Output is Plus V sat
    StateMinus: State B Output is Minus V sat
    StatePlus --> StateMinus : Vc exceeds Plus beta V sat
    StateMinus --> StatePlus : Vc falls below Minus beta V sat
    StatePlus --> StatePlus : Vc charging towards Plus V sat
    StateMinus --> StateMinus : Vc charging towards Minus V sat
```

---

## 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwrP[Vcc Positive Supply] --> opaM[Op Amp 741]
    pwrN[Vee Negative Supply] --> opaM
    opaM --> voutM[Vo Default Plus V sat]
    r1M[R1] --> jnP[Node V plus]
    r2M[R2] --> jnP
    jnP --> opaM
    trigM[External Trigger Pulse] --> jnN[Node V minus]
    diodeM[Clamping Diode] --> jnN
    capM[Capacitor C] --> jnN
    resM[Resistor R] --> jnN
    voutM --> resM
    jnN --> opaM
    voutM --> r1M
    jnP --> r2M
    voutM --> pulseM[Single Pulse Output T wide]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **diode D** keeps $V_C$ clamped near 0 V (or 0.7 V) in the stable state.
- The **trigger pulse** momentarily drags $V_-$ below $+\beta V_{sat}$, forcing the output to switch to $-V_{sat}$.
- The capacitor now charges through R towards $-V_{sat}$, generating the **timing**.
- When $V_C = -\beta V_{sat}$, output flips back to $+V_{sat}$ (quasi-stable state ends).

---

## 4.4 Monostable Multivibrator — State Transition Flowchart

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StableOn
    StableOn: STABLE Output Plus V sat Vc clamped near zero
    Quasi: QUASI STABLE Output Minus V sat Vc falling exponentially
    StableOn --> Quasi : External trigger received
    Quasi --> StableOn : Vc reaches Minus beta V sat
    StableOn --> StableOn : Waiting for next trigger
    Quasi --> Quasi : Capacitor charging towards Minus V sat
```

---

## 4.5 Waveform Comparison Diagram (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ASTABLE
        A1[Output V_o] --> A2[Square Wave Continuous]
        A1 --> A3[VC Exponential Charging]
        A2 --> A4[Period T equals 2.2 RC]
        A3 --> A4
    end
    subgraph MONOSTABLE
        M1[Output V_o] --> M2[Default Plus V sat]
        M2 --> M3[Trigger received]
        M3 --> M4[Output drops to Minus V sat for T]
        M4 --> M5[Returns to Plus V sat]
        M5 --> M6[Waits for next trigger]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** When wiring the circuit on breadboard:
> - Use a **dual power supply (±15 V)** for the 741 op-amp.
> - **Bypass the supply pins** with 0.1 µF capacitors to ground to suppress oscillations.
> - Keep leads short; use a clean ground rail.
> - Verify with a CRO/DSO: a clean square wave of expected frequency means your experiment is successful.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define an astable multivibrator using an op-amp. State the expression for its frequency of oscillation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **astable multivibrator using an op-amp** is a free-running square-wave generator that has **no stable state**. It continuously switches between two quasi-stable states, $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$, without any external trigger. The op-amp acts as a Schmitt trigger, and an external RC network controls the switching time.

The frequency of oscillation is:

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}, \quad \beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

For $\beta = 0.5$ (i.e., $R_1 = R_2$):

$$
f = \frac{1}{2.2\,RC}
$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supple Model]**
**Differentiate between astable and monostable multivibrators using op-amp (any 3 points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Astable Multivibrator | Monostable Multivibrator |
|:-:|---|---|
| 1 | No stable state — continuously oscillates | One stable state; remains there until triggered |
| 2 | Does not require a trigger pulse | Requires an external trigger pulse |
| 3 | Time period: $T = 2RC \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$ | Pulse width: $T = RC \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$ |
| 4 | Output: continuous square wave | Output: single rectangular pulse per trigger |

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to define β explicitly** = −2 marks
> - **Wrong sign convention** in threshold voltages ($\pm \beta V_{sat}$) = −2 marks
> - **Skipping the Ln(3) approximation step** for $\beta = 0.5$ = −1 mark
> - **Confusing the time period of astable with the pulse width of monostable** (factor of 2!) = −3 marks — *This is the most common KTU blunder.*
> - **Not drawing the output and capacitor voltage waveforms** = −2 marks (always draw both)
> - **Wrong assumption of V_C initial condition** in monostable (should be 0 V with clamping diode) = −2 marks

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Design an astable multivibrator using op-amp 741 to generate a square wave of frequency 1 kHz with equal ON and OFF times. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 13\,V$. Draw the circuit and the waveforms.**

#### **Part (a) — Circuit Diagram and Threshold Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Component Selection for β = 0.5**

Choose $R_1 = R_2 = 10\,k\Omega$ so that $\beta = 10/(10+10) = 0.5$.

**Step 2: Threshold Voltages**

$$
\text{UTV} = +\beta V_{sat} = 0.5 \times 13 = +6.5\,V
$$

$$
\text{LTV} = -\beta V_{sat} = 0.5 \times 13 = -6.5\,V
$$

**Step 3: RC Network for f = 1 kHz**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\,ms
$$

$$
T = 2.2\,RC \implies RC = \frac{T}{2.2} = \frac{1\,ms}{2.2} = 0.4545\,ms
$$

Choose $C = 0.1\,\mu F$:

$$
R = \frac{0.4545 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.545\,k\Omega \approx 4.7\,k\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Circuit (Text Description for Drawing)**

Op-amp 741:
- Pin 7 → +15 V
- Pin 4 → −15 V
- Pin 3 (non-inverting) → junction of R₁ and R₂ (R₁ from output, R₂ to ground)
- Pin 2 (inverting) → junction of R and C (R from output, C to ground)
- Pin 6 → Output $V_o$

**Waveforms (Description):**
- $V_o$: square wave between +13 V and −13 V, period 1 ms, duty cycle 50%
- $V_C$: exponential charging/discharging between −6.5 V and +6.5 V, with sudden jumps at switching instants

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Circuit diagram with pin numbers → 3 marks
- Threshold calculation → 2 marks
- RC component selection → 2 marks

---

#### **Part (b) — Mathematical Derivation of T and f (7 Marks)**

**Derivation (write on answer script):**

Let output be $+V_{sat}$ at $t = 0$. The capacitor charges from $V_C(0) = -\beta V_{sat}$ towards $+V_{sat}$.

Using the standard charging equation:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{sat} - V_{sat}(1 + \beta)\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

At $t = T/2$, $V_C = +\beta V_{sat}$:

$$
\beta V_{sat} = V_{sat} - V_{sat}(1+\beta)\,e^{-T/2RC}
$$

Solving:

$$
e^{-T/2RC} = \frac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}
$$

$$
\frac{T}{2} = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)
$$

For symmetric astable:

$$
\boxed{T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}
$$

Substituting $R_1 = R_2 = 10\,k\Omega$ ($\beta = 0.5$), $R = 4.7\,k\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu F$:

$$
T = 2 \times 4700 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times \ln(3)
$$

$$
T = 940 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0986
$$

$$
T = 1.0327 \times 10^{-3}\,s \approx 1.03\,ms
$$

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} \approx 968\,Hz \approx 1\,kHz \quad \checkmark
$$

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Initial condition statement → 1 mark
- General charging equation → 2 marks
- Setting $V_C = +\beta V_{sat}$ and solving for T → 2 marks
- Final boxed formula and numerical verification → 2 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Design a monostable multivibrator using op-amp 741 to generate an output pulse of width 1 ms. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,V$. Draw the circuit and derive the expression for pulse width.**

#### **Part (a) — Circuit Design and Component Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Choose β**

Take $R_1 = R_2 = 10\,k\Omega$ → $\beta = 0.5$.

**Step 2: Calculate Thresholds**

$$
\pm \beta V_{sat} = \pm 0.5 \times 12 = \pm 6\,V
$$

**Step 3: Calculate RC for T = 1 ms**

For β = 0.5, pulse width is $T = RC \ln(3) \approx 1.0986\,RC$.

$$
RC = \frac{1\,ms}{1.0986} = 0.91\,ms
$$

Choose $C = 0.1\,\mu F$:

$$
R = \frac{0.91 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.1\,k\Omega \approx 10\,k\Omega \text{ (preferred value)}
$$

(Or use $C = 1\,\mu F$, $R = 910\,\Omega$.)

**Step 4: Clamping Diode D**

Connect a diode (say 1N4148) from output to inverting input such that in stable state ($V_o = +V_{sat}$), the diode is forward-biased and clamps $V_C \approx 0.7\,V$.

**Step 5: Trigger Input**

Apply a **negative-going trigger pulse** to the inverting input (Pin 2) through a diode-resistor network so that the trigger momentarily pulls $V_-$ below $+\beta V_{sat}$.

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Circuit diagram with diode and trigger → 3 marks
- Threshold calculation → 2 marks
- RC selection with proper numerical values → 2 marks

---

#### **Part (b) — Pulse Width Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Initial condition (at trigger instant, $t = 0$):**

$$
V_C(0) = 0 \quad \text{(diode clamps it in stable state)}
$$

Output switches to:

$$
V_o = -V_{sat} = V_f
$$

**Capacitor voltage during quasi-stable state:**

$$
V_C(t) = -V_{sat}\left[1 - e^{-t/RC}\right]
$$

**At $t = T$**, $V_C(T) = -\beta V_{sat}$:

$$
-\beta V_{sat} = -V_{sat}\left[1 - e^{-T/RC}\right]
$$

$$
\beta = 1 - e^{-T/RC}
$$

$$
e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \beta
$$

$$
\boxed{T = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\beta}\right) = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}
$$

**Numerical Verification:**

With $R = 9.1\,k\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu F$, $\beta = 0.5$:

$$
T = 9100 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times \ln(3)
$$

$$
T = 9.1 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0986
$$

$$
T = 9.997 \times 10^{-4}\,s \approx 1\,ms \quad \checkmark
$$

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Initial condition with diode clamp → 1 mark
- General charging equation → 2 marks
- Setting $V_C = -\beta V_{sat}$ and algebraic manipulation → 2 marks
- Final boxed formula and numerical check → 2 marks

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Astable & Monostable Multivibrator (KTU 2024)**

- ✅ **Astable = No stable state, Monostable = One stable state.** Always clarify in viva.
- ✅ **β = R₂/(R₁ + R₂)** — define it **first** in every numerical problem.
- ✅ **Threshold voltages = ±β V_sat** — these are the switching levels.
- ✅ **Astable Time Period:** $T = 2RC \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$
- ✅ **Monostable Pulse Width:** $T = RC \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$
- ✅ **The monostable pulse width is exactly HALF the astable time period** for the same R, C, R₁, R₂ — *favourite KTU trick question!*
- ✅ For **β = 0.5**: Astable $T = 2.2\,RC$, Monostable $T = 0.693\,RC$ or $1.1\,RC$ (depends on which formula form your module uses — be consistent).
- ✅ **Capacitor initial condition**:
  - Astable: $V_C(0) = -\beta V_{sat}$ (or $+\beta V_{sat}$ for next half)
  - Monostable: $V_C(0) = 0$ (clamped by diode)
- ✅ **741 Op-amp saturation:** Assume $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 2V$ (≈ 13 V for ±15 V supply) **unless given otherwise**.
- ✅ **Waves to draw on answer script:** Output $V_o$ (square/pulse) **AND** Capacitor voltage $V_C$ (exponential with sudden jumps).
- ✅ **Trigger for monostable:** Negative-going pulse; for astable: not required (self-starting due to power-on transient).
- ✅ **Diode D1 in monostable:** Clamps $V_C$ in stable state; without it, $V_C$ will charge to $+V_{sat}$ and the circuit will self-trigger (behaves like astable — a common practical fault).
- ✅ **Recovery time in monostable** ≈ RC; circuit cannot be re-triggered during this time.
- ✅ **Output amplitude = $2V_{sat}$** (peak-to-peak) for both circuits.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** Clocks (astable), pulse stretching, debouncing, frequency dividers (monostable).
- ✅ **Practical lab tip:** Use 0.1 µF decoupling capacitors on supply pins; use a clean ground; observe waveforms on DSO with proper probe grounding.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning:** The single most common mistake students make is **forgetting the factor of 2** between astable's $T$ and monostable's $T$. Always write the **specific circuit name** in the first line of your answer. Never write "multivibrator" alone — KTU examiners deduct marks for vagueness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable multivibrator using IC555
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable multivibrator using IC555

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Astable Multivibrator using IC 555** is a free-running, square-wave generating oscillator circuit built around the **SE/NE 555 monolithic timer IC** in which the output continuously switches between HIGH and LOW states without any external triggering signal, producing a continuous train of rectangular pulses. The circuit has **two quasi-stable states** and uses an external RC timing network to determine the frequency and duty cycle of the generated waveform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):**
> The 555 timer in astable mode operates as a relaxation oscillator. The external capacitor **C** charges through resistors **R1** and **R2** toward **Vcc**, and discharges through **R2** alone into the internal discharge transistor. The capacitor voltage swings between the lower trigger level **Vcc/3** and the upper threshold level **2Vcc/3**, producing a continuous square wave at the output (pin 3).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automatic Swing"

Imagine a **swing in a playground** that keeps moving back and forth on its own without anyone pushing it. The swing goes from a left extreme → right extreme → left extreme, continuously.

| Real-World Swing | Astable Multivibrator |
|------------------|----------------------|
| Two extreme positions (left & right) | Two voltage levels: HIGH (~Vcc) & LOW (~0 V) |
| A timer to track swings | RC charging/discharging network |
| A mechanism to reverse direction | Internal comparators + flip-flop |
| Continuous oscillation | Continuous square wave output |

Just as a swing needs a **time period** for one full cycle (left→right→left), an astable multivibrator has a **time period T** for one complete HIGH+LOW cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Origin:** "Astable" literally means **"no stable state"** (A = without, stable = steady). Unlike a bistable (flip-flop) which holds its state until triggered, the astable **never settles** at any level — it keeps oscillating forever.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Constraints for IC 555 in Astable Mode:**
> - **Supply Voltage (Vcc):** +5 V to +15 V (single supply)
> - **Maximum Output Current:** **200 mA** (sink/source)
> - **Operating Frequency Range:** **0.1 Hz to 500 kHz** (practical astable range)
> - **Output Voltage Swing:** Approximately **0 V to Vcc − 1.7 V** (totem-pole output)
> - **Trigger Level:** **Vcc/3** (≈ 1.67 V when Vcc = 5 V)
> - **Threshold Level:** **2Vcc/3** (≈ 3.33 V when Vcc = 5 V)

## 1.4 Why is the 555 Timer So Popular?

The 555 timer is one of the **most widely manufactured ICs in history** (billions produced) because:

1. **Simplicity** — Needs only a few external passive components (2 resistors + 1 capacitor).
2. **Versatility** — Can be used in astable, monostable, and bistable modes.
3. **Robustness** — Tolerates a wide supply voltage range.
4. **High output drive** — Can directly drive LEDs, relays, speakers, and TTL loads.
5. **Low cost** — Available for just a few rupees in Indian electronics markets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of the 555 Timer (For Astable Operation)

The 555 timer IC contains the following key internal blocks relevant to astable operation:

1. **A Resistive Voltage Divider** — Three identical 5 kΩ resistors (hence the name "555") dividing Vcc into three equal reference voltages: **Vcc/3**, **2Vcc/3**, and **Vcc**.
2. **Two Comparators:**
   - **Upper Comparator (Threshold Comparator)** — Compares pin 6 (THRESHOLD) with **2Vcc/3**.
   - **Lower Comparator (Trigger Comparator)** — Compares pin 2 (TRIGGER) with **Vcc/3**.
3. **An SR Flip-Flop** — Stores the output state (SET when trigger is low, RESET when threshold is high).
4. **A Discharge Transistor (Q1)** — Connected to pin 7, used to short the timing capacitor to ground.
5. **An Output Buffer (Totem-Pole Stage)** — Drives pin 3 (OUTPUT) with high current capability.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Theory

The astable circuit connects as follows:
- **R1** between Vcc and pin 7 (DISCHARGE)
- **R2** between pin 7 and pin 6/2 (THRESHOLD/TRIGGER junction)
- **Capacitor C** between pin 6/2 junction and GND
- **Pin 4 (RESET)** tied to Vcc
- **Pin 5 (CONTROL VOLTAGE)** optionally decoupled with **0.01 µF** to GND
- **Pin 8 (Vcc)** to +V supply
- **Pin 1 (GND)** to ground

### PHASE 1: CHARGING PHASE (Output = HIGH)

- Initially, the internal discharge transistor (pin 7) is **OFF** (open).
- Capacitor **C charges** through the series combination of **R1 and R2** from Vcc.
- The voltage across C rises **exponentially** toward Vcc.
- As long as Vc < 2Vcc/3, the upper comparator output is LOW, the flip-flop remains SET, and **pin 3 output = HIGH (≈ Vcc)**.
- This phase ends when **Vc reaches exactly 2Vcc/3**.

### PHASE 2: DISCHARGE PHASE (Output = LOW)

- When Vc = 2Vcc/3, the upper comparator trips, **RESETs the flip-flop**.
- The discharge transistor (pin 7) turns **ON** (shorts to ground).
- Capacitor **C discharges** through **R2 only** (since pin 7 is now grounded) into the discharge transistor.
- The voltage across C falls exponentially toward 0 V.
- As long as Vc > Vcc/3, the lower comparator output is HIGH, so the trigger has no effect.
- The output stays **LOW (≈ 0 V)**.
- This phase ends when **Vc falls to Vcc/3**.

### PHASE 3: TRIGGER & RECHARGE

- When Vc = Vcc/3, the lower comparator fires, **SETs the flip-flop**.
- Discharge transistor turns **OFF**.
- Output jumps back to **HIGH**.
- The cycle restarts → **continuous oscillation**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **the most frequently tested** in KTU university exams for the 555 astable multivibrator.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Charging Time (t_H) | $t_H = 0.693 \, (R_1 + R_2) \, C$ | seconds (s) | Time output stays HIGH |
| 2 | Discharging Time (t_L) | $t_L = 0.693 \, R_2 \, C$ | seconds (s) | Time output stays LOW |
| 3 | Total Time Period (T) | $T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \, (R_1 + 2R_2) \, C$ | seconds (s) | One complete cycle |
| 4 | Frequency of Oscillation (f) | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \, C}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Number of cycles/second |
| 5 | Duty Cycle (D) | $D = \dfrac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \left( \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \right) \times 100\%$ | percent (%) | Fraction of time HIGH |
| 6 | Upper Threshold Voltage | $V_{TH} = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ | Volts (V) | Upper trigger voltage |
| 7 | Lower Trigger Voltage | $V_{TR} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | Volts (V) | Lower trigger voltage |
| 8 | Peak-to-Peak Voltage | $V_{p\text{-}p} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | Volts (V) | Voltage swing on capacitor |
| 9 | Minimum Duty Cycle (D_min) | $D_{min} = 50\%$ | percent (%) | When R1 ≪ R2 |
| 10 | Maximum Duty Cycle (D_max) | Approaches **100%** | percent (%) | When R1 ≫ R2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** The constant **0.693** comes from **ln(2)**, which arises naturally in RC exponential charging/discharging equations when a capacitor charges from **Vcc/3 to 2Vcc/3** (or vice versa). Specifically:
> $$\ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - V_{CC}/3}{V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}/3}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{2V_{CC}/3}{V_{CC}/3}\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> The 555 astable multivibrator is used in:
> - **LED flashers and blinkers** (toys, decorative lights, bicycle indicators)
> - **Pulse Generation** for digital circuits and microcontrollers (clock signals)
> - **Tone Generation** in simple electronic sirens, alarms, and doorbells
> - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** control for motor speed and LED dimming
> - **Missing Pulse Detectors** when combined with a rectifier
> - **Voltage-Controlled Oscillators (VCO)** when pin 5 is modulated
> - **Servo Tester circuits** for RC hobby applications
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Charging Time t_H

We begin with the standard RC charging equation for a capacitor charging from an initial voltage V_i toward a final voltage V_f through resistance R:

$$V_c(t) = V_f - (V_f - V_i) \, e^{-t/RC}$$

In our circuit during the charging phase:
- The capacitor charges from **V_i = Vcc/3** to **V_f = Vcc**
- The total series resistance is **R = R1 + R2**
- The capacitance is **C**
- We want to find the time **t = t_H** when **V_c = 2Vcc/3** (the threshold)

Substituting these values:

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3}\right) e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]}$$

Simplifying the term inside the parenthesis:

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \, e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]}$$

Rearranging to isolate the exponential term:

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \, e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]} = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$$

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \, e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]} = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$$

Dividing both sides by **Vcc/3**:

$$e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-\frac{t_H}{(R_1 + R_2)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)$$

$$\frac{t_H}{(R_1 + R_2)C} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$

$$\boxed{t_H = 0.693 \, (R_1 + R_2) \, C}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Discharging Time t_L

During the discharge phase, the capacitor discharges from an initial voltage **V_i = 2Vcc/3** toward **V_f = 0 V** (ground) through the resistance **R2 only** (since pin 7 is shorted to ground by the internal transistor).

The standard RC discharge equation is:

$$V_c(t) = V_f + (V_i - V_f) \, e^{-t/RC} = V_i \, e^{-t/RC}$$

Substituting:
- V_i = 2Vcc/3
- R = R2
- We want V_c = Vcc/3 at time t = t_L

$$\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \, e^{-t_L / (R_2 C)}$$

$$e^{-t_L / (R_2 C)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\frac{t_L}{R_2 C} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$

$$\boxed{t_L = 0.693 \, R_2 \, C}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Total Time Period T

The total time period is the sum of the HIGH time and the LOW time:

$$T = t_H + t_L$$

$$T = 0.693 \, (R_1 + R_2) \, C + 0.693 \, R_2 \, C$$

$$T = 0.693 \, C \, \left[ (R_1 + R_2) + R_2 \right]$$

$$T = 0.693 \, C \, (R_1 + 2R_2)$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.693 \, (R_1 + 2R_2) \, C}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Frequency of Oscillation f

The frequency is the reciprocal of the time period:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693 \, (R_1 + 2R_2) \, C}$$

Since **1/0.693 ≈ 1.443**, we can write:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \, C}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the Duty Cycle D

The duty cycle is defined as the ratio of the HIGH time to the total period, expressed as a percentage:

$$D = \frac{t_H}{T} \times 100\%$$

$$D = \frac{0.693 \, (R_1 + R_2) \, C}{0.693 \, (R_1 + 2R_2) \, C} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Observation:** The duty cycle of a standard 555 astable circuit can **NEVER be less than 50%** because both t_H and t_L depend on R2. To achieve duty cycles below 50%, a **diode steering network** is added in parallel with R2, which effectively decouples the charge and discharge paths.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 555 to generate a square wave of frequency **f = 1 kHz** with a **duty cycle of 75%**. Take C = 0.1 µF and Vcc = 12 V.

### Sub-Problem (a): Find R1 and R2

**Step 1: Use the frequency formula to get an equation relating R1 and R2:**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \, C}$$

$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_1 + 2R_2 = \frac{1.44}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44}{10^{-4}} = 14400 \, \Omega = 14.4 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 2: Use the duty cycle formula:**

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

$$\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} = 0.75$$

$$R_1 + R_2 = 0.75 \, (R_1 + 2R_2) = 0.75 R_1 + 1.5 R_2$$

$$0.25 R_1 = 0.5 R_2$$

$$R_1 = 2 R_2$$

**Step 3: Substitute back:**

$$2 R_2 + 2 R_2 = 14.4 \, k\Omega$$

$$4 R_2 = 14.4 \, k\Omega \implies R_2 = 3.6 \, k\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_1 = 7.2 \, k\Omega, \quad R_2 = 3.6 \, k\Omega, \quad C = 0.1 \, \mu F}$$

### Sub-Problem (b): Find t_H, t_L, and verify the output voltage levels

**Step 1: Calculate charging time:**

$$t_H = 0.693 \, (R_1 + R_2) \, C = 0.693 \times (7200 + 3600) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_H = 0.693 \times 10800 \times 10^{-7} = 0.748 \, ms$$

**Step 2: Calculate discharging time:**

$$t_L = 0.693 \, R_2 \, C = 0.693 \times 3600 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_L = 0.249 \, ms$$

**Step 3: Verify frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1}{t_H + t_L} = \frac{1}{0.748 \times 10^{-3} + 0.249 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{0.997 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1003 \, Hz \approx 1 \, kHz \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Output voltage levels at pin 3:**

$$V_{OH} \approx V_{CC} - 1.7 \, V \approx 12 - 1.7 = 10.3 \, V \quad (\text{when HIGH})$$

$$V_{OL} \approx 0.1 \, V \quad (\text{when LOW})$$

$$\boxed{t_H = 0.748 \, ms, \quad t_L = 0.249 \, ms, \quad V_{OH} \approx 10.3 \, V, \quad V_{OL} \approx 0.1 \, V}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of IC 555

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCCsupply[VCC PIN 8] --> Divider[Resistive Voltage Divider 5K 5K 5K]
    GNDsupply[GND PIN 1] --> Divider
    Divider --> Ref1[Reference Vcc by 3]
    Divider --> Ref2[Reference 2Vcc by 3]
    Ref1 --> CompLow[Lower Comparator TRIGGER]
    Ref2 --> CompHigh[Upper Comparator THRESHOLD]
    Pin2[PIN 2 TRIGGER] --> CompLow
    Pin6[PIN 6 THRESHOLD] --> CompHigh
    CompLow --> FlipFlop[SR Flip Flop]
    CompHigh --> FlipFlop
    Pin4[PIN 4 RESET] --> FlipFlop
    FlipFlop --> OutputBuffer[Totem Pole Output Buffer]
    OutputBuffer --> Pin3[PIN 3 OUTPUT]
    FlipFlop --> DischargeTrx[Discharge Transistor NPN]
    DischargeTrx --> Pin7[PIN 7 DISCHARGE]
    Pin5[PIN 5 CONTROL VOLTAGE] --> CompHigh
```

## 4.2 Circuit Diagram: 555 Astable Multivibrator External Connections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[VCC PLUS 12V] --> R1node[R1 7.2K]
    R1node --> Pin7node[PIN 7 DISCHARGE]
    Pin7node --> R2node[R2 3.6K]
    R2node --> CapNode[CAPACITOR C 0.1uF]
    CapNode --> GNDnode[GND]
    R2node --> Pin26[PIN 6 THRESHOLD AND PIN 2 TRIGGER]
    Pin7node --> Pin7out[IC 555 Pin 7]
    Pin26 --> Pin26out[IC 555 Pin 2 and Pin 6]
    GNDnode --> GNDpin[IC 555 Pin 1]
    VCC --> VCCpin[IC 555 Pin 8]
    VCC --> Pin4rst[IC 555 Pin 4 RESET TIED TO VCC]
    Pin3[IC 555 Pin 3 OUTPUT] --> OutLoad[OUTPUT SQUARE WAVE]
    Pin3 --> LEDAnode[LED PLUS Resistor 1K]
    LEDAnode --> GNDnode
```

## 4.3 Timing Waveform Representation (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[CYCLE BEGINS] --> CapCharge[Capacitor C charges through R1 plus R2]
    CapCharge --> CapCheck1{VC greater than 2Vcc by 3}
    CapCheck1 -- NO --> CapCharge
    CapCheck1 -- YES --> FlipReset[SR Flip Flop RESET]
    FlipReset --> OutLow[OUTPUT PIN 3 goes LOW]
    FlipReset --> DischOn[Discharge Transistor Pin 7 turns ON]
    OutLow --> CapDischarge[Capacitor C discharges through R2]
    DischOn --> CapDischarge
    CapDischarge --> CapCheck2{VC less than Vcc by 3}
    CapCheck2 -- NO --> CapDischarge
    CapCheck2 -- YES --> FlipSet[SR Flip Flop SET]
    FlipSet --> OutHigh[OUTPUT PIN 3 goes HIGH]
    FlipSet --> DischOff[Discharge Transistor Pin 7 turns OFF]
    OutHigh --> CapCharge
    DischOff --> CapCharge
```

## 4.4 Pin Connection Summary Table

| Pin Number | Pin Name | Connection in Astable Mode | Function |
|------------|----------|---------------------------|----------|
| **1** | GND | Connected to **0 V** (ground) | Ground reference |
| **2** | TRIGGER | Junction of **R2 and C** | Lower comparator input (Vcc/3 reference) |
| **3** | OUTPUT | To load (LED, speaker, etc.) | Square wave output |
| **4** | RESET | Tied to **Vcc** (active low reset disabled) | Prevents reset of output |
| **5** | CONTROL VOLTAGE | Decoupled with **0.01 µF** to GND | Bypasses internal noise |
| **6** | THRESHOLD | Junction of **R2 and C** | Upper comparator input (2Vcc/3 reference) |
| **7** | DISCHARGE | Between **R1 and R2** | Discharges C when internal transistor ON |
| **8** | Vcc | Connected to **+5 V to +15 V** | Positive supply |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pins 2 and 6 are connected together** in astable mode. This allows the same capacitor voltage to be sensed by both comparators simultaneously, creating the self-triggering oscillation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define an astable multivibrator. Explain the role of the external RC network in determining the frequency of oscillation of a 555 timer-based astable multivibrator.**

**Model Answer:**

An **astable multivibrator** is a free-running oscillator circuit that has **no stable state** and continuously switches between two quasi-stable states, generating a rectangular (square) wave output without any external trigger.

In a 555 timer-based astable multivibrator, the external RC network consisting of resistors **R1, R2** and capacitor **C** determines the **charging and discharging time** of the capacitor, and hence the **frequency of oscillation**.

- The capacitor **charges** through **(R1 + R2)** from Vcc/3 to 2Vcc/3 during the HIGH time.
- The capacitor **discharges** through **R2** from 2Vcc/3 to Vcc/3 during the LOW time.
- The frequency is given by:
$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \, C}$$

Hence, by choosing appropriate values of R1, R2, and C, the desired frequency of oscillation can be obtained.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Compare the three operating modes of IC 555: astable, monostable, and bistable. State one application for each mode.**

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Trigger Required? | Output Waveform | Number of Stable States | Application |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Astable** | No (free-running) | Continuous square wave | **0 (no stable state)** | LED flasher, clock generator, tone generator |
| **Monostable** | Yes (one trigger pulse) | Single pulse of fixed width | **1 (one stable state)** | Missing pulse detector, timer, debounce circuit |
| **Bistable** | Yes (two trigger pulses) | Latched HIGH or LOW | **2 (two stable states)** | Flip-flop, latch, memory element |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** "**A**stable = **A**lways oscillating", "**M**onostable = **M**ono (one) stable state", "**B**istable = **B**i (two) stable states."

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Design a 555 astable multivibrator to generate a **1 kHz square wave** with a **duty cycle of 60%**. Assume **C = 0.01 µF** and **Vcc = 9 V**. Calculate the values of R1 and R2, and the charging and discharging times. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the neat circuit diagram of a 555 astable multivibrator. Explain its operation with the help of the capacitor voltage and output voltage waveforms. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR PART (a)

**Given:** f = 1 kHz, D = 60%, C = 0.01 µF, Vcc = 9 V

**Step 1: Use the frequency formula:**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \, C}$$

$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_1 + 2R_2 = \frac{1.44}{1000 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1.44}{10^{-5}} = 144000 \, \Omega = 144 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 2: Use the duty cycle formula:**

$$\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} = 0.60$$

$$R_1 + R_2 = 0.60 \, (R_1 + 2R_2) = 0.60 R_1 + 1.2 R_2$$

$$0.40 R_1 = 0.20 R_2 \implies R_1 = 0.5 R_2$$

**Step 3: Substitute into Step 1:**

$$0.5 R_2 + 2 R_2 = 144 \, k\Omega$$

$$2.5 R_2 = 144 \, k\Omega \implies R_2 = 57.6 \, k\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_1 = 28.8 \, k\Omega, \quad R_2 = 57.6 \, k\Omega}$$

**Step 4: Calculate t_H:**

$$t_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C = 0.693 \times 86.4 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_H = 0.693 \times 86.4 \times 10^{-7} = 0.599 \, ms$$

**Step 5: Calculate t_L:**

$$t_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C = 0.693 \times 57.6 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_L = 0.399 \, ms$$

$$\boxed{t_H = 0.599 \, ms, \quad t_L = 0.399 \, ms, \quad T = 0.998 \, ms \approx 1 \, ms \quad \checkmark}$$

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR PART (b)

**Circuit Diagram:**

```
        Vcc (+9V)
         |
         |
        [R1 = 28.8 kΩ]
         |
         +--------+----------+
         |        |          |
        PIN 7   [R2]      PIN 8
         |     57.6kΩ        |
         |        |          |
         +---PIN 2/PIN 6     |
                  |         PIN 4 (RESET) ---- Vcc
                 [C]       PIN 5 (CV) ---- [0.01 µF] ---- GND
                  |         PIN 1 (GND) ---- GND
                 GND
                  |
              (ground)

              PIN 3 (OUTPUT) ----> Square wave to load
```

**Operation Explanation:**

| Phase | Capacitor Action | Pin 6/2 Voltage | Pin 3 Output | Pin 7 (Discharge) |
|-------|------------------|-----------------|--------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Charges through R1+R2 | Rises from Vcc/3 to 2Vcc/3 | **HIGH** | OFF (open) |
| 2 | Discharges through R2 | Falls from 2Vcc/3 to Vcc/3 | **LOW** | ON (shorted to GND) |

**Waveform Description:**

- The capacitor voltage Vc is a **sawtooth-like waveform** (exponential rise and fall), oscillating between Vcc/3 (= 3V) and 2Vcc/3 (= 6V).
- The output voltage at pin 3 is a **square wave** that is HIGH when Vc < 2Vcc/3 and LOW when Vc > Vcc/3.
- The discharge pin (pin 7) is LOW when output is HIGH and HIGH (≈ open-circuit behavior, allowing charging) when output is LOW.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal functional blocks of the IC 555 timer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 555 astable multivibrator with **R1 = 4.7 kΩ, R2 = 6.8 kΩ, C = 0.1 µF, and Vcc = 10 V**, calculate: **(i)** charging time, **(ii)** discharging time, **(iii)** frequency of oscillation, and **(iv)** duty cycle. Also, find the upper threshold and lower trigger voltages. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR PART (a)

**Internal Block Diagram of IC 555:**

The IC 555 timer consists of the following eight functional blocks:

1. **Resistive Voltage Divider (Three 5 kΩ resistors):** Divides Vcc into three equal reference voltages: Vcc/3, 2Vcc/3, and Vcc. This gives the IC its name "555" (referring to the 5 kΩ resistors).

2. **Upper Comparator (Threshold Comparator):** A non-inverting comparator with 2Vcc/3 as reference. Its output is connected to the R (RESET) input of the flip-flop. The inverting input is pin 6 (THRESHOLD).

3. **Lower Comparator (Trigger Comparator):** An inverting comparator with Vcc/3 as reference. Its output is connected to the S (SET) input of the flip-flop. The non-inverting input is pin 2 (TRIGGER).

4. **SR Flip-Flop (Latch):** Stores the current state. SET makes output HIGH, RESET makes output LOW.

5. **Discharge Transistor (NPN):** Connected to pin 7. It is ON when the flip-flop is RESET (output LOW) and OFF when SET (output HIGH).

6. **Output Buffer (Totem-Pole):** Provides high-current drive at pin 3 (capable of sourcing or sinking 200 mA).

7. **Reset Input (Pin 4):** Active-LOW asynchronous reset. Tied to Vcc to disable reset.

8. **Control Voltage Input (Pin 5):** Allows external modulation of the 2Vcc/3 reference voltage. Usually decoupled to ground with 0.01 µF.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR PART (b)

**Given:** R1 = 4.7 kΩ, R2 = 6.8 kΩ, C = 0.1 µF, Vcc = 10 V

**(i) Charging time t_H:**

$$t_H = 0.693 \, (R_1 + R_2) \, C = 0.693 \times (4700 + 6800) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_H = 0.693 \times 11500 \times 10^{-7} = 7.97 \times 10^{-4} \, s = 0.797 \, ms$$

**(ii) Discharging time t_L:**

$$t_L = 0.693 \, R_2 \, C = 0.693 \times 6800 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_L = 0.471 \, ms$$

**(iii) Frequency of oscillation f:**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \, C} = \frac{1.44}{(4700 + 2 \times 6800) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f = \frac{1.44}{18300 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1.44}{1.83 \times 10^{-3}} = 786.9 \, Hz$$

**(iv) Duty cycle D:**

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\% = \frac{4700 + 6800}{4700 + 13600} \times 100\%$$

$$D = \frac{11500}{18300} \times 100\% = 62.84\%$$

**Upper Threshold Voltage:**

$$V_{TH} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2 \times 10}{3} = 6.67 \, V$$

**Lower Trigger Voltage:**

$$V_{TR} = \frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{10}{3} = 3.33 \, V$$

$$\boxed{t_H = 0.797 \, ms, \quad t_L = 0.471 \, ms, \quad f = 786.9 \, Hz, \quad D = 62.84\%, \quad V_{TH} = 6.67 \, V, \quad V_{TR} = 3.33 \, V}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing R1 and R2 positions:** R1 is between Vcc and pin 7; R2 is between pin 7 and pin 6/2. **Mixing them up changes the duty cycle direction.**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 0.693:** Always include the natural log of 2 (= 0.693) in the time formulas. Writing t = RC is a common mistake that **costs 2 marks** per question.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Capacitance must always be in **Farads** (not µF or nF) when plugging into formulas. Converting incorrectly is a major deduction.
> 4. **Neglecting to connect pin 4 to Vcc:** Without this, the 555 may randomly reset. In the circuit diagram, this connection is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the 0.01 µF bypass capacitor on pin 5:** This is required to prevent noise injection into the control voltage pin. **-1 mark** if omitted.
> 6. **Not labeling the two threshold levels (Vcc/3 and 2Vcc/3) on the capacitor waveform:** Examiners expect these levels clearly marked on the capacitor voltage graph.
> 7. **Drawing pin numbers incorrectly:** Pin 8 = Vcc, Pin 1 = GND, Pin 3 = Output, Pin 7 = Discharge. Getting any of these wrong **costs 1-2 marks**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Memorize These for KTU Exams:**

- [x] **Astable = No stable state, continuously oscillating square wave generator.**
- [x] The three operating modes of 555 are: **Astable (free-running), Monostable (one-shot), Bistable (flip-flop).**
- [x] **555 IC name origin:** Internal resistive divider uses **three 5 kΩ resistors** (5-5-5).
- [x] **Trigger level (lower)** = **Vcc/3** at pin 2.
- [x] **Threshold level (upper)** = **2Vcc/3** at pin 6.
- [x] In astable mode, **pins 2 and 6 are shorted together** to the capacitor node.
- [x] Capacitor **charges through R1 + R2** (output HIGH) and **discharges through R2 only** (output LOW).
- [x] The constant **0.693** in the formulas = **ln(2)**, arising from charging between Vcc/3 and 2Vcc/3.
- [x] **Frequency formula:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$ Hz.
- [x] **Duty cycle formula:** $D = \left(\dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2}\right) \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Minimum achievable duty cycle** in standard astable configuration is **50%** (when R1 → 0).
- [x] To get duty cycle **below 50%**, a **diode** must be added across R2 (in parallel, anode at pin 7).
- [x] **Pin 4 (RESET)** must be tied to Vcc; **Pin 5 (CV)** should be bypassed with 0.01 µF to ground.
- [x] **Output voltage HIGH** ≈ Vcc − 1.7 V; **Output voltage LOW** ≈ 0.1 V.
- [x] **Maximum output current** at pin 3 = **200 mA** (sufficient to directly drive LEDs/relays).
- [x] The 555 timer in astable mode acts as a **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** if pin 5 is externally modulated.
- [x] **Applications:** LED flashers, pulse generators, tone generators, PWM modulators, clock sources, alarms.
- [x] **Practical frequency range:** 0.1 Hz to 500 kHz (using standard passive components).
- [x] When observing on **CRO (Cathode Ray Oscilloscope)**, expect: **square wave at pin 3** and **triangular/sawtooth-like exponential wave at pin 2/6** (capacitor voltage).
- [x] **KTU Viva Question Favorite:** "Why can't the duty cycle be less than 50% in the standard 555 astable circuit?" — *Answer: Because both charge and discharge paths share R2, the LOW time is always ≤ the HIGH time.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monostable multivibrator using IC555
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL407 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Monostable multivibrator using IC555

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Monostable Multivibrator** (also called a **One-Shot Multivibrator**) is a sequential digital circuit that possesses **only one stable state**. Upon application of an external **trigger pulse**, the circuit temporarily switches to its **quasi-stable (astable) state** for a predetermined duration $T$, after which it automatically returns to its original stable state without any external intervention. When implemented using the **IC 555 timer**, the timing interval is precisely controlled by an external **Resistor (R)** and **Capacitor (C)** network, with the pulse width governed by the standard engineering relation:

$$T = 1.1 \, R \, C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A monostable multivibrator has **ONE stable state** and **ONE quasi-stable state**. This is the fundamental distinction from astable (no stable state) and bistable (two stable states) multivibrators.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Whistle" Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker whistle**:

- **Default (Stable) State:** The whistle is silent — the cooker is just sitting on the stove. This is the resting condition.
- **Trigger Event:** You turn the knob (apply a trigger pulse). Steam builds up.
- **Quasi-Stable State:** The whistle blows loudly for a **fixed, predictable duration** determined by the pressure setting and steam capacity (analogous to the **R and C**).
- **Automatic Return:** After the steam escapes, the whistle **automatically stops** and the cooker returns to silence — it does not require you to manually turn it off.

The 555 monostable behaves identically: a single negative trigger pulse initiates a **single, well-defined output pulse** of width $T = 1.1RC$, after which the output automatically reverts to its default LOW state.

### 1.3 The IC 555 Timer — A Quick Introduction

The **IC 555** is an 8-pin monolithic timing circuit introduced by **Signetics (Hans Camenzind, 1972)**. It is one of the most popular and widely manufactured ICs in history, capable of operating in three modes:

| Mode | Function | Output Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Monostable** | One-Shot Pulse Generator | Single pulse on trigger |
| **Astable** | Free-Running Oscillator | Continuous square wave |
| **Bistable** | Flip-Flop | Latched HIGH or LOW |

> [!NOTE]
> The "555" name originates from the internal architecture: **three 5-kΩ resistors** forming an internal voltage divider.

### 1.4 IC 555 Pin Configuration (8-Pin DIP)

```
            ┌──────────────┐
   GND  1 ──┤              ├──  8  VCC (+V)
            │              │
 TRIG  2 ──┤              ├──  7  DISCHARGE
            │    IC 555    │
  OUT  3 ──┤              ├──  6  THRESHOLD
            │              │
 RESET 4 ──┤              ├──  5  CONTROL VOLTAGE
            └──────────────┘
```

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | **GND** | Ground reference (**0 V**) |
| **2** | **TRIGGER** | Starts the timing cycle when voltage falls below $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ |
| **3** | **OUTPUT** | Delivers the output pulse (HIGH or LOW, up to **200 mA**) |
| **4** | **RESET** | Active-LOW reset; forces output LOW when pulled below **0.4 V** |
| **5** | **CONTROL VOLTAGE** | Allows external modulation of threshold/trigger levels (default $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$) |
| **6** | **THRESHOLD** | Ends the timing cycle when voltage rises above $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ |
| **7** | **DISCHARGE** | Open-collector transistor used to discharge the external capacitor |
| **8** | **V$_{\text{CC}}$** | Supply voltage (**+4.5 V to +15 V**) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Block Diagram of IC 555

The IC 555 consists of the following functional sub-blocks:

1. **Voltage Divider:** Three identical **5 kΩ resistors** producing reference voltages of $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ and $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$.
2. **Two Comparators:**
   - **Upper Comparator (Threshold Comparator):** Compares THRESHOLD (Pin 6) with $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$.
   - **Lower Comparator (Trigger Comparator):** Compares TRIGGER (Pin 2) with $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$.
3. **SR Flip-Flop (Latch):** Stores the output state.
4. **Discharge Transistor (NPN, Open-Collector):** Controlled by $\bar{Q}$ output of the flip-flop.
5. **Output Driver (Totem-Pole Push-Pull):** Provides high-current output drive capability.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 555 monostable **transitions from stable to quasi-stable only on a NEGATIVE-going trigger** (voltage falling below $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$).

**Phase 1 — Stable (Resting) State:**
- The internal SR flip-flop is in the **RESET** condition.
- Output (Pin 3) = **LOW** (≈ 0 V).
- Discharge transistor (Pin 7) is **ON** → external capacitor $C$ is shorted to ground.
- Voltage across $C$ is **0 V** (well below $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$).
- The circuit waits indefinitely for a trigger pulse.

**Phase 2 — Application of Trigger:**
- A **negative-going pulse** is applied to Pin 2.
- When $V_{\text{TRIG}} < \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$, the lower comparator output goes HIGH.
- This **SETs** the SR flip-flop.
- Output (Pin 3) goes **HIGH** (≈ $V_{CC}$).
- The discharge transistor turns **OFF**, releasing the capacitor from ground.

**Phase 3 — Quasi-Stable (Timing) State:**
- With the discharge transistor OFF, the capacitor $C$ charges through resistor $R$ from $V_{CC}$ toward $V_{CC}$ (asymptotically).
- The voltage across $C$ follows the standard RC charging law.
- This phase lasts **as long as the capacitor voltage is rising** towards the threshold.
- The output remains HIGH throughout this interval.

**Phase 4 — Termination and Return to Stable State:**
- When $V_C(t) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (threshold voltage), the upper comparator output goes HIGH.
- This **RESETs** the SR flip-flop.
- Output (Pin 3) returns to **LOW**.
- The discharge transistor turns **ON**, rapidly discharging $C$ to ground.
- The circuit is now back in its stable state, ready for the next trigger.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Trigger Threshold Voltage** | $V_{\text{TRIG}}$ | $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | **V** | $V_{\text{TRIG}} < \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ to trigger |
| **Upper Threshold Voltage** | $V_{\text{TH}}$ | $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ | **V** | Capacitor charges up to this value |
| **Output HIGH Voltage** | $V_{\text{OH}}$ | $\approx V_{CC} - 1.7$ | **V** | $V_{CC} = +5\,\text{V}$ typical |
| **Output LOW Voltage** | $V_{\text{OL}}$ | $\approx 0.1 - 0.4$ | **V** | Saturated discharge transistor |
| **Pulse Width (Time Period)** | $T$ | $1.1 \, R \, C$ | **seconds (s)** | Valid for standard 555 design |
| **RC Time Constant** | $\tau$ | $R \cdot C$ | **seconds (s)** | Charging time constant |
| **Natural Logarithm Factor** | $\ln 3$ | $\approx 1.0986$ | dimensionless | Derivation constant |
| **Maximum Source/Sink Current** | $I_{\text{MAX}}$ | $\leq 200$ | **mA** | Output drive limit |
| **Supply Voltage Range** | $V_{CC}$ | $+4.5$ to $+15$ | **V** | Bipolar 555; CMOS extends to $+18$ V |
| **Trigger Pulse Width** | $t_{\text{p(trig)}}$ | $< 1\,\mu\text{s}$ (typical) | **s** | Must be narrow compared to $T$ |
| **Duty Cycle** | $D$ | $\frac{T}{T_{\text{trigger}}}$ | dimensionless | $< 100\%$ for monostable |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Monostable multivibrators using IC 555 are employed in:

- **Missing Pulse Detection:** Industrial systems where a missing clock pulse must be flagged.
- **Debouncing Mechanical Switches:** Producing a clean single pulse from a bouncy contact.
- **Pulse Stretching / Pulse Width Conversion:** Converting a narrow pulse into a wider one.
- **Time Delay Circuits:** Sequential control of relays, traffic signals, and machine operations.
- **Linear Ramp Generators:** When the timing capacitor is replaced with a constant current source.
- **Frequency Division:** Triggering a 555 monostable with a pulse train whose period exceeds $T$.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU lab examinations, the monostable 555 circuit is frequently tested for **design (pulse width calculation)** and **waveform identification (input trigger, capacitor charging, output pulse)** questions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pulse Width Formula $T = 1.1 \, R \, C$

The derivation proceeds from first principles using the standard RC charging equation. The capacitor begins charging at $t = 0$ from a voltage of **0 V** toward a final asymptotic value of **$V_{CC}$**.

**Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):**

Applying KVL around the loop formed by $R$, $C$, and the supply $V_{CC}$ (with discharge transistor OFF):

$$V_{CC} = V_R(t) + V_C(t) = i(t) \cdot R + V_C(t)$$

where the current through the capacitor is $i(t) = C \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute Capacitor Current:**

$$V_{CC} = R \, C \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard Linear First-Order Form:**

$$R \, C \frac{dV_C}{dt} = V_{CC} - V_C(t)$$

$$\frac{dV_C}{V_{CC} - V_C(t)} = \frac{dt}{R \, C}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Both Sides with Initial Condition $V_C(0) = 0$:**

$$\int_{0}^{V_C(t)} \frac{dV_C}{V_{CC} - V_C} = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{dt}{R \, C}$$

$$-\ln\left(V_{CC} - V_C\right)\Big|_{0}^{V_C(t)} = \frac{t}{R \, C}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Definite Integral:**

$$-\ln\left(V_{CC} - V_C(t)\right) + \ln\left(V_{CC} - 0\right) = \frac{t}{R \, C}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC} - V_C(t)}\right) = \frac{t}{R \, C}$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate to Isolate the Capacitor Voltage:**

$$\frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC} - V_C(t)} = e^{\,t / (RC)}$$

$$V_{CC} - V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot e^{\,-t/(RC)}$$

$$\boxed{V_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{\,-t/(RC)}\right)}$$

This is the **canonical RC charging equation** — the foundation of all 555 timing analysis.

**Step 7 — Apply the Termination Condition:**

The quasi-stable state ends when the capacitor voltage reaches the upper threshold:

$$V_C(T) = \frac{2 \, V_{CC}}{3}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute into the Charging Equation:**

$$\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{\,-T/(RC)}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Divide Both Sides by $V_{CC}$:**

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{\,-T/(RC)}$$

**Step 10 — Isolate the Exponential Term:**

$$e^{\,-T/(RC)} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 11 — Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides:**

$$-\frac{T}{R \, C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln 3$$

**Step 12 — Final Result — The Pulse Width Formula:**

$$\boxed{T = R \, C \cdot \ln 3 \approx 1.0986 \, R \, C \approx 1.1 \, R \, C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Approximation:** Since $\ln 3 \approx 1.0986 \approx 1.1$, KTU examiners and textbooks universally adopt the simplified form $T \approx 1.1 \, R \, C$ with **less than 0.2% error**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Design Example

**Problem (KTU Typical):** Design a monostable multivibrator using IC 555 to generate an output pulse of width **$T = 1\,\text{ms}$**.

**Step A — Choose a Convenient Capacitor Value:**

Let $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F} = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{F} = 10^{-7}\,\text{F}$.

**Step B — Solve for Resistor $R$ Using $T = 1.1 \, R \, C$:**

$$R = \frac{T}{1.1 \cdot C} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$R = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{10^{4}}{1.1}$$

$$R = 9090.9\,\Omega \approx 9.1\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step C — Use the Standard Preferred Value:**

Select $R = 9.1\,\text{k}\Omega$ (E12 series) or $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ for an approximate $T = 1.1\,\text{ms}$.

**Step D — Verify Trigger Pulse Requirements:**

- Trigger pulse must drop below $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (i.e., below $1.67\,\text{V}$ for $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$).
- Trigger pulse width must be **less than $T$** (typically $< 10\,\mu\text{s}$).

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Monostable vs. Astable vs. Bistable

| Property | **Monostable** | **Astable** | **Bistable** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Number of Stable States** | **One** | **Zero** | **Two** |
| **Also Called** | One-Shot | Free-Running Oscillator | Flip-Flop / Latch |
| **Trigger Required?** | Yes (single pulse) | No (self-oscillating) | Yes (SET/RESET) |
| **Output Waveform** | Single pulse | Continuous square wave | Latched HIGH or LOW |
| **Timing Component** | Single $R$ and $C$ | Two $R$ and one $C$ | None (logic-controlled) |
| **Time Period Formula** | $T = 1.1 RC$ | $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$ | Not applicable |
| **Typical Application** | Pulse generation, delay | Clock generation, LEDs | Latch, memory cell |
